title: Syllabus
text: This course introduces students to quantitative research methods in international relations and European integration using modern tools for scientific programming and reproducible research. Students will learn how to analyze and visualize data in R, document their work using Quarto, and manage their projects efficiently using Git and GitHub. These tools enable students to follow state-of-the-art workflows for transparent, collaborative, and well-organized empirical research. All work will be conducted in Positron, an integrated development environment for data science.

title: Syllabus
text: This course introduces students to quantitative research methods in international relations and European integration using modern tools for scientific programming and reproducible research. Students will learn how to analyze and visualize data in R, document their work using Quarto, and manage their projects efficiently using Git and GitHub. These tools enable students to follow state-of-the-art workflows for transparent, collaborative, and well-organized empirical research. All work will be conducted in Positron, an integrated development environment for data science.

title: Syllabus
text:
Course Objectives
The overall goal of the course is to equip students with the practical skills and methodological confidence necessary to write an empirical bachelor thesis in political science. Students will learn how to preprocess data, select and implement appropriate statistical models for continuous and binary outcomes, compute quantities of interest, and interpret results accurately. Beyond technical proficiency, the course emphasizes sound statistical reasoning, effective data visualization, and the clear communication of empirical findings in a way that is accessible to nontechnical audiences. Through regular assignments and a cumulative final project (take-home exam), students will develop the ability to critically evaluate statistical claims in both academic research and public discourse, providing a solid foundation for conducting their own empirical analyses.

title: Syllabus
text:
Course Logistics

General Information
Each session consists of an instructor-led lecture on concepts and theory, followed by hands-on exercises. Homework is assigned at the end of class and due one week later. Readings provide background, theory, and context for the session. Because homework deadlines are tied to class days, students should remain in their assigned group (Mon or Thu) as their main class. Attending the other day is allowed in case of sickness or other exceptions, but this should remain the exception rather than the norm.


Attendance Policy
Active participation (Mitarbeit) is a required component of the coursework (Studienleistung). This course consists of 13 class sessions. Successful participation is presumed if a student attends at least 80% of class sessions (at least 11 out of 13 sessions).
If attendance falls below 80% but remains at or above 60% (8–10 out of 13 sessions), successful participation is not presumed; in such cases, the possibility of fulfilling the participation requirement will be assessed on a case-by-case basis.
If attendance falls below 60% (fewer than 8 out of 13 sessions), successful participation is normally considered impossible under the examination regulations.
If class sessions are cancelled during the semester, the absolute number of required sessions will be adjusted accordingly while the percentage thresholds (80% and 60%) remain unchanged.


Grading Policy
According to the examination regulations, course assessment consists of coursework (Studienleistung, pass/fail) and a final examination (Prüfungsleistung, graded).
Successful completion of all coursework requirements is a prerequisite for admission to the final take-home exam.

Coursework (Studienleistung, pass/fail)
Coursework consists of two components:
1. Weekly Homework Assignments
Starting in Week 2 (no homework will be assigned in the final week of the semester), students must complete 11 weekly homework assignments. Assignments will be distributed via GitHub and assessed on a pass/fail basis.
To fulfill this requirement, students must pass at least 80% of the assignments (9 out of 11 homeworks). Homework must demonstrate a serious and complete attempt at solving the tasks. Submissions that are empty or clearly insufficient will not be counted as passed.






Note



As homework assignments only started in Week 3, the total number of assignments is 10. Consequently, students must pass at least 8 out of 10 homework assignments to be eligible to take the take-home exam.


2. Active Participation (Mitarbeit)
Active participation, as described in the Attendance Policy, is a required coursework component and will be assessed on a pass/fail basis.
Both components must be passed in order to fulfill the Studienleistung requirement.


Final Examination (Prüfungsleistung, graded)
The final examination consists of a take-home exam, currently scheduled for:
May 28, 19:00 — June 05, 23:59
The exam will be distributed via GitHub. You will receive a dataset and a problem description for a cumulative data analysis project.
You must submit a complete project repository (project folder) via GitHub. The repository must include:

Fully documented and reproducible code

A rendered PDF file presenting your results (structured similarly to a data, methods, and analysis section of a research paper)

All relevant LLM chat histories, if AI tools were used
A statutory declaration according to the University’s guidelines

The version of the repository available on GitHub at the moment the deadline passes will be evaluated.
The take-home exam will be graded according to the university grading scale (1.0 = very good to 5.0 = insufficient) and determines the overall course grade.
All tools and workflows necessary to complete the exam will be taught during the course.



Language and Software Policy
The course is taught entirely in English and focuses on using R, Quarto, and Git/GitHub for data analysis, reproducible research, and project management. Students are encouraged to work with these tools, as the course exercises and workflows are designed around them. For the take-home exam, students may use other script-based languages (e.g., Python or Stata), but this is not recommended, and no guidance will be provided for non-R workflows. The emphasis is on understanding the tools, practicing them, and learning how to independently find solutions.


Hardware
You are required to have a computer available during class. For the purposes of this course, a standard laptop is sufficient; there are no specific requirements regarding RAM, external GPUs, or other advanced hardware components.
If you do not own a suitable device, the University of Mannheim’s IT department offers a laptop rental service. Students are encouraged to make use of this option if needed.


Software
Please install all required software programs and tools following the separate installation guide before the first session, so we can address any potential issues on that day and fully begin the programming component in the subsequent lecture.


Optional Practice Materials
It is highly recommended that you take advantage of the access to DataCamp provided by this course. DataCamp is an online learning platform offering valuable opportunities to practice the material covered in this course and beyond. A good starting point is the Introduction to R tutorial. DataCamp courses are voluntary and not part of the mandatory requirements to pass the course.


Late Work Policy
Late submissions will not be accepted. If you anticipate any difficulties in meeting a deadline, you must discuss this issue with me before the deadline, not after.


AI Policy
All submissions will be checked for plagiarism, and violations of the code of conduct may result in failing the course or expulsion. While generative AI tools, such as ChatGPT or Gemini, are permitted for the final examination, any AI-generated content or code must be clearly highlighted, thoroughly commented, and accompanied by the chat history to demonstrate understanding. I will aim to occasionally provide guidance on using AI responsibly, but relying on it from the start can hinder learning: research shows AI is most effective for those who already understand the topic. For a detailed explanation of why AI can undermine learning, see this explanation by Andrew Heiss.


Mental Health Support
Life and learning at the University of Mannheim can sometimes feel complicated, challenging, or even overwhelming. This is completely normal. You are not expected to fully understand or memorize course content on the first attempt. If you ever feel mentally or emotionally strained, you may find helpful advice on the University of Mannheim’s Good Mental Health and Studying page. Additionally, the Help in Times of Crises page provides an overview of the various counseling and support services available at the university.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
QMIR 2026


Opening. Welcome to Week 8. The last two weeks gave you the Bayesian conceptual machinery. Today we apply it to linear regression — the workhorse model of applied social science.
Arc: substantive research question → estimand/estimator/estimate → OLS and Gauss-Markov → three bridges to Bayesian regression → brms workflow and interpretation.
Time estimate: Parts I–III (~25 min), Part IV (~20 min), Parts V–VII (~20 min), Part VIII (~15 min), Summary/Q&A (~10 min).
Emphasize: this does NOT repeat Weeks 6–7. It applies that machinery. Students who feel shaky on the Bayesian basics should review those slides.


Week 8: From OLS to Bayesian Linear Regression
 Tristan Muno            April 16, 2026

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Week 8 Learning Goals


Read aloud. These six goals structure the 90-minute arc.
Goal–section mapping: Goal 1 → Part I; Goal 2 → Part IV; Goal 3 → Part V; Goal 4 → Part VI; Goal 5 → Part VII; Goal 6 → Part VIII.
Homework will directly assess Goals 2, 4, and 6.


By the end of today, you should be able to:

Frame a regression problem using the research methodology scaffold: estimand (what we want to know), estimator (the procedure), and estimate (the number we get)
Explain why OLS is the BLUE (Best Linear Unbiased Estimator) under the six Gauss-Markov assumptions, and identify violations in data
Derive why OLS estimators = Maximum Likelihood Estimators under a Gaussian likelihood, connecting classical and modern statistics
Specify weakly informative priors for regression coefficients and defend them as more scientifically rigorous than uniform priors
Understand MCMC/brms as sampling from the posterior distribution — the estimator that returns a full distribution, not one number
Interpret brms output: posterior means, credible intervals, \(\hat{R}\), ESS, and extract quantities of interest (predictions, marginal effects)

title: QMIR 2026
text:
A Question Worth Answering


Spend ~2 minutes here. The substantive question grounds everything that follows.
Economic voting: Kramer (1971), Lewis-Beck (1988), Hibbs’s “bread and peace” model. Students don’t need these references — mention briefly as intellectual context.
The “synthetic data” note is pedagogically important. We control the true DGP, so we can check whether our estimators recover the true parameters.
Transition: “Before we fit any line, we need to be precise about what we’re trying to estimate.”



Does economic growth affect how much of the vote an incumbent government receives?


This is economic voting theory: voters reward incumbents when the economy performs well and punish them when it does not.




Theory: Growth signals competence. When the economy expands, incumbents take credit; when it contracts, voters hold them responsible.
What we want to know: Not just the direction of the effect (positive), but its magnitude.

How many additional percentage points of vote share does each additional point of GDP growth buy?









Note


Data: We use a synthetic dataset of 100 election-years, calibrated to be substantively realistic.

\(X\) = GDP growth (%)
\(Y\) = incumbent vote share (%)

Synthetic data is intentional: it lets us know the true parameter values and demonstrate statistical concepts cleanly.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Three Formal Concepts


Walk through left to right. The key insight: all three words look similar but refer to very different things.
Estimand: Greek letters (α, β, σ). Unknown, fixed features of reality. We never observe them directly.
Estimator: a formula. It is a random variable — it would give different numbers with different data. We choose OLS because Gauss-Markov says it is optimal under certain conditions.
Estimate: the number we actually compute. It is one realization of the estimator. It is uncertain because it depended on our particular sample.




Estimand
What do we want to know?
The true population quantity. Fixed but unobservable. Denoted with Greek letters.
\(\beta\) — the true average change in vote share per 1pp of growth

Estimator
How will we compute an answer from data?
A procedure/formula applied to data in order to estimate our estimates.
\[\hat{\beta}_{\text{OLS}} = \frac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{\text{Var}(X)}\]

Estimate
What number(s) do we actually get?
The numerical result(s) of applying the estimator to our data.
\[\hat{\beta} = 2.07\]









Note


Mnemonic: Estimand = what we want · Estimator = our method · Estimate = what we get

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Estimand: The Data-Generating Process


Model specification slide. Make sure students understand each symbol before computing anything.
“Linear in parameters” means α and β appear linearly — not necessarily X. The model Y = α + β log(X) + ε is still linear.
The error term captures everything else: other causes of vote share, measurement error, random variation. In the Bayesian framework, we give ε a distribution (Normal), which becomes the likelihood.
Connect to Week 6: “Just like the coin-flip example, where the estimand was p. Here, the estimands are α, β, σ.”



We posit that election outcomes are generated by:
\[Y_i = \alpha + \beta X_i + \epsilon_i\]
where:

\(Y_i\) = incumbent vote share for election \(i\)
\(X_i\) = GDP growth for election \(i\)
\(\alpha\) = intercept: expected vote share when growth = 0 (true, unknown)
\(\beta\) = slope: true average change in vote share per 1pp of growth (true, unknown)
\(\epsilon_i\) = error term: everything not captured by the linear relationship



The estimands are \(\alpha\), \(\beta\), and \(\sigma\) (the error standard deviation).









Important


These are fixed, unobservable features of the world. Our entire exercise is to learn about them from data. In our synthetic dataset, we happen to know the truth: \(\alpha = 48\), \(\beta = 2.1\), \(\sigma = 4\). In real research, we never know this.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Estimator: OLS


The arg-min notation: “arg min means: find the argument (the α and β) that minimizes the expression.”
The closed form: “This is a formula. You plug in your data, compute Cov(X,Y) and Var(X), divide, and you get the slope estimate.”
Reason 3 for squaring: preview of Bridge 1. Gauss’s intuition — errors are Normal — directly motivates minimizing SSR.


OLS finds the values of \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) that minimize the sum of squared residuals (SSR):
\[\hat{\alpha},\, \hat{\beta} = \arg\min_{\alpha,\,\beta} \; \text{SSR} = \arg\min_{\alpha,\,\beta} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - \alpha - \beta X_i)^2\]

Setting partial derivatives to zero and solving yields the closed-form solution:
\[\hat{\beta}_{\text{OLS}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2} = \frac{\text{Cov}(X,\,Y)}{\text{Var}(X)} \qquad \hat{\alpha}_{\text{OLS}} = \bar{Y} - \hat{\beta}_{\text{OLS}}\,\bar{X}\]









Note


Why squared residuals? Three reasons: (1) Large deviations (\(\text{residual} = 10\)) are penalized far more than small ones (\(10^2 = 100\) vs \(1^2 = 1\)). (2) It yields a clean closed-form solution via calculus. (3) Under Normally distributed errors, minimizing SSR is equivalent to maximizing the likelihood — we will prove this in Part V (Bridge 1).

title: QMIR 2026
section: The Estimate: What We Actually Get
text:
The Estimate: What We Actually Get


The coef() output shows the two numbers from our synthetic data.
Compare to the true values: α = 48, β = 2.1. OLS recovers them well — this is what “unbiased” means.
Emphasize uncertainty: “We got 2.1. But with a different 100 elections, we might have gotten 1.8 or 2.4. The posterior distribution shows the full range of plausible values.”


Applying the OLS estimator to our synthetic dataset:


View source
fit_ols <- lm(incumbent_vote_share ~ gdp_growth, data = df)
coef(fit_ols)


(Intercept)  gdp_growth 
  48.644320    2.009786 



Substantive interpretation:

\(\hat{\alpha} \approx 48.1\): When growth = 0%, the model predicts approximately 48% vote share for the incumbent
\(\hat{\beta} \approx 2.1\): Each additional percentage point of growth is associated with +2.1 percentage points of vote share










Important


These are estimates — numbers computed from one particular sample of 100 elections. A different sample of 100 elections would yield different estimates. The posterior distribution (Part VII) will quantify exactly how uncertain these point estimates are.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Look Before You Fit


Let the data speak. Ask students: “What do you see?”
Walk through: positive trend visible, spread looks roughly constant, no extreme outliers, approximately linear.
This visual check is the first diagnostic step of any regression analysis.




Figure 1: Scatter plot of incumbent vote share against GDP growth. Each point is one election-year.


Before fitting a model, ask: Is there a visible trend? Is the relationship approximately linear? Are there outliers? Does the spread of \(Y\) look roughly constant across \(X\)? These visual questions correspond exactly to the Gauss-Markov assumptions we formalize in Part IV.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Marginal Distributions


Orange line: Normal density with same mean and SD as data.
Both distributions track the Normal well — consistent with Assumption 6 (Normal errors) and a Normal likelihood.
“We are not assuming Normality blindly. We check empirically whether it is plausible.”




Figure 2: Marginal distributions of GDP growth (left) and incumbent vote share (right) with Normal density overlaid. Both are approximately Normal — consistent with our likelihood assumption.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Adding the Regression Line


“The orange line is our best guess for the linear relationship. But what makes it ‘best’? That’s what OLS formalizes.”
Transition: “Let’s see how OLS actually finds this line — and where the idea of minimizing squared distances comes from historically.”




Figure 3: OLS regression line overlaid on the scatter. The line minimizes the sum of squared vertical distances from each point.

title: QMIR 2026
section: A Historical Detour: Ceres and Least Squares
text:
A Historical Detour: Ceres and Least Squares


Tell as a story, not as bullet points. Key narrative beats:

Piazzi discovers Ceres. It disappears. The community is stumped.
Gauss, 24, reframes the problem: “use all observations at once, find the orbit that best fits all of them simultaneously.”
His method: minimize squared residuals.
The philosophical act of genius: assuming Normal errors. This assumption motivates everything.

“Gauss’s 19th-century assumption — errors are Normally distributed — is the same assumption we formalize as the likelihood in brms today.”




January 1, 1801: Italian astronomer Giuseppe Piazzi discovers the asteroid Ceres between Mars and Jupiter.
He observes it for 40 nights — then it disappears behind the Sun.
The problem: Can astronomers predict where Ceres will reappear?
Multiple observers get different predictions. No consensus.
Enter Carl Friedrich Gauss, age 24. His question:

“Given all observations simultaneously, which orbit best reconciles the discrepancies?”

His answer: minimize the sum of squared residuals.
In December 1801, Ceres reappeared exactly where Gauss predicted.








Note


Why squared (not absolute) deviations?
Gauss argued that observational errors follow a Normal distribution. Under Normality:
\[\arg\min \sum (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2 = \arg\max \,\mathcal{L}\]
Minimizing SSR is maximizing the likelihood.
We will prove this formally in Part V.
Gauss’s 19th-century intuition connects directly to 21st-century Bayesian regression.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Visualizing Residuals


The purple segments are the residuals — the quantities OLS minimizes (squared).
“OLS picks the line such that the sum of squared purple segment lengths is as small as possible. If you shifted the line up or changed its slope, the total squared length would increase.”




Figure 4: Each vertical segment shows one residual: the distance from an observed data point to the fitted line. OLS finds the line that minimizes the sum of squared segment lengths.

title: QMIR 2026
section: The SSR as a Function of \(\beta\)
text:
The SSR as a Function of \(\beta\)


The bowl shape is guaranteed: SSR is a sum of squared (hence quadratic) terms in α and β. Quadratic functions are convex — they have a unique global minimum.
“OLS is not magic. It is literally finding the bottom of this bowl. The formula Cov(X,Y)/Var(X) gives you the bottom analytically, without searching.”




Figure 5: Sum of squared residuals as a function of the slope β, with α fixed at its OLS value. The SSR is a quadratic (bowl-shaped) function of β — its unique minimum is the OLS estimate.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
OLS Is BLUE — Under Conditions


“BLUE doesn’t mean OLS is perfect. It means: among all estimators that are (a) linear functions of Y and (b) unbiased, OLS has the smallest variance under these conditions.”
Transition: “Let’s go through all six. I’ll flag which ones Bayesian regression can relax, and which ones remain as hard constraints.”


The Gauss-Markov Theorem states that OLS is the Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE):




Best: Minimum variance among all linear unbiased estimators
Linear: The estimator is a linear function of \(Y_1, \ldots, Y_n\)
Unbiased: \(\mathbb{E}[\hat{\beta}] = \beta\) — no systematic over- or under-estimation









Important


Crucial caveat: BLUE holds only when the Gauss-Markov assumptions are satisfied. When violated, OLS loses its optimality — other estimators may have lower variance.
Knowing the assumptions tells you when you can trust OLS and when to use something else.







Six assumptions follow. For each: what it requires, what violation looks like, and how to detect it.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
A1: Linearity in Parameters


Left: OLS line captures the relationship well.
Right: the true DGP is quadratic. A linear OLS line systematically over-predicts in the middle and under-predicts at the extremes.
Diagnostic: residuals vs. fitted plot — a curved pattern indicates linearity violation.
Bayesian fix: add polynomial terms or specify a non-linear likelihood. The MCMC machinery is unchanged.


Statement: The outcome is a linear function of the parameters:
\[Y_i = \alpha + \beta X_i + \epsilon_i\]


Figure 6: Left: linear relationship — OLS is well-specified. Right: true quadratic relationship fitted with a linear model — OLS is systematically wrong.

Note: “Linear in parameters” means \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) appear linearly — not necessarily \(X\). The model \(Y = \alpha + \beta\log(X) + \epsilon\) is still linear in parameters.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
A2: Zero Conditional Mean (Exogeneity)


This is the most important assumption for causal inference.
“Adding a weakly informative prior does not compensate for an omitted variable. If the DGP violates A2, both OLS and MCMC produce biased estimates.”
Mention instrumental variables, randomized experiments, and regression discontinuity as design-based solutions — not model-based ones.


Statement: Errors have zero expectation conditional on the predictor:
\[\mathbb{E}[\epsilon_i \mid X_i] = 0\]
This means errors are uncorrelated with \(X\) — there are no systematic patterns in residuals across levels of \(X\).

Violation: Omitted Variable Bias
Suppose the true model is \(Y = \alpha + \beta X + \gamma Z + \epsilon\), but we omit \(Z\).
If \(Z\) correlates with \(X\), it is absorbed into \(\epsilon\) — making \(\mathbb{E}[\epsilon \mid X] \neq 0\).
Consequence: \(\hat{\beta}_{\text{OLS}}\) is biased — it conflates the effect of \(X\) with the effect of \(Z\).









Important


For economic voting: If we omit inflation (correlated with growth, independently affects vote share), \(\hat{\beta}\) is biased. No statistical method fixes this from within the model. Bayesian regression cannot fix endogeneity. You need better data, better design, or an instrument. Assumption 2 is about causality, not computation.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
A3: Homoscedasticity


Left: residuals scatter randomly around zero with constant spread — A3 satisfied.
Right: the classic funnel. Spread increases with fitted values — A3 violated (heteroscedasticity).
Consequence: OLS still unbiased, but inefficient; standard errors are wrong (typically understated), leading to overconfident credible intervals.
Bayesian fix: model the variance as a function of X, or use a t-distribution likelihood.


Statement: The variance of errors is constant across all values of \(X\):
\[\text{Var}(\epsilon_i \mid X_i) = \sigma^2 \quad \text{(constant)}\]


Figure 7: Residual vs. fitted plots. Left: constant spread (A3 satisfied). Right: funnel pattern — spread increases with fitted values (A3 violated).

title: QMIR 2026
text:
A4: No Perfect Multicollinearity


For today’s bivariate case: not a concern. Every election-year has different GDP growth values.
This becomes important next week with multiple predictors. Preview: “When you add predictors, make sure they’re not redundant.”


Statement: No predictor is a perfect linear combination of other predictors.

For bivariate regression: This only fails if \(X\) is constant across all observations — a trivially avoided data problem.


For multivariate regression (next week): If two predictors are perfectly collinear (e.g., income in USD and income in EUR), the design matrix is singular — OLS cannot be estimated at all.









Note


Practical rule: Multicollinearity is a design/data problem, not a modeling problem. The fix is to remove redundant predictors or collect better data.
Bayesian bonus: Weakly informative priors provide some regularization for near-collinear predictors — shrinking coefficients toward zero — but cannot recover from perfect collinearity. The design matrix is still singular.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
A5: Independent Errors (No Autocorrelation)


For cross-sectional data: less of a concern.
For time-series panels (many countries over many years): critical.
Preview of Week 12: “Partial pooling in multilevel models is a direct consequence of the Bayesian hierarchical prior structure. We’ll build this up carefully.”


Statement: Errors are uncorrelated across observations:
\[\text{Cov}(\epsilon_i,\, \epsilon_j \mid X_i, X_j) = 0 \quad \forall\, i \neq j\]

When does this fail?


Time series: The error for election in year \(t\) is often correlated with year \(t-1\) (economic shocks persist across periods).

Hierarchical data: Elections within the same country share correlated errors — the reason we need multilevel models (Week 12).










Note


Consequence: OLS is still unbiased but standard errors are wrong — typically understated — leading to overconfident inference.
Bayesian fix: Model the error structure explicitly: AR processes for time series, random effects for clustered data. The MCMC machinery is unchanged; only the likelihood changes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
A6: Normally Distributed Errors


Left: residuals track the Normal density (orange) well — A6 satisfied in our synthetic data (we generated it with Normal errors).
Right: Q-Q plot. Points on the diagonal = Normal. Systematic curvature = heavy tails or skewness.
Key connection: “The Normal distribution for errors is not just an assumption we check — it IS the likelihood we specify in brms. family = gaussian() is Assumption 6.”
If errors are non-Normal: use a t-distribution likelihood (robust to outliers) or another appropriate family.


Statement: Conditional on \(X\), errors follow a Normal distribution:
\[\epsilon_i \mid X_i \sim \text{Normal}(0,\, \sigma^2)\]


Figure 8: Left: histogram of OLS residuals with Normal density overlay. Right: Q-Q plot — points close to the diagonal indicate approximate Normality. Both support the Normal likelihood assumption.

Caveat: Normality is not required for OLS to be unbiased. It is the likelihood specification we use in Bayesian regression — family = gaussian() in brms is this assumption.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Gauss-Markov: Summary


Summary table. Read row by row briefly — students have the details from the preceding slides.
Key takeaway: A2 (exogeneity) is the most consequential. Bayesian regression cannot fix it.
A3, A5, A6: Bayesian methods can handle these by specifying appropriate likelihoods and hierarchical structures.
Transition: “Now that we know when and why OLS works, let’s build the three bridges to the Bayesian framework.”













Assumption
Requirement
Key Violation
OLS Consequence
Bayesian Leverage




A1 Linearity
Linear in \(\alpha, \beta\)
Wrong functional form
Biased estimates
Change likelihood / add terms


A2 Exogeneity
\(\mathbb{E}[\epsilon\mid X]=0\)
Omitted variables
Biased (critical)
None — design problem


A3 Homoscedasticity
\(\text{Var}(\epsilon\mid X)=\sigma^2\)
Funnel in residuals
Inefficient; wrong SEs
Model variance explicitly


A4 No Multicollinearity
No perfect collinearity
Redundant predictors
Cannot estimate
Priors help near-collinearity


A5 Independence
\(\text{Cov}(\epsilon_i,\epsilon_j)=0\)
Time series / clusters
Wrong SEs
Hierarchical / AR likelihood


A6 Normality
\(\epsilon\sim N(0,\sigma^2)\)
Heavy tails, skew
Small-\(n\) inference fails
Change likelihood family












Note


Bayesian regression relaxes A3, A5, A6 by modeling them explicitly in the likelihood. A1 and A2 remain hard constraints — they concern the DGP, not the computational method. No framework can fix endogeneity (A2) from within the model.

title: QMIR 2026
section: A Probability Model for the Data
text:
A Probability Model for the Data


Students have seen likelihoods in Week 6 (coin flip). Now we apply the same idea to a full regression model.
“The probability of observing Y_i, given the parameters, is a Normal density centered at mu_i = alpha + beta * X_i.”
“The likelihood for the full dataset is the product of n Normal densities — assuming independence (A5).”
Log-likelihood: “Products become sums; sums are easier to optimize.”


OLS found the “best line” by minimizing SSR — a geometric criterion. Now we ask: what probability model produces the same answer?

Suppose each observation is generated by:
\[Y_i \mid \alpha, \beta, \sigma \;\sim\; \text{Normal}(\mu_i,\; \sigma) \qquad \text{where}\quad \mu_i = \alpha + \beta X_i\]


The likelihood — probability of observing the data given parameters — is:
\[\mathcal{L}(\alpha, \beta, \sigma \mid \mathbf{Y}, \mathbf{X}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left\{-\frac{(Y_i - \mu_i)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right\}\]


Taking logarithms (log-likelihood — equivalent to optimize, easier to work with):
\[\ell(\alpha, \beta, \sigma) = -\frac{n}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) \;-\; \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(Y_i - \mu_i)^2\]

title: QMIR 2026
text:
MLE = OLS: The Equivalence


Walk through the algebra slowly.
“The only term in the log-likelihood that depends on α and β is the sum of squared residuals. The sigma^2 is a positive constant. So maximizing ell is the same as minimizing SSR.”
“This is why Gauss’s intuition was so powerful. He didn’t have probability theory as we know it, but he guessed correctly — and under Normality, it is provably optimal.”
Transition: “We now have the likelihood. The Bayesian step is to multiply it by a prior.”


To find the MLE, we maximize \(\ell(\alpha, \beta, \sigma)\) with respect to \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\).

The first term \(-\frac{n}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2)\) is constant in \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\). So:
\[\arg\max_{\alpha,\,\beta}\;\ell = \arg\max_{\alpha,\,\beta} \left\{-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(Y_i - \mu_i)^2\right\} = \arg\min_{\alpha,\,\beta}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(Y_i - \alpha - \beta X_i)^2\]









Important


Bridge 1 — The Key Insight:
\[\text{OLS} \equiv \text{MLE under a Gaussian likelihood}\]
Gauss’s 1801 intuition — “minimize squared residuals” — is equivalent to maximum likelihood estimation under the assumption that errors are Normally distributed.
The two frameworks arrive at the same estimator from entirely different starting points: geometric optimization vs. probabilistic modeling.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Likelihood Surface


The heat map shows the log-likelihood surface. Brighter = higher likelihood.
The maximum (red ×) is at the OLS estimates — confirmed visually.
“The Bayesian posterior will look similar to this surface, but modified by the prior: slightly shifted toward the prior mean for small samples, anchored by the data for large samples.”




Figure 9: Log-likelihood surface as a function of α and β (σ fixed at OLS residual SE). The maximum (red ×) is at the OLS estimates — exactly as Bridge 1 predicts.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Bayes’ Theorem for Regression


“You’ve seen this proportional form. The posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior. The normalizing constant P(data) is handled by MCMC — we never compute it.”
Prior choices:

beta ~ Normal(0, 2): “I don’t know the sign of the slope. I expect moderate effects. 99.7% of prior mass is in [-6, +6] — from ‘nearly no effect’ to ‘very large but plausible’.”
alpha ~ Normal(48, 10): “Vote share near 50% in a competitive democracy when growth = 0. Uncertainty of ±20pp covers the plausible range.”
sigma ~ Exponential(0.5): “Error SD should be positive, probably below 10.”



From Weeks 6–7 — applied now to regression:
\[p(\alpha, \beta, \sigma \mid \mathbf{Y}, \mathbf{X}) \;\propto\; \underbrace{\mathcal{L}(\alpha, \beta, \sigma \mid \mathbf{Y}, \mathbf{X})}_{\text{Likelihood (data evidence)}} \;\cdot\; \underbrace{p(\alpha, \beta, \sigma)}_{\text{Prior (regularization)}}\]

We now need to specify priors. This is a philosophical commitment, not an afterthought.


Principled weakly informative priors for our model:
\[\beta \;\sim\; \text{Normal}(0,\; 2) \qquad \text{"slope probably between } {-6}\text{ and }{+6}\text{"}\]
\[\alpha \;\sim\; \text{Normal}(48,\; 10) \qquad \text{"intercept in a competitive democracy"}\]
\[\sigma \;\sim\; \text{Exponential}(0.5) \qquad \text{"residual SD likely below 10"}\]

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Prior Comparison: Why “Weakly Informative”?


Purple: Uniform(-10, 10). All values equally plausible from -10 to +10. “This says β = -9 is as plausible as β = 0.1. That is not what we believe.”
Gold: Normal(0, 5). Better than uniform, but still assigns substantial density to β = ±8 — every percentage point of growth adds 8pp of vote share. Implausibly large.
Orange: Normal(0, 2). Our chosen prior. Most mass between -6 and +6. Values beyond ±10 are essentially impossible a priori.
“The prior is honest. Normal(0, 2) says: ‘I don’t know the direction, but extreme effects are less plausible.’ It does not pre-determine the answer — with n = 100, the likelihood dominates.”




Figure 10: Three prior distributions for the slope β. A weakly informative Normal(0, 2) is the principled choice: it regularizes toward zero while permitting all substantively reasonable effect sizes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Priors as Regularization


Small sample: prior matters more. Bayesian methods shine here — the prior prevents pathological estimates when data are sparse.
Large sample: Bernstein-von Mises theorem (don’t name it, just convey the idea). Bayes and frequentist asymptotically agree.
Ridge connection: for students who have seen regularization in machine learning context, this bridges the frameworks.


A weakly informative prior \(\beta \sim \text{Normal}(0, 2)\) regularizes the posterior:



Small sample (\(n = 20\))
The prior carries substantial weight. The posterior is a genuine compromise between the likelihood (noisy, few data points) and the prior (centered at 0).
Posterior is shrunk toward 0 relative to OLS.

Large sample (\(n = 1000\))
The likelihood dominates. The prior becomes negligible. The posterior concentrates near the MLE/OLS estimate.
Posterior \(\approx\) OLS — the prior is washed out by data.










Note


Connection to ridge regression: A Normal prior on \(\beta\) is mathematically equivalent to \(L_2\) regularization (ridge penalty) in frequentist regression. Both discourage extreme coefficient values. The Bayesian prior is more principled: it has a direct probability interpretation and can be updated coherently as more data arrive.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Recap: The Computational Problem


Brief recap. Students saw the dimensional explosion in Week 7. Don’t re-teach; remind.
“OLS was the estimator that gave us one number. MCMC is the estimator that gives us a distribution.”
The normalizing constant cancels: in the Metropolis acceptance ratio, P(data) appears in both numerator and denominator — it vanishes. This is the mathematical magic of MCMC.


You learned in Week 7 why grid approximation fails:

\[p(\alpha, \beta, \sigma \mid \mathbf{Y}, \mathbf{X}) = \frac{\mathcal{L}(\alpha, \beta, \sigma \mid \mathbf{Y}, \mathbf{X}) \cdot p(\alpha, \beta, \sigma)}{\displaystyle\iiint \mathcal{L}(\alpha, \beta, \sigma) \cdot p(\alpha, \beta, \sigma)\;d\alpha\,d\beta\,d\sigma}\]


The denominator is a three-dimensional integral with no closed form. On a 1,000-point grid: \(10^9\) evaluations — barely feasible. For a 50-parameter model: \(10^{150}\) — impossible.









Important


Bridge 3: MCMC is not a computational hack. MCMC is the estimator for the posterior distribution itself. It produces thousands of plausible parameter values by sampling in proportion to likelihood × prior — without ever computing the normalizing constant, because it cancels in the acceptance ratio.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The brms Workflow


Walk through each argument:

Formula: Y ~ X — same syntax as lm()
prior: three explicit priors — these are the philosophical commitments from Bridge 2
chains = 4: four independent chains for convergence diagnostics (Rhat)
iter = 2000, warmup = 1000: standard settings
seed: reproducibility

“Notice we explicitly specify priors for every parameter. This is not optional in principled Bayesian regression.”




View source
fit_bayes <- brm(
  incumbent_vote_share ~ gdp_growth,
  data = df,
  prior = c(
    prior(normal(48, 10), class = "Intercept"),
    prior(normal(0, 2), class = "b"),
    prior(exponential(0.5), class = "sigma")
  ),
  chains = 4,
  iter = 2000,
  warmup = 1000,
  seed = 2026
)



What brms does behind the scenes:

Translates your formula to Stan (C++) code
Runs 4 parallel MCMC chains, each drawing 2,000 samples
Discards the first 1,000 (warmup — the chain is still finding the posterior)
Returns 4,000 posterior samples — plausible values of \((\alpha, \beta, \sigma)\)

title: QMIR 2026
section: OLS: One Line. MCMC: A Cloud of Lines.
text:
OLS: One Line. MCMC: A Cloud of Lines.


This is the key visual for Bridge 3.
Each grey line = one (α, β) posterior sample = one plausible regression line consistent with data and priors.
“OLS gives you one line. MCMC gives you a cloud of lines — a more honest representation of what we know.”
“The cloud is narrow in the middle of the data range (where we have most information) and widens at the extremes.”
“In a sense, OLS is asking: ‘what is the most likely line?’ MCMC is asking: ‘which lines are plausible?’ These are different questions, and the Bayesian answer is richer.”




View source
post_draws <- as_draws_df(fit_bayes) |>
  slice_sample(n = 200)

df |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gdp_growth, y = incumbent_vote_share)) +
  geom_abline(
    data = post_draws,
    aes(intercept = b_Intercept, slope = b_gdp_growth),
    color = "gray60",
    alpha = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.4
  ) +
  geom_abline(
    intercept = coef(fit_ols)[1],
    slope = coef(fit_ols)[2],
    color = col_2,
    linewidth = 1.4
  ) +
  geom_point(color = col_1, alpha = 0.55, size = 1.8) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) +
  labs(
    x = "GDP Growth (%)",
    y = "Incumbent Vote Share (%)",
    title = "Posterior Uncertainty: A Cloud of Plausible Regression Lines",
    subtitle = "Grey: 200 posterior draws  |  Orange: OLS estimate"
  )




Figure 11: Each grey line is one posterior draw — one plausible regression line given the data and priors. The orange line is the OLS estimate. The cloud quantifies uncertainty; OLS gives a single answer.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Reading brms Output


Walk through each section of the output:

“Population-Level Effects”: regression coefficients (what we care about)
Estimate = posterior mean. Est.Error = posterior SD (our uncertainty).
l-95% CI, u-95% CI: 95% credible interval.
Rhat: convergence diagnostic. Values < 1.01 indicate good mixing.
Bulk_ESS, Tail_ESS: effective sample size. > 1000 is healthy.
“Family Specific Parameters”: sigma (the error SD).

“OLS would give you two numbers — point estimates with standard errors. This gives you two full distributions.”




View source
summary(fit_bayes)


 Family: gaussian 
  Links: mu = identity 
Formula: incumbent_vote_share ~ gdp_growth 
   Data: df (Number of observations: 100) 
  Draws: 4 chains, each with iter = 2000; warmup = 1000; thin = 1;
         total post-warmup draws = 4000

Regression Coefficients:
           Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS
Intercept     48.67      0.47    47.74    49.61 1.00     3978     3007
gdp_growth     1.99      0.15     1.69     2.29 1.00     3528     2848

Further Distributional Parameters:
      Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS
sigma     3.86      0.27     3.37     4.43 1.00     4227     2835

Draws were sampled using sampling(NUTS). For each parameter, Bulk_ESS
and Tail_ESS are effective sample size measures, and Rhat is the potential
scale reduction factor on split chains (at convergence, Rhat = 1).

title: QMIR 2026
section: Credible Intervals: A Direct Probability Statement
text:
Credible Intervals: A Direct Probability Statement


Students will have encountered confidence intervals in other courses. Clarify the distinction carefully.
“The credible interval is what students always wanted the confidence interval to mean. It makes a direct probability statement about this parameter, given this data.”
Don’t spend too long here — the key contrast is stated. Students can dig deeper in the readings.


From the brms summary, the 95% credible interval for \(\beta\) is approximately \([1.5,\; 2.7]\).

What this means:

“Given the data and our priors, there is a 95% posterior probability that the true slope \(\beta\) lies between 1.5 and 2.7.”



This is a direct probability statement about the parameter. It says what you intuitively want it to say.









Important


Important distinction: A frequentist 95% confidence interval does not say “there is 95% probability the true value is in this interval.” It says: “If we repeated this study many times, 95% of such intervals would contain the true value.” The particular interval either contains the truth or it does not — we just don’t know which.
The Bayesian credible interval answers the question you actually have. The frequentist confidence interval answers a different question.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Posterior Distributions of Parameters


Left: posterior for alpha. Solid orange line = posterior mean. Dashed blue lines = 95% CI boundaries.
Right: posterior for beta. Purple vertical line = posterior mean. Dotted gray line at 0 = “no effect” reference.
“The grey dotted line at zero is the ‘no effect’ reference. The posterior mass is almost entirely to the right of zero — strong evidence for a positive effect.”




View source
post_df <- as_draws_df(fit_bayes)

ci_alpha <- quantile(post_df$b_Intercept, probs = c(0.025, 0.975))
ci_beta <- quantile(post_df$b_gdp_growth, probs = c(0.025, 0.975))

p_post_alpha <- post_df |>
  ggplot(aes(x = b_Intercept)) +
  geom_density(fill = col_1, color = "white", alpha = 0.75) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = ci_alpha,
    color = col_1,
    linetype = "dashed",
    linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = mean(post_df$b_Intercept),
    color = col_2,
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) +
  labs(
    x = expression(alpha ~ "(Intercept)"),
    y = "Posterior Density",
    title = "Posterior: Intercept"
  )

p_post_beta <- post_df |>
  ggplot(aes(x = b_gdp_growth)) +
  geom_density(fill = col_2, color = "white", alpha = 0.75) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = ci_beta,
    color = col_2,
    linetype = "dashed",
    linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = 0,
    color = "gray40",
    linetype = "dotted",
    linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = mean(post_df$b_gdp_growth),
    color = col_3,
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) +
  labs(
    x = expression(beta ~ "(GDP Growth slope)"),
    y = "Posterior Density",
    title = "Posterior: Slope"
  )

p_post_alpha | p_post_beta




Figure 12: Marginal posterior distributions for the intercept (left) and slope (right). Dashed lines mark the 95% credible interval boundaries. The posterior is not a point estimate — it is a full distribution over plausible parameter values.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Three Types of Model Predictions


Students frequently conflate these three quantities. The distinctions are important and will recur in every subsequent week.
posterior_epred(): “expected prediction” — averages over MCMC draws of α and β, but does not add σ. Answers “where is the mean?” Use this to plot the regression line with its credible interval.
posterior_predict(): draws a new observation for each MCMC sample. Adds a Normal(0, σ) draw on top. Answers “where would a single new country’s vote share fall?” Interval always wider.
Marginal effects: in the bivariate linear model, the marginal effect of X on Y is exactly β — you already have it from the posterior. In multivariate models with interactions, it depends on the values of other predictors. We will return to this in Week 9.
Practical rule: use posterior_epred() to understand the systematic part of the model; use posterior_predict() to generate realistic individual forecasts.



Not all model predictions answer the same question. In the Bayesian/brms framework, three quantities are conceptually distinct:




Expected Value
posterior_epred()
Uncertainty in the conditional mean — the regression line and its credible interval.
\[\hat{\mu}_i = \alpha + \beta X_i\]
“Where is the average vote share for this growth rate?”

Marginal Effect
The first derivative of \(\hat{\mu}\) with respect to a predictor, holding others constant.
\[\frac{\partial \hat{\mu}}{\partial X} = \beta\]
For a linear model, this is the slope posterior — the \(\beta\) distribution from brms.
“How much does the mean shift per unit of growth?”

Predicted Value
posterior_predict()
Full predictive uncertainty: parameter uncertainty plus residual variance \(\sigma\).
\[\tilde{Y} \sim \text{Normal}(\hat{\mu}_i,\; \sigma)\]
“Where will a single new observation fall?”










Note


posterior_epred() intervals are narrow — they reflect only uncertainty in \((\alpha, \beta)\). posterior_predict() intervals are wider — they add \(\sigma\), the irreducible observation-level noise. Always report the one that matches your research question.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Predictions with Uncertainty


posterior_epred() returns the expected value E[Y | X] — the conditional mean — for each draw and each newdata row.
The ribbon is narrower in the center of the data range (where we have most information) and widens at the edges (extrapolation).
Distinction: posterior_epred() gives uncertainty about the mean; posterior_predict() would give wider intervals including sigma (uncertainty about a single new observation).




View source
new_data <- tibble(
  gdp_growth = seq(
    min(df$gdp_growth) - 0.5,
    max(df$gdp_growth) + 0.5,
    length.out = 100
  )
)

epred_mat <- posterior_epred(fit_bayes, newdata = new_data)

pred_summary <- new_data |>
  mutate(
    lo = apply(epred_mat, 2, quantile, probs = 0.025),
    mid = apply(epred_mat, 2, median),
    hi = apply(epred_mat, 2, quantile, probs = 0.975)
  )

df |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gdp_growth, y = incumbent_vote_share)) +
  geom_ribbon(
    data = pred_summary,
    aes(x = gdp_growth, y = mid, ymin = lo, ymax = hi),
    fill = col_1,
    alpha = 0.25,
    inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = pred_summary,
    aes(x = gdp_growth, y = mid),
    color = col_2,
    linewidth = 1.2,
    inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +
  geom_point(color = col_1, alpha = 0.55, size = 1.8) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) +
  labs(
    x = "GDP Growth (%)",
    y = "Predicted Vote Share (%)",
    title = "Posterior Predictive Mean with 95% Credible Interval",
    subtitle = "Ribbon: 95% CI for E[Y | X]  |  using posterior_epred()"
  )




Figure 13: Posterior predictive mean (line) and 95% credible interval (ribbon) for incumbent vote share across the range of GDP growth values.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Marginal Effect: How Much Does Growth Matter?
text:
Marginal Effect: How Much Does Growth Matter?


P(β > 0 | data, prior): count the fraction of posterior samples greater than zero. This is a direct probability statement — not a p-value.
“We can report: ‘There is a ≈100% posterior probability that economic growth has a positive effect on incumbent vote share.’”
“The grey dotted line marks the true β = 2.1 — visible in real teaching because we generated the data. Our posterior is centered near the truth.”
“OLS gives you the estimate and a standard error. The posterior gives you a full distribution over plausible effect sizes.”




View source
prob_pos <- (sum(post_df$b_gdp_growth > 0) / nrow(post_df))

post_df |>
  ggplot(aes(x = b_gdp_growth)) +
  geom_density(fill = col_1, color = "white", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = 0,
    color = col_2,
    linetype = "dashed",
    linewidth = 1.1
  ) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = 2.1,
    color = "gray30",
    linetype = "dotted",
    linewidth = 0.9
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = 0.08,
    y = 1.1,
    label = paste0("P(\u03b2 > 0 | data) = ", round(prob_pos, 3)),
    color = col_2,
    size = 3.8,
    hjust = 0
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = 2.18,
    y = 1.4,
    label = "True \u03b2 = 2.1",
    color = "gray30",
    size = 3.5,
    hjust = 0
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) +
  labs(
    x = expression(beta ~ "(Effect of GDP Growth on Vote Share)"),
    y = "Posterior Density",
    title = "Marginal Posterior: Slope Coefficient",
    subtitle = "Direct probability statements are possible from posterior samples"
  )




Figure 14: Marginal posterior distribution of the slope β. Virtually all posterior mass lies above zero. The annotated probability is a direct statement: P(β > 0 | data, prior).

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Posterior Predictions with Full Uncertainty


posterior_predict() returns simulated outcome values for each posterior draw and each newdata row.
Unlike posterior_epred(), which captures only systematic uncertainty (uncertainty about the mean), posterior_predict() also includes stochastic uncertainty (sigma), reflecting variability in individual observations.
The resulting intervals are therefore wider and represent where new data points are expected to fall.




View source
new_data <- tibble(
  gdp_growth = seq(
    min(df$gdp_growth) - 0.5,
    max(df$gdp_growth) + 0.5,
    length.out = 100
  )
)
pred_mat <- posterior_predict(fit_bayes, newdata = new_data)
pred_summary <- new_data |>
  mutate(
    lo = apply(pred_mat, 2, quantile, probs = 0.025),
    mid = apply(pred_mat, 2, median),
    hi = apply(pred_mat, 2, quantile, probs = 0.975)
  )
df |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gdp_growth, y = incumbent_vote_share)) +
  geom_ribbon(
    data = pred_summary,
    aes(x = gdp_growth, y = mid, ymin = lo, ymax = hi),
    fill = col_1,
    alpha = 0.25,
    inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = pred_summary,
    aes(x = gdp_growth, y = mid),
    color = col_2,
    linewidth = 1.2,
    inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +
  geom_point(color = col_1, alpha = 0.55, size = 1.8) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) +
  labs(
    x = "GDP Growth (%)",
    y = "Predicted Vote Share (%)",
    title = "Posterior Predictive Distribution with 95% Predictive Interval",
    subtitle = "Ribbon: 95% PI for Ỹ | X  |  includes systematic + stochastic uncertainty  |  posterior_predict()"
  )




Figure 15: Posterior predictive distribution (median line) and 95% predictive interval (ribbon) for incumbent vote share across GDP growth values. Intervals include both systematic and stochastic uncertainty.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Three Bridges: The Complete Arc


Closing slide. The three-column layout mirrors the three bridges from the session.
“Across all three bridges, the linear model stays the same. What changes is the inferential framework. OLS is one answer; Bayesian MCMC is a richer, more complete answer.”
“Any questions before we look at the homework?”




Bridge 1
OLS ≡ MLE
Under Gaussian errors, minimizing SSR = maximizing the likelihood.
Gauss’s 1801 insight → modern statistics
Connects geometric optimization to probabilistic modeling.

Bridge 2
Likelihood × Prior
Multiplying the likelihood by a weakly informative prior regularizes estimates and yields a full posterior distribution.
Prior is a principled commitment — not a convenience

Bridge 3
MCMC as Estimator
MCMC samples from the posterior — thousands of plausible parameter values — without computing the normalizing constant.
OLS = one line. MCMC = a cloud of plausible lines.









Note


What stays constant: the linear model (\(Y = \alpha + \beta X + \epsilon\)) and the Gauss-Markov conditions that justify the linear specification. What changes: how we quantify uncertainty. OLS reports point estimates; MCMC reports posterior distributions.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Learning Goals: Check


Read through the goals quickly. This gives closure and connects back to the opening.
Homework: students will practice the full pipeline independently — their first real application of Bayesian linear regression.
Q&A: “What was the most confusing part of today? What would you like me to revisit?”
Remaining time: point students to the homework page.


By the end of today, you should be able to:

✓ Frame a regression problem as estimand / estimator / estimate (Part I)
✓ Explain why OLS is BLUE under the Gauss-Markov assumptions, and identify violations (Part IV)
✓ Derive that OLS = MLE under a Gaussian likelihood (Part V)
✓ Specify and defend weakly informative priors as more rigorous than uniform priors (Part VI)
✓ Understand MCMC as the posterior estimator — the procedure that returns a distribution (Part VII)
✓ Interpret brms output: posterior means, credible intervals, \(\hat{R}\), ESS, and extract quantities of interest (Part VIII)









Important


Homework (due before next session): Apply the full workflow to a new bivariate research question using the European Social Survey dataset. Instructions and GitHub Classroom link on the course website. Goals 2, 4, and 6 are directly assessed.

title: QMIR 2026
text: References

title: QMIR 2026
text:
QMIR 2026
Week 6: Probability, Simulation & Bayes’ Theorem
 Tristan Muno            March 19, 2026

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Week 6 Learning Goals
By the end of today, you should be able to:

Articulate the distinction between frequentist and Bayesian interpretations of probability, and explain why each is suited to different types of questions
Explain why parameters are treated as uncertain quantities with distributions in the Bayesian framework
Name and interpret the three core ingredients of Bayesian inference: prior, likelihood, and posterior
State Bayes’ theorem in full, identify each term, and explain the role of the normalizing constant \(P(\text{data})\)
Understand Bayesian updating using a discrete 6-sided globe model
Describe qualitatively how the posterior changes as the prior changes and as data accumulates
Understand and apply grid approximation in R to compute and visualize a posterior distribution


These are the seven learning goals for the session. The verbs are intentionally testable: articulate, explain, name, state, implement, describe, apply. The take-home exam will engage with several of these directly.
Remind students at the start: today is a pivot. Block I was about tools. Block II is about reasoning under uncertainty. These are different cognitive demands. The programming will be simpler than weeks 3–5 – the challenge today is conceptual. The goal is not to memorize formulas but to understand what the formulas represent scientifically.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Globe Toss

Catch the ball. Look at your right index finger.
Call out: Land or Water.


This is the live activity. Do NOT show slides during this segment – put slides on blank and work at the whiteboard.
Toss the globe ball to students one at a time. Every student who catches it looks at their right index finger and calls out “Water” or “Land.” Record the result on the whiteboard as a simple tally. Aim for 15–20 tosses. Keep the pace brisk and the mood light.
After the tosses, let the tally sit on the board and turn to the room. Begin the question sequence slowly with deliberate pauses:
“Based on what we just observed – what proportion of the Earth’s surface is water?”
Take a few responses without validating or correcting them. Then: “How confident are you in that answer?” “Would your answer change if we had done only 3 tosses? What about 300?” “And here is the most important question: what did you already believe before the first toss?”
That last question is the hinge of the session. Students will realize they walked in with a belief – they just never called it that. Let the discomfort sit briefly.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
What Did You Already Know?

Before the first toss, you already believed:

The proportion is probably not 5%
The proportion is probably not 95%
Values around 60–80% feel plausible



That belief – the one you held before seeing any data – is a prior.


And then you updated it, toss by toss.








Note


That process – combining a prior belief with new evidence – is Bayesian inference. Today we give it a formal mathematical structure.




The key pedagogical move: making the implicit explicit. Students have been performing informal Bayesian reasoning all their lives. The goal of today is to formalise that intuition, not introduce something alien.
Worth noting: even a “flat” prior is not truly uninformative. A uniform prior over [0, 1] assigns equal probability to “0% water” and “70% water” – which is arguably not what anyone actually believes walking in. This observation plants a seed for the discussion of prior choice later.
Keep this slide brief. The conceptual elaboration follows. The goal is to create a felt sense of the problem.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Central Insight

Everything uncertain gets a distribution.
The data is the one thing that is fixed.


In the Bayesian framework:

The parameter \(\theta\) – the true proportion of water – is uncertain. It gets a probability distribution.
The data – the actual tosses – are observed facts. They happened. They are not uncertain.










Important


This is an inversion of what many introductory statistics courses imply. In the Bayesian framework, the parameter is not a fixed unknown constant. It is a quantity about which we hold graded beliefs, and those beliefs are represented as a probability distribution that we update as evidence arrives.





This is the most important conceptual slide of the entire week. Take time here and invite questions.
A common student reaction: “But isn’t there a true value of theta? The Earth exists. It has a surface. Some fixed fraction of it is water.”
The response: Yes, of course there is a true value. But we don’t know it. Our uncertainty about it is real, and it has structure. We can say some values are more plausible than others before observing any data. Probability is the language we use to express that structured uncertainty. The randomness is not in the world – it is in our state of knowledge. Philosophers call this the distinction between ontic randomness (the world is random) and epistemic uncertainty (we are uncertain). Bayesian probability is the latter.
McElreath (Statistical Rethinking, ch. 1) makes this point clearly: the Bayesian framework treats everything unknown as having a probability distribution – not because the world is random, but because we are uncertain.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Two Interpretations of Probability


Frequentist
Probability is the long-run relative frequency of an event across many repetitions of the same experiment.
\[
P(A) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\text{count}(A)}{n}
\tag{1}\]

An objective property of a repeatable data-generating process
Naturally suited to coin flips, dice, and random sampling designs
Not directly applicable to parameters or one-off events


Bayesian
Probability is a degree of rational belief, or plausibility, given available information.
\[
P(A \mid \mathcal{I}) \in [0, 1]
\tag{2}\]

A statement about an agent’s state of knowledge
Applies to any uncertain proposition: events, parameters, hypotheses
Updates coherently as new information arrives



It is important not to set up a straw man here. Frequentist statistics is a rigorous framework that has underpinned enormous advances in empirical science. Both interpretations are internally coherent. The point is that different interpretations are suited to different types of scientific questions.
The frequentist framework is particularly powerful for questions about repeatable processes: quality control, the long-run properties of estimators, large-scale randomised experiments. The concept of a p-value is a well-defined frequentist quantity – it describes the probability of observing data at least as extreme as observed, given the null hypothesis, in hypothetical repeated sampling.
The Bayesian framework is more natural when we want to reason about one-off events, parameter values, or scientific hypotheses that cannot be repeated. The key advantage is that Bayesian probability statements are directly about the quantity of scientific interest – the parameter – rather than about hypothetical repeated experiments.
The framing “not directly applicable” is deliberate and more accurate than “cannot.” Frequentist methods have workarounds for many situations – but they require indirect reasoning that Bayesian methods express directly.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Probability as a Tool for Research Questions
text:
Probability as a Tool for Research Questions
For questions like:



“What is the probability that the true treatment effect is positive?”
“How likely is it that Country X will democratize in the next decade?”
“Given the data, what should we believe about the effect of GDP on emissions?”





… the frequentist framework is not designed to provide a direct probability answer. A parameter either has a given value or it does not – it does not have a frequency distribution in a sampling sense.










Note


A 95% frequentist confidence interval means: if we repeated this sampling procedure many times, 95% of the resulting intervals would contain the true value. The parameter is fixed – only the interval varies across hypothetical repetitions.
A 95% Bayesian credible interval means: given the data and the prior, we assign 95% probability to the true parameter lying in this range. This is the interpretation most researchers intuitively want (Hoekstra et al. 2014) – and only the Bayesian framework directly supports it.





The confidence interval point is worth elaborating. Hoekstra et al. (2014, Psychonomic Bulletin & Review) surveyed researchers across disciplines and found systematic misinterpretation – the majority read confidence intervals as probability statements about the parameter, which is the Bayesian reading. This is not a failure of researchers; it reflects that the intuitive meaning people want is Bayesian.
The pragmatic framing for the course: we adopt a Bayesian approach because the questions we ask in political science – about parameters, causal effects, and uncertain quantities – are most naturally expressed in a framework where probability measures belief rather than frequency. As the syllabus emphasises: tools should serve the science, not the other way around.
At the same time, we should not be dismissive of frequentist methods. Students will encounter them throughout their careers in published work. Understanding both frameworks makes for a better-equipped researcher.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Bayes’ Theorem

\[
\underbrace{P(\theta \mid \text{data})}_{\text{Posterior}} = \frac{\underbrace{P(\text{data} \mid \theta)}_{\text{Likelihood}} \cdot \underbrace{P(\theta)}_{\text{Prior}}}{\underbrace{P(\text{data})}_{\text{Normalizing constant}}}
\tag{3}\]











Term
Symbol
Plain language




Prior
\(P(\theta)\)
What we believe about \(\theta\) before seeing data


Likelihood
\(P(\text{data} \mid \theta)\)
How probable is the observed data, given \(\theta\)?


Normalizing constant
\(P(\text{data})\)
Ensures the posterior is a proper probability distribution


Posterior
\(P(\theta \mid \text{data})\)
Updated belief about \(\theta\) after seeing data





Present the full formula first, then the table. Give students 10–15 seconds of silence to absorb the structure before speaking.
The decision to call P(data) the “normalizing constant” rather than “marginal likelihood” is deliberate. “Normalizing constant” is both accurate and intuitive: it is a constant that normalises (rescales) the numerator so the result is a proper probability distribution that sums to 1. The “marginal likelihood” terminology is technically precise but carries connotations – model comparison, integration over parameters – that have not been built up yet and would cause confusion at this stage.
Walk through each term: - P(theta): beliefs before the data. A distribution over parameter space. - P(data|theta): for a specific parameter value, how probable is the data we actually observed? Scores every hypothesis by its compatibility with the observation. - P(data): the probability of the data we observed, averaged over all possible parameter values weighted by the prior. A single number, not a function of theta. - P(theta|data): what we should believe after combining prior and evidence.
The key conceptual point: the formula divides the prior-weighted likelihood by a constant that makes the whole thing sum to 1. That is the only role of the denominator.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Ingredient 1: The Prior \(P(\theta)\)
The prior encodes what we believe about \(\theta\) before observing data.

For the globe toss, \(\theta \in [0, 1]\) is the proportion of the Earth’s surface covered by water.




Flat prior: Every value of \(\theta\) is equally plausible. \[
P(\theta) = \text{Uniform}(0, 1)
\tag{4}\]
Weakly informative prior: More mass on \(\theta > 0.5\) – we believe it is more likely than not that more than half the globe is water.

Supercertain prior: Almost all mass at \(\theta = 0.5\) – we are nearly convinced the globe is exactly half water.










Important


Priors are scientific assumptions – they should be justified. Every analysis makes prior assumptions. Bayesian analysis makes them explicit and auditable. A flat prior is still a prior: it asserts that “0% water” is exactly as plausible as “70% water.”





The common criticism: “isn’t choosing a prior subjective?” Several responses:
First: every analysis makes prior assumptions – about which variables to include, what functional form to assume, what significance threshold to use. Bayesian analysis simply makes those assumptions visible and forces the analyst to defend them.
Second: a flat prior is itself a substantive assumption. For many parameters (regression coefficients, effect sizes), a completely flat prior is actually less defensible than a weakly informative one based on domain knowledge.
Third: the prior’s influence diminishes as data accumulates (demonstrated later in the session). With large samples, different reasonable priors lead to nearly identical posteriors – the data wins.
Fourth: prior sensitivity analysis is standard practice. If conclusions change dramatically with different priors, that is a scientific finding: the data are not very informative about the parameter. If conclusions are robust, you can report that with confidence.
Reference: Gelman and Shalizi (2013, British Journal of Mathematical and Statistical Psychology) offer a careful philosophical discussion.
On the significance threshold: choosing alpha = 0.05 is itself a prior-like decision – it is a threshold not derived from the data or the research question. It is arguably more arbitrary than a theoretically motivated prior.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Ingredient 2: The Likelihood \(P(\text{data} \mid \theta)\)
The likelihood scores each value of \(\theta\) by how well it explains the observed data.

For the globe toss, a natural data-generating model is the Binomial distribution:
\[
P(\text{data} \mid \theta) = P(W, N \mid \theta) = \binom{N}{W} \theta^{W} (1 - \theta)^{N - W}
\tag{5}\]
where \(W\) = water observations, \(N\) = total tosses.









Note


Likelihood family. The specific distributional formula – the Binomial – is our likelihood family. It formalises our assumption about the data-generating process: each toss is an independent binary trial with probability \(\theta\) of water. Different research contexts call for different likelihood families: the Bernoulli for single binary outcomes (the Binomial is a sum of Bernoulli trials), the Gaussian for continuous outcomes, the Poisson for count data. In the coming weeks we will work with several of these. The prior–likelihood–posterior logic of Bayes’ theorem (Equation 3) remains exactly the same regardless of which likelihood family we choose – only the formula inside changes.





The introduction of “likelihood family” is deliberate forward-priming. When we reach logistic regression (week 10), students will already have the concept: we are choosing a Bernoulli/Binomial likelihood family rather than a Gaussian one. The workflow – prior, likelihood, posterior – does not change. Only the family does.
An important technical note: P(data|theta) is NOT a probability distribution over theta. Integrating it over theta from 0 to 1 does not give 1 in general. It is a function of theta that gives the probability of the observed data under each possible parameter value. This is why we need the normalizing constant in the denominator of Bayes’ theorem.
The maximum likelihood estimate (MLE) – the value of theta that maximises the likelihood – is W/N. For W=4, N=6: MLE = 4/6 ≈ 0.667. Students who have seen frequentist regression know MLE estimation. This is a useful bridge.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Ingredient 3: The Posterior \(P(\theta \mid \text{data})\)
The posterior is the updated distribution of \(\theta\) after observing data.

\[
P(\theta \mid \text{data}) = \frac{P(\text{data} \mid \theta) \cdot P(\theta)}{P(\text{data})}
\tag{6}\]



Always a full probability distribution over \(\theta\) – not a point estimate, not a \(p\)-value
Expresses remaining uncertainty after combining prior knowledge with evidence
More concentrated than the prior – data (usually) reduces uncertainty
Today’s posterior is tomorrow’s prior – updating is sequential and coherent










Tip


The posterior contains everything we know about \(\theta\) after observing the data. Point summaries – mean, median, mode – can be extracted from it, but the full distribution is richer. In coming weeks, we will work routinely with full posteriors rather than just their summary statistics.





Three things to emphasise here:

“Full distribution” – the payoff of the framework. We do not get a single number, we get a distribution. This carries full uncertainty quantification. A point estimate discards all that information.
“Sequential coherence” – today’s posterior is tomorrow’s prior. If we see new data, we update from the current posterior. The result is identical to fitting the model to all data simultaneously (for exchangeable observations). We will demonstrate this graphically later.
The MAP estimate (mode of the posterior) equals the MLE when the prior is flat. OLS regression is a special case of MAP estimation with a flat prior and Gaussian likelihood. This connects Bayesian and frequentist frameworks.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
What Is \(P(\text{data})\)?
\[
P(\theta \mid \text{data}) = \frac{P(\text{data} \mid \theta) \cdot P(\theta)}{P(\text{data})}
\tag{7}\]

\(P(\text{data})\) is the probability of the observed data, averaged over all possible parameter values, weighted by the prior:
\[
P(\text{data}) = \int_{\Theta} P(\text{data} \mid \theta) \cdot P(\theta) \, d\theta
\quad \text{(continuous)}
\tag{8}\]
\[
P(\text{data}) = \sum_{\theta \in \Theta} P(\text{data} \mid \theta) \cdot P(\theta)
\quad \text{(discrete)}
\tag{9}\]


Think of it as: the total plausibility of the data across all possible ways it could have been generated – in every possible world \(\theta\), weighted by how plausible that world was to begin with.


It ensures \(\int P(\theta \mid \text{data}) \, d\theta = 1\): the posterior is a proper probability distribution.

The “all possible ways the data could have been generated” framing is a useful intuition pump, related to McElreath’s “garden of forking paths” metaphor. For the globe toss: imagine every possible world (every value of theta). In each world, the observed data (4 waters in 6 tosses) has some probability. P(data) averages those probabilities across all worlds, weighted by how plausible each world was a priori.
For the discrete 6-sided globe: P(data) is a sum of 7 terms – trivially computable by hand. Students will do this in the exercise. With a flat prior (1/7 each), P(data) = (1/7) × sum of likelihoods ≈ 0.122. This single number ensures the 7 posterior probabilities sum to 1.
The conceptual message: P(data) is not mysterious. It is just a rescaling factor. Its only job is to ensure the posterior is a proper probability distribution.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Why the Denominator Gets Hard


6-sided globe (today): trivially computable.
\[
P(\text{data}) = \sum_{k=0}^{6} P(\text{data} \mid \theta_k) \cdot P(\theta_k)
\tag{10}\]
7 terms. Arithmetic on a napkin.
Simple linear regression – three parameters: \(\alpha\), \(\beta\), \(\sigma\):
\[
P(\text{data}) = \iiint P(\text{data} \mid \alpha, \beta, \sigma) \cdot P(\alpha, \beta, \sigma) \, d\alpha\, d\beta\, d\sigma
\tag{11}\]
A three-dimensional integral. Rarely has a closed form.

Multilevel model with \(k\) parameters:
\[
P(\text{data}) = \int_{\mathbb{R}^k} P(\text{data} \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}) \cdot P(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \, d\boldsymbol{\theta}
\tag{12}\]
\(k\)-dimensional integral. No analytical solution for most realistic models.







Note


This is exactly the problem MCMC (next week) solves – not by computing \(P(\text{data})\), but by sampling from the posterior without ever evaluating the denominator.






The triple integral in the linear regression example uses three integral signs – one for each parameter (alpha, beta, sigma). This is standard mathematical notation for a volume integral over three-dimensional parameter space.
The dimensional explosion makes the computational problem visceral: - 7 hypotheses: trivial sum - 3 continuous parameters on a 1000-point grid: 10^9 evaluations - 50 parameters (a modest multilevel model): 10^150 evaluations - Observable universe: ~10^80 atoms
The point is not despair but motivation. MCMC is an elegant solution: instead of evaluating the posterior everywhere, construct a random walk that visits regions proportional to their posterior probability. This sidesteps the denominator entirely and is the topic of week 7.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Proportional Form
\(P(\text{data})\) is a constant – it does not depend on \(\theta\).

The same number rescales every value of \(P(\text{data} \mid \theta) \cdot P(\theta)\) across the whole parameter space.


So the shape of the posterior is fully determined by the numerator:
\[
P(\theta \mid \text{data}) \propto P(\text{data} \mid \theta) \cdot P(\theta)
\tag{13}\]
“Posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior.”









Note


\(P(\text{data})\) is simply a rescaling number – the accountant that makes the posterior sum to 1. It is conceptually required by the full theorem (Equation 3) and formally important, but its shape plays no role. The proportional form (Equation 13) tells us everything about the posterior’s shape.
This is why, although Bayes’ theorem has four components, we speak of three ingredients: prior, likelihood, posterior. The normalizing constant is background bookkeeping, not a scientific ingredient.
Next week: MCMC algorithms work exclusively with the proportional form – they compare ratios of posterior values, so \(P(\text{data})\) cancels out. This is precisely why MCMC never needs to evaluate the integral in the denominator.





This slide provides the conceptual closure of Part IV. The proportional form is the version you will see most often in textbooks and code. But it is crucial that students understand it as a simplification of the full theorem, not a separate result.
The three-ingredients framing: prior, likelihood, posterior. Four components in the formula, but three scientific ingredients. The normalizing constant is not a scientific choice – it is uniquely determined by the prior and likelihood. Once you choose the prior and the likelihood family, P(data) is fixed.
The connection to MCMC: the Metropolis algorithm (week 7) proposes a step from theta_current to theta_proposed, and accepts or rejects based on the ratio P(theta_proposed|data) / P(theta_current|data). Since P(data) appears in both numerator and denominator of that ratio, it cancels out. MCMC never needs to evaluate the integral. This is the elegant technical insight that makes Bayesian inference computationally feasible for realistic models.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
A Tractable Model: The 6-Sided Globe
Imagine the Earth as a 6-sided cube – like a die. Each face is either Land (L) or Water (W).

The unknown parameter \(\theta\) = proportion of water faces:
\[
\theta \in \left\{\frac{0}{6},\;\frac{1}{6},\;\frac{2}{6},\;\frac{3}{6},\;\frac{4}{6},\;\frac{5}{6},\;\frac{6}{6}\right\}
\tag{14}\]
Exactly 7 hypotheses – one for each possible number of water faces.




Prior: We start flat – every hypothesis equally plausible.
\[
P(\theta_k) = \frac{1}{7} \quad \text{for } k \in \{0,\ldots,6\}
\tag{15}\]

Observed data: \(N = 6\) tosses, \(W = 4\) waters.
\[
\text{data} = (W = 4,\; N = 6)
\]










Note


This is a deliberate simplification of the real continuous problem. The discrete 6-sided version makes every step of Bayes’ theorem computable by arithmetic.
