The logic is identical to the continuous case – only the number of hypotheses differs. The globe toss as a teaching device is adapted from McElreath (2020).





The 6-sided globe is pedagogically preferable to a 10-sided version because 7 hypotheses fit in a compact table.
W=4, N=6 is chosen so the MLE = 4/6 ≈ 0.667 coincides exactly with one of the grid points (theta_4 = 4/6). This makes it easy to identify the posterior peak on the table and plot.
The flat prior (1/7 each) is not truly uninformative in a technical sense – Jeffreys’ prior would differ – but it is the natural pedagogical starting point.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Likelihood: Scoring Each Hypothesis
Our likelihood family is Binomial (Equation 5) – chosen because each toss is an independent binary trial (water or land). For each \(\theta_k\):
\[
P(W{=}4,\; N{=}6 \mid \theta_k) = \binom{6}{4} \theta_k^{4} (1 - \theta_k)^{2} = 15 \cdot \theta_k^{4} (1 - \theta_k)^{2}
\tag{16}\]












\(k\)
\(\theta_k\)
\(\theta_k^4\)
\((1-\theta_k)^2\)
\(P(W{=}4 \mid \theta_k)\)

0/6 = 0.000
0.000

1

5
5/6 ≈ 0.833
0.482

0.201


6
6/6 = 1.000
1.000
0.000
0.000






Peak at \(\theta_4 = 4/6 \approx 0.667 = W/N\). This is the Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE).

Walk through the table carefully. The binomial coefficient C(6,4) = 15 is constant across every row – it counts the number of distinct orderings of 4 waters and 2 lands in 6 tosses. What changes row by row is theta^4 * (1-theta)^2.
At theta=0: any water observation is impossible, so the likelihood is exactly 0. At theta=1: any land observation is impossible, so also exactly 0.
Sum of all likelihoods: 0 + 0.008 + 0.082 + 0.234 + 0.329 + 0.201 + 0 = 0.854.
With a flat prior of 1/7: P(data) = 0.854/7 ≈ 0.122. This is the normalizing constant.
Connection to likelihood family: the formula in this table IS the Binomial likelihood from eq-binomial. The likelihood family determines the formula. In week 8, using a Gaussian likelihood family, the formula will look different – but the table logic (score each hypothesis, multiply by prior, normalize) will be exactly the same.
The MLE connection: in frequentist maximum likelihood, 4/6 = 0.667 would be the estimate. In Bayesian analysis, we use the full likelihood column to update the prior, not just its maximum.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Posterior with Flat Prior: The Calculation
text:
Posterior with Flat Prior: The Calculation
With \(P(\theta_k) = 1/7\) for all \(k\):
\[
P(\theta_k \mid \text{data}) = \frac{P(\text{data} \mid \theta_k) \cdot \frac{1}{7}}{\displaystyle\sum_{j=0}^{6} P(\text{data} \mid \theta_j) \cdot \frac{1}{7}} = \frac{P(\text{data} \mid \theta_k)}{\displaystyle\sum_{j=0}^{6} P(\text{data} \mid \theta_j)}
\tag{17}\]

The \(\frac{1}{7}\) cancels – the posterior is the normalised likelihood.


\[
P(\text{data}) = \frac{1}{7} \times (0 + 0.008 + 0.082 + 0.234 + 0.329 + 0.201 + 0) = \frac{0.854}{7} \approx 0.122
\tag{18}\]









Note


With a flat prior, the prior cancels from the calculation entirely. The posterior is driven solely by the data. This is the neutral starting point – no prior knowledge, just the evidence.





This is the full Bayes’ theorem calculation made explicit. Every term corresponds to a row in the likelihood table.
Key insight: with a flat prior, the posterior IS the normalised likelihood. The prior has no influence on shape – only the data matters. This will change when we introduce non-flat priors.
The connection to frequentist MLE: under a flat prior, Bayesian MAP (the posterior mode) = frequentist MLE. The two approaches agree when the prior is uninformative. They diverge as the prior becomes more informative – and the extent of divergence diminishes as data accumulates.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Flat Prior: Posterior = Normalised Likelihood


Figure 1: Flat prior: the posterior mirrors the likelihood. W = 4, N = 6.

With an uninformative prior, the data alone determines the posterior shape. All five columns are proportional to one another.

Visual observations to narrate: - The prior (blue) is flat – all 7 bars identical height. - The likelihood (gold) peaks at 4/6 and falls off symmetrically. - The posterior (red) is identical in shape to the likelihood, just differently scaled to sum to 1.
Ask students: “Does this feel right as a starting point?” Provoke them to think about whether a flat prior is defensible. The following slides show what happens when prior knowledge is incorporated.

title: QMIR 2026
section: The Three Priors at a Glance
text:
The Three Priors at a Glance
All three priors cover the same 7 hypotheses – they differ only in where they place belief.












\(k\)
\(\theta_k\)
Water faces
Flat \(P(\theta_k)\)
Weak \(P(\theta_k)\)
Supercertain \(P(\theta_k)\)

0/6

Flat: completely agnostic – equal weight to every hypothesis
Weak: modest scientific prior – \(\theta > 0.5\) is twice as plausible as \(\theta \leq 0.5\) (raw weights 2:1, sum = 10, normalised)
Supercertain: near-dogmatic conviction that \(\theta = 0.5\) – almost no mass elsewhere



This table is the reference slide for the three priors. Students can refer back to it throughout the session and in the homework.
The weak prior uses raw weights c(1,1,1,1,2,2,2): four hypotheses at weight 1 (theta ≤ 0.5) and three at weight 2 (theta > 0.5). Sum = 4 + 6 = 10. After normalising: 1/10 = 0.100 for the low-weight hypotheses and 2/10 = 0.200 for the high-weight ones.
The supercertain prior: 0.94 at theta=0.5, 0.01 at all others. Sum = 0.94 + 6×0.01 = 1.00. Already normalised.
Point out to students: all three are valid probability distributions – they all sum to 1. They differ only in where probability mass is concentrated. The question is always whether the prior’s placement of mass can be scientifically justified.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Weakly Informative Prior: Adding Scientific Knowledge
text:
Weakly Informative Prior: Adding Scientific Knowledge
We believe – on scientific grounds – that it is more likely the globe is more than half covered in water. We encode this modestly: hypotheses with \(\theta > 0.5\) get double the prior weight.



\[
P(\theta_k) \propto \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \theta_k \leq 0.5 \\ 2 & \text{if } \theta_k > 0.5 \end{cases}
\tag{19}\]
Raw weights: \(\boldsymbol{w} = (1,1,1,1,2,2,2)\), sum \(= 10\)
\[
\Rightarrow P(\theta_k) = \left(\tfrac{1}{10},\tfrac{1}{10},\tfrac{1}{10},\tfrac{1}{10},\tfrac{2}{10},\tfrac{2}{10},\tfrac{2}{10}\right)
\tag{20}\]

. . .
This prior does not rule anything out – every hypothesis remains possible. It merely encodes a moderate scientific belief that a water-majority globe is more plausible.







Note


This is what “weakly informative” means in practice: the prior has a preferred direction, but it does not concentrate belief strongly enough to override moderate evidence.






The 2:1 weight ratio is arbitrary but simple and illustrative. In practice, a researcher would justify the prior based on domain knowledge, previous studies, or physical theory.
Normalisation: raw weights c(1,1,1,1,2,2,2). There are 4 hypotheses at weight 1 (theta = 0/6, 1/6, 2/6, 3/6) and 3 hypotheses at weight 2 (theta = 4/6, 5/6, 6/6). Sum = 4×1 + 3×2 = 4 + 6 = 10. Each weight-1 hypothesis gets 1/10 = 0.1; each weight-2 hypothesis gets 2/10 = 0.2. Together: 4×0.1 + 3×0.2 = 0.4 + 0.6 = 1.0. ✓

title: QMIR 2026
section: Weakly Informative Prior: Posterior Shifts Right
text:
Weakly Informative Prior: Posterior Shifts Right


Figure 2: Weakly informative prior shifts posterior toward higher θ. W = 4, N = 6.

The prior pushes probability mass toward \(\theta > 0.5\), reducing posterior weight on land-majority hypotheses. With only \(N = 6\) tosses, the data cannot fully override a prior – but it does constrain it.

Point out the shift visually: theta=4/6 and 5/6 gain posterior weight relative to the flat-prior case, while theta=1/6 and 2/6 lose some. The posterior has moved right.
The shift is modest because the prior is only weakly informative (2:1 ratio), and the data (W=4/6, favouring theta ≈ 0.667) happens to be roughly consistent with the prior’s preferred direction. If the data were pulling in a different direction – say, mostly land – the posterior would be a more visible compromise.
Ask: “With only 6 tosses, how much should the prior matter?” Answer: quite a bit. 6 observations is limited evidence. The next two slides demonstrate the self-correcting property: with more data, the prior influence diminishes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Supercertain Prior
Suppose we are nearly convinced the globe is exactly half water: \(\theta = 3/6 = 0.5\).
\[
P(\theta_k) = (0.01,\; 0.01,\; 0.01,\; 0.94,\; 0.01,\; 0.01,\; 0.01)
\tag{21}\]
Almost all prior mass is at \(\theta = 0.5\). The data supports \(\theta \approx 0.67\).


Figure 3: Supercertain prior: 6 tosses cannot shift a highly concentrated belief.

A strong prior resists the data. 6 tosses is not sufficient evidence to override a nearly certain prior.

The supercertain prior (94% at theta=0.5) overwhelms 6 observations. The posterior stays concentrated near 0.5 despite the data suggesting 0.667.
Ask students: “Is this a problem or a feature?” The answer depends on context: - If the prior is well-founded (strong theory or previous experiments with thousands of observations), a prior that resists weak data is scientifically appropriate – new evidence should need to be substantial to overturn established knowledge. - If the prior is poorly motivated or dogmatic, this is a liability.
This is exactly why prior sensitivity analysis matters: report how much the conclusions change when you vary the prior. If conclusions are entirely prior-driven and unresponsive to data, that is a finding worth reporting.
The supercertain prior is deliberately extreme to make the effect visible in 7 bars. In practice, such concentrated priors would require very strong scientific justification.

title: QMIR 2026
section: The Self-Correcting Property: More Data Wins
text:
The Self-Correcting Property: More Data Wins
What happens with 10 times more data – but the same observed proportion \(W/N = 4/6\)?


Figure 4: With N = 60, all three priors converge to the same posterior.

With 60 tosses, even the supercertain prior is starts to move toward the data-driven likelihood. The data wins. All posteriors converge.

This is the self-correcting property of Bayesian inference: under regularity conditions, the posterior converges to a spike at the true parameter value as N → ∞, regardless of the (non-degenerate) prior.
Practical implication: with large samples, prior choices do not matter much. Two researchers who started with very different priors will arrive at nearly identical posteriors. This is why Bayesian and frequentist analyses tend to agree with large samples – the prior becomes negligible relative to the likelihood.
Corollary: with small samples, priors matter more. The Bayesian framework scales the influence of the prior naturally with sample size, without requiring any arbitrary tuning parameter.
For students who worry about prior subjectivity: this is the empirical answer. Priors matter when data are scarce, and converge when data are abundant. Making priors explicit and subjecting them to sensitivity analysis is more scientifically honest than pretending prior beliefs do not exist.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Sequential Updating: Learning Toss by Toss
text:
Sequential Updating: Learning Toss by Toss
Bayes’ theorem can be applied one observation at a time. Today’s posterior is tomorrow’s prior (McElreath 2020).


Figure 5: Sequential updating from flat prior: W, W, L, W.

Walk through each panel from left to right: - Panel 1 (flat prior): all 7 hypotheses equally plausible before any tosses. - Panel 2 (after W): water shifts mass toward higher theta. Hypotheses where most faces are water become more plausible. - Panel 3 (after W, W): another water observation pushes the distribution further right. - Panel 4 (after W, W, L): observing land pulls mass back slightly toward intermediate values (3/6, 4/6 recover). - Panel 5 (after W, W, L, W): 3 waters in 4 tosses. The distribution concentrates around 4/6.
Key property: computing the posterior from all 4 tosses simultaneously from the flat prior gives exactly the same result as this sequential procedure. Order does not matter for exchangeable observations.
The practical implication for applied work: you do not need all data at once. You can update the model incrementally as new data arrives. The result is always the same as a full analysis on the combined dataset.

title: QMIR 2026
section: The Formal Logic of Sequential Updating
text:
The Formal Logic of Sequential Updating
At each step, Bayes’ theorem (Equation 3) is applied with the previous posterior as the new prior:
\[
P(\theta \mid \text{data}_{1:t}) \propto P(\text{data}_t \mid \theta) \cdot \underbrace{P(\theta \mid \text{data}_{1:t-1})}_{\text{previous posterior = new prior}}
\tag{22}\]

This follows directly from the product rule of probability – assuming tosses are independent given \(\theta\):
\[
P(\theta \mid \text{data}_{1:T}) \propto P(\theta) \cdot \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(\text{data}_t \mid \theta)
\tag{23}\]









Note


Result: Updating one observation at a time gives exactly the same posterior as fitting the model to all observations simultaneously. Bayesian updating is fully coherent – the path to the posterior does not matter, only the data (and the prior).





This slide provides the formal justification for sequential updating. The key assumption is conditional independence of observations given theta: each toss is an independent Bernoulli trial. Under this assumption, the joint likelihood factors as a product of individual likelihoods.
The formal derivation: P(theta | data_1:T) ∝ P(data_1:T | theta) * P(theta) = P(theta) * prod_t P(data_t | theta) by independence. This is the same expression regardless of whether we compute it in one go or sequentially.
Practical implications: 1. Models can be updated incrementally as new data arrive – no need to refit from scratch. 2. Meta-analysis has a natural Bayesian interpretation: each study is a sequential update. 3. The result is always the same as a joint analysis – coherence is guaranteed by the probability calculus, not by convention.
If a student asks: “What if I have a bad prior at the start?” The self-correcting property from the previous slides answers this: with enough data, the prior is overwhelmed. Sequential updating with bad priors corrects itself over time.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
From 7 Hypotheses to 1000 Grid Points
The same logic – prior, likelihood family, posterior – works with a finer grid over continuous \(\theta \in [0, 1]\).







Note


In the earlier example, we simplified the problem by treating \(\theta\) as if it could only take 7 possible values—like approximating the Earth with a 6-sided cube instead of a smooth sphere. Grid approximation works by increasing the number of possible values for \(\theta\). In code, this is done with length.out in theta_grid. Setting length.out = 7 reproduces the simplified example, while length.out = 1000 effectively “slices” the parameter space into many small pieces—like approximating the globe with many tiny flat panels. As the number of grid points increases, the approximation becomes closer to a smooth continuous parameter.






View source
# ── Change these to explore ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
W <- 4 # observed waters  ← try 1, 4, 40, 400
N <- 6 # total tosses     ← try 6, 60, 600
prior_type <- "flat" # prior            ← try "flat", "weak", "supercertain"

# ── Grid ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
theta_grid <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 1000)

# ── Prior ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
prior <- switch(
  prior_type,
  flat = rep(1, 1000),
  weak = ifelse(theta_grid > 0.5, 2, 1), # more mass above 0.5
  supercertain = dnorm(theta_grid, mean = 0.5, sd = 0.02) # spike at 0.5
)
prior <- prior / sum(prior)

# ── Likelihood: Binomial family (eq. 8) ──────────────────────────────────────
likelihood <- dbinom(W, size = N, prob = theta_grid)

# ── Full Bayes' theorem (eq. 5) ───────────────────────────────────────────────
numerator <- likelihood * prior
normalizing_const <- sum(numerator) # P(data) ≈ integral of numerator
posterior <- numerator / normalizing_const

cat("P(data) ≈", round(normalizing_const, 5), "\n")
cat("sum(posterior) =", round(sum(posterior), 6), "\n") # should be ≈ 1



Walk through each block of the code – each corresponds to a term in Bayes’ theorem.
prior: P(theta), one of three shapes matching exactly the three priors students have seen on the discrete slides. The continuous version of “weak” uses a step function at 0.5; “supercertain” uses a narrow Normal distribution with sd=0.02.
likelihood: P(data|theta) at every grid point. The dbinom() function IS our Binomial likelihood family (eq-binomial). The family choice determines the function used here.
numerator: P(data|theta) * P(theta) – the numerator of Bayes’ theorem.
normalizing_const: sum(numerator) – the discrete approximation of P(data), the normalizing constant. This IS the integral in eq-pdata-cont, approximated by a sum over 1000 points.
posterior: numerator / normalizing_const – the full posterior, verified to sum to 1 by the cat() line.
Encourage students to try all combinations of W, N, and prior_type. The key patterns to find: - W=4, N=6, flat → posterior = normalised likelihood (matches the discrete figure) - W=4, N=6, weak → posterior shifts right - W=4, N=6, supercertain → posterior clings to 0.5 - W=40, N=60, all priors → priors converge - W=400, N=600 → very narrow posterior spike

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Playground: Explore the Posterior
You can copy the code below into an empty Quarto code cell and play around to get an intuition for how the posterior changes for different types of priors and numbers of observations.


View source
# ── Change these ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
W <- 40000 # try 40, 400, 4000
N <- 60000 # try 60, 600, 6000
prior_type <- "weak" # try: prior="weak", "supercertain"; W=40

theta_grid <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 1000)
prior <- switch(
  prior_type,
  flat = rep(1, 1000),
  weak = ifelse(theta_grid > 0.5, 2, 1),
  supercertain = dnorm(theta_grid, mean = 0.5, sd = 0.02)
)
prior <- prior / sum(prior)
likelihood <- dbinom(W, size = N, prob = theta_grid)
numerator <- likelihood * prior
posterior <- numerator / sum(numerator)

tibble(
  theta = theta_grid,
  Prior = prior / max(prior),
  Likelihood = likelihood / max(likelihood),
  Posterior = posterior / max(posterior)
) |>
  pivot_longer(-theta, names_to = "dist", values_to = "val") |>
  mutate(dist = factor(dist, levels = c("Prior", "Likelihood", "Posterior"))) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = theta, y = val, color = dist, linetype = dist)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("steelblue", "goldenrod3", "firebrick"),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  scale_linetype_manual(
    values = c("solid", "dashed", "twodash"),
    name = NULL
  ) +

  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    aspect.ratio = 0.42,
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  ) +
  labs(
    x = expression(theta),
    y = "Scaled density (max = 1)",
    caption = paste0("prior = ", prior_type, "  |  W = ", W, "  |  N = ", N)
  )




The three curves are each scaled by their own maximum (divided by max()) so they are visually comparable on the same y-axis regardless of absolute scale. This is for display purposes only – the posterior values are correctly normalised.
Patterns to discuss: 1. flat prior, any W/N: posterior = likelihood shape 2. weak prior, W=4/6: posterior shifted right (consistent with prior) 3. supercertain prior, W=4/6: posterior clings to 0.5 despite data 4. any prior, W=40/N=60: all posteriors converge on 2/3 5. any prior, W=400/N=600: very narrow spike at 2/3
Students should run this code themselves in the exercise and homework, varying the parameters to build intuition. The three patterns they should be able to articulate by the end: (a) Flat prior → posterior = normalised likelihood (b) Informative prior → posterior is a compromise between prior and likelihood (c) More data → posterior concentrates regardless of prior

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Three Ideas to Take Away

1. Probability as degree of belief
Probability quantifies graded beliefs about uncertain quantities. This makes it applicable to parameters and hypotheses – not just to repeatable experiments.


2. Everything uncertain gets a distribution
Parameters are uncertain; we represent that uncertainty with probability distributions. The data is fixed – we observed it. The parameter is what we infer. Today’s posterior is tomorrow’s prior.


3. Posterior = likelihood × prior, normalised
\[
P(\theta \mid \text{data}) = \frac{P(\text{data} \mid \theta) \cdot P(\theta)}{P(\text{data})} \propto P(\text{data} \mid \theta) \cdot P(\theta)
\tag{24}\]
Three ingredients (prior, likelihood family, posterior) and a normalizing constant that ensures a valid probability scale.

These three ideas should be retainable and explainable. If a student can walk a friend through the globe toss example using these three ideas, they have understood the session.
Reinforce the connection forward: - Weeks 8–13: every model produces a posterior over regression coefficients, intercepts, variance parameters, and group-level effects. - We will always ask: what do we believe about this parameter after seeing the data? How much uncertainty remains? - The prior forces explicit assumptions. The likelihood family encodes the data-generating assumption. The posterior carries full uncertainty.
Invite remaining questions before the cliffhanger.

title: QMIR 2026
section: The Cliffhanger: Why This Breaks at Scale
text:
The Cliffhanger: Why This Breaks at Scale
Consider the simplest regression model with three parameters: \(\alpha\), \(\beta\), \(\sigma\).

Grid approximation with 1000 points per parameter:
\[
1000^3 = 10^9 \text{ grid cells}
\]


A multilevel model with 50 parameters:
\[
1000^{50} = 10^{150} \text{ grid cells}
\]
The observable universe contains approximately \(10^{80}\) atoms.









Important


Grid approximation is computationally infeasible for any realistic model. We need a fundamentally different approach – one that explores the posterior rather than evaluating it everywhere.
That approach is Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC). The way to understand it involves a king, a chain of islands, and a surprisingly simple rule about when to move and when to stay. Crucially: MCMC never needs to compute \(P(\text{data})\) at all – which is exactly why it can scale to realistic models.





The curse of dimensionality: in k-dimensional space, the volume grows exponentially with k. Most of that volume has near-zero posterior probability. A grid wastes almost all its computation in the desert of parameter space where nothing interesting is happening.
MCMC’s key insight: do not evaluate the posterior everywhere. Instead, construct a random walk that visits regions in proportion to their posterior probability. Spend time where the posterior mass actually is.
The Metropolis algorithm achieves this with one simple rule: propose a random step; accept it if the posterior is higher there; accept it with probability equal to the posterior ratio if lower. This rule uses only the ratio P(posterior_proposed) / P(posterior_current). The normalizing constant P(data) cancels in that ratio. MCMC never has to evaluate the integral.
Do not resolve this mystery now. The cliffhanger should create genuine curiosity. One concrete sentence: “After next week, when brms prints ‘Chains: 4, Iterations: 2000’, you will know exactly what is happening and why.”

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Session Summary










#
Learning goal
Answer in brief
Slide




1
Frequentist vs. Bayesian probability
Frequentist = long-run frequency; Bayesian = degree of rational belief. Bayesian is more natural for parameter inference.
Section 2.1


2
Parameters as uncertain distributions
The parameter \(\theta\) is unknown – we represent that uncertainty with a probability distribution. The data is fixed and the parameter is what we infer.
Section 1.3


3
Three ingredients: prior, likelihood, posterior
Prior \(P(\theta)\): beliefs before data. Likelihood \(P(\text{data}\mid\theta)\): how well data fit each \(\theta\). Posterior \(P(\theta\mid\text{data})\): updated beliefs after data.
Section 3


4
Full Bayes’ theorem + normalizing constant
\(P(\theta\mid\text{data}) = \frac{P(\text{data}\mid\theta)\cdot P(\theta)}{P(\text{data})}\). \(P(\text{data})\) rescales the numerator so the posterior sums to 1.
Section 3.1


5
6-sided globe: Bayesian updating
Each observation updates the prior into a posterior and that posterior becomes the next prior. Repeating toss-by-toss yields the same result as fitting all data at once.
Section 5.10


6
Posterior changes with prior and data
Informative prior shifts posterior and more data overwhelms any reasonable prior (self-correcting property).
Section 5.9


7
Grid approximation in R
Define grid → compute prior and likelihood → multiply → divide by sum (= \(P(\text{data})\)) → posterior. Breaks down at scale → motivates MCMC (next week).
Section 6





This summary slide maps directly onto the seven learning goals announced at the start of the session. Use it as a rapid-fire recap in the final 3–4 minutes.
The slide references (Part II, Part III, etc.) correspond to the section headers in the deck. Students can navigate back to any section if they want to review before the homework.
After this slide, move to the homework overview. There is no in-class exercise this week – the homework replaces it.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Appendix A: Bayes’ Theorem from First Principles
text:
Appendix A: Bayes’ Theorem from First Principles

Bayes’ theorem is a direct consequence of the product rule of probability – no special assumptions required.



For any two events \(A\) and \(B\):
\[
P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)
\tag{25}\]




Setting the two expressions equal and dividing by \(P(B) > 0\):
\[
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}
\tag{26}\]




This is Bayes’ theorem in its most general form – with no reference to parameters, data, or inference.




Replacing \(A\) with \(\theta\) (parameter) and \(B\) with \(\text{data}\) (observations) gives the inferential form:
\[
P(\theta \mid \text{data}) = \frac{P(\text{data} \mid \theta) \cdot P(\theta)}{P(\text{data})}
\tag{27}\]


This slide makes explicit that Bayes’ theorem is not a statistical assumption or a modelling choice – it is a mathematical theorem derived from the axioms of probability theory.
The product rule P(A ∩ B) = P(A|B)·P(B) is one of the core axioms of conditional probability. Applying it symmetrically to P(A ∩ B) = P(B|A)·P(A) and equating the two expressions gives Bayes’ theorem directly.
The substitution A → theta, B → data is purely notational. It maps the abstract result onto the inferential setting: theta is the unknown quantity we want to learn about; data is the information we condition on.
The denominator P(B) = P(data) is the probability of the conditioning event – it ensures the conditional probability P(A|B) sums to 1 over all A. In the inferential setting, this is the normalizing constant we have discussed throughout the session.
This derivation is due to Thomas Bayes (published posthumously, 1763) and was independently developed and extended by Pierre-Simon Laplace (1812). The framework as a general epistemology for scientific inference is associated with Harold Jeffreys, Leonard Savage, Bruno de Finetti, and more recently E.T. Jaynes (Probability Theory: The Logic of Science, 2003).

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Appendix B: Common Likelihood Families
The likelihood family encodes the data-generating assumption. Different outcome types call for different families.


Figure 6: Shape of five common likelihood families. Parameters chosen for visual clarity.

This slide is a reference for students encountering these distributions in the homework, future sessions, or external reading.
Gaussian (Normal): the bell curve. Two parameters: mean mu (location) and standard deviation sigma (spread). Used for continuous outcomes with unbounded support. The likelihood family for standard linear regression.
Binomial(N, theta): the number of successes in N independent Bernoulli trials. Parameters: number of trials N and success probability theta. The likelihood family for the globe toss and any count-of-successes outcome.
Bernoulli(theta): a single binary trial – the special case Binomial(1, theta). Parameters: success probability theta. The likelihood family for logistic regression with binary outcomes (0/1).
Poisson(lambda): the number of events occurring in a fixed interval, with events independent and occurring at a constant rate lambda. Used for count data that is unbounded above. The likelihood family for event counts, rare events, and time-series of discrete occurrences.
Beta(alpha, beta): a flexible distribution defined on [0, 1]. Parameters alpha and beta control shape: alpha=beta gives symmetric distributions (uniform when both=1, peaked at 0.5 when both>1); alpha>beta gives right-skewed (mass near 1); alpha<beta gives left-skewed (mass near 0). The natural prior distribution for a proportion parameter theta – and the conjugate prior for the Binomial likelihood (the posterior is also Beta, making the update analytically tractable).
The conjugate Beta-Binomial relationship is worth mentioning: prior Beta(a,b) + data W successes in N trials → posterior Beta(a+W, b+N-W). This is the only case in our course where the posterior has a closed form. For everything else (Gaussian regression, multilevel models), we will need MCMC.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Appendix B: Likelihood Functions – Reference











Family
Parameters
Support
Likelihood \(P(\text{data} \mid \boldsymbol{\theta})\)
Typical use




Gaussian
\(\mu, \sigma\)
\(\mathbb{R}\)
\(\displaystyle\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(y-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\)
Continuous outcomes; linear regression


Binomial
\(N, \theta\)
\(\{0,\ldots,N\}\)
\(\displaystyle\binom{N}{W}\theta^W(1-\theta)^{N-W}\)
Count of successes; globe toss


Bernoulli
\(\theta\)
\(\{0,1\}\)
\(\theta^y(1-\theta)^{1-y}\)
Single binary trial; logistic regression


Poisson
\(\lambda\)
\(\{0,1,2,\ldots\}\)
\(\displaystyle\frac{\lambda^k e^{-\lambda}}{k!}\)
Event counts; rare events


Beta
\(\alpha,\beta\)
\([0,1]\)
\(\displaystyle\frac{\theta^{\alpha-1}(1-\theta)^{\beta-1}}{B(\alpha,\beta)}\)
Prior for proportions; conjugate to Binomial












Note


The Bernoulli is the Binomial with \(N = 1\). The Beta is most commonly used as a prior distribution rather than a likelihood. All these families appear in the proportional form of Bayes’ theorem (Equation 13): the likelihood function changes, but the update logic – prior × likelihood, normalise – is always the same.





This table is a compact reference for the five distributions students will encounter most often in this course and in their reading.
The Gaussian likelihood: the standard normal density. Parameters mu and sigma are the mean and standard deviation of the outcome. In linear regression, mu is modelled as a linear function of predictors (mu = alpha + beta*x), and sigma is estimated as a free parameter.
The Binomial likelihood: already encountered in the globe toss. W is the observed count of successes; N is the fixed number of trials; theta is the success probability.
The Bernoulli likelihood: P(y|theta) = theta^y * (1-theta)^(1-y). For y=1 (success): P = theta. For y=0 (failure): P = 1-theta. This is the link to logistic regression, where theta is modelled as a logistic function of predictors.
The Poisson likelihood: P(k|lambda) = lambda^k * e^{-lambda} / k!. Lambda is the expected count per unit time or space. Used when outcomes are non-negative integers with no fixed upper bound.
The Beta distribution: technically a distribution over [0,1] with a flexible shape. More commonly used as a prior for proportion parameters than as a likelihood. B(alpha, beta) in the denominator is the beta function – a normalizing constant.
For the take-home exam: students will primarily use Gaussian (linear regression, weeks 8–9), Bernoulli/Binomial (binary outcomes, week 10), and Gaussian with group structure (fixed effects, multilevel, weeks 11–12). The Poisson is introduced for completeness. The Beta is most relevant as a prior distribution.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Git & GitHub – A Folder Structure Reminder
text:
Git & GitHub – A Folder Structure Reminder
A few students ran into problems with last week’s homework. Here is a summary of how to set things up correctly.
One dedicated QMIR folder, at a stable path on your computer:
Documents/
  QMIR_2026/  ← your top-level course folder. Individual repos go INSIDE here, e.g:
    hw-w04/ 
    lab-w04/
    ...

✅ Place individual repositories inside the QMIR folder
❌ Do not turn the top-level QMIR_2026/ folder itself into a Git repo — Git repos inside Git repos cause problems
❌ Do not place your QMIR folder inside a cloud-synced folder (iCloud Drive, Google Drive, OneDrive, Dropbox) — syncing services and Git conflict with each other and corrupt repositories

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Tidyverse – A Coherent Ecosystem


Last week: packages are collections of functions.
The tidyverse is a collection of packages that share:

a common philosophy of how data should be structured
a consistent grammar (functions behave predictably)
seamless interoperability – they are designed to work together



View source
library(tidyverse)


One library() call loads the core set.




Source: Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel (2020)

title: QMIR 2026
section: The Tibble – A Better Data Frame
text:
The Tibble – A Better Data Frame


The tidyverse uses tibbles instead of base R data frames.
They are the same idea – rows × columns, each column one type – but with better defaults:

Prints only the first 10 rows
Shows column types below the header
Never converts strings to factors silently
Never creates row names




View source
library(gapminder)

# gapminder is already a tibble
gapminder


# A tibble: 1,704 × 6
   country     continent  year lifeExp      pop gdpPercap
   <fct>       <fct>     <int>   <dbl>    <int>     <dbl>

 2 Afghanistan Asia       1957    30.3  9240934      821.

# ℹ 1,694 more rows

title: QMIR 2026
text:
What is Tidy Data?

Tidy datasets are all alike, but every messy dataset is messy in its own way.
– Hadley Wickham


A dataset is tidy when:

Each variable has its own column
Each observation has its own row
Each value has its own cell

title: QMIR 2026
section: Tidy vs. Wide Data – An Example
text:
Tidy vs. Wide Data – An Example
Wide format (common in spreadsheets, not tidy):

gdpPercap_1957
gdpPercap_1962




Germany
7144

12902

Long (tidy) format (what the tidyverse expects):

year
gdpPercap




Germany
1952
7144


Germany
1957
10188


France
1952
7030


France
1957
8663






In the tidy version, year is a variable – a column. In the wide version, year is hiding inside column names. The tidyverse (and especially ggplot2) expects tidy data.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Reshaping with tidyr – A Brief Preview
text:
Reshaping with tidyr – A Brief Preview
You will encounter wide data in the wild. tidyr provides two functions to convert between formats:

pivot_longer() – wide → long (tidy)
pivot_wider() – long → wide



View source
# Hypothetical wide dataset
wide_data |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("gdpPercap_"),
    names_to = "year",
    values_to = "gdpPercap"
  )



We won’t go deep on reshaping today – the gapminder data is already tidy. But knowing these functions exist will save you hours when you start working with real-world data.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Data Science Cycle


Everything we do in this course fits somewhere in this cycle:
Import → Tidy → Transform → Visualise → Model → Communicate
Where we are today:

✅ Import – readr
✅ Tidy – we got lucky, gapminder is already tidy
✅ Transform – dplyr
👀 Visualise – ggplot2 preview
📅 Model – coming later in the course
✅ Communicate – Quarto, all along





Source: Wickham et al. (2023)

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Installing and Loading Packages
Before you can use a package, you need to:

Install it once (downloads from CRAN to your computer)
Load it in each session (makes its functions available)



View source
# Install once -- you only need to do this one time
install.packages("gapminder")
install.packages("tidyverse")

# Load every session -- goes at the top of your .qmd
library(tidyverse)
library(gapminder)









Tip


Never put install.packages() inside a Quarto document that you intend to render or share. Quarto blocks package installation during rendering for security reasons, so the document will fail to render and throw an error. In addition, even if it were allowed, it would attempt to reinstall the package every time the document is rendered, which can create unnecessary delays and compatibility issues in other users’ environments.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Data Import with readr
readr is the tidyverse package for importing rectangular data files.



Function
File type




read_csv("file.csv")
Comma-separated values


read_tsv("file.tsv")
Tab-separated values


read_delim("file.txt", delim = ";")
Any delimiter






View source
# Import a CSV file and assign it to an object
my_data <- read_csv("data/my_dataset.csv")




For other formats:


View source
library(haven)
my_data <- read_dta("data/dataset.dta") # Stata
my_data <- read_sav("data/dataset.sav") # SPSS

library(readxl)
my_data <- read_excel("data/dataset.xlsx") # Excel





Always assign the result to an object. read_csv("file.csv") alone reads the file but immediately discards it.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Data Export
To save a data frame to disk:


View source
# Write to CSV
write_csv(my_data, "output/my_dataset_clean.csv")

# Write to RDS (R's native format -- preserves types exactly)
saveRDS(my_data, "output/my_dataset_clean.rds")

# Read it back
my_data <- readRDS("output/my_dataset_clean.rds")











Format
Pros
Cons




.csv
Universal, opens in Excel
Loses type info (factors become strings)


.rds
Preserves all R types exactly
Only readable in R


.dta
Stata-compatible
Requires haven

title: QMIR 2026
section: dplyr – A Grammar of Data Manipulation
text:
dplyr – A Grammar of Data Manipulation


All dplyr functions follow the same pattern:

First argument: the data frame (automatically filled if working with pipe |>)
Subsequent arguments: what to do
Return value: always a (new) data frame


The core verbs:



Verb
What it does




filter()
Keep rows that meet a condition


select()
Keep or drop columns


mutate()
Create or modify variables


arrange()
Sort rows


summarise()
Compute summary statistics


group_by()
Group rows for grouped operations


ungroup()
Remove grouping

title: QMIR 2026
section: Putting It Together – A Full Pipeline

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Bad Practice vs. Good Practice


Bad practice – nested or intermediate objects:


View source
gap_2007 <- filter(gapminder, year == 2007)
gap_gdp <- mutate(gap_2007, gdp = pop * gdpPercap)
gap_grp <- group_by(gap_gdp, continent)
summarise(gap_grp, mean_gdp = mean(gdp))



Good practice – piped chain:


View source
gapminder |>
  filter(year == 2007) |>
  mutate(gdp = pop * gdpPercap) |>
  group_by(continent) |>
  summarise(mean_gdp = mean(gdp))




Both produce the same result. The piped version reads like a sentence and requires no intermediate objects. However, you should always be aware of what each individual pipe step does. Especially when learning, test each step separately in the console to make sure you understand how the data changes at every stage.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Grammar of Graphics
ggplot2 builds plots from layers. Every plot starts the same way:
ggplot(data = <DATA>, aes(x = <VAR>, y = <VAR>, ...)) +
  geom_*()

The three essential pieces:

ggplot() – sets up the canvas and declares the data
aes() – maps variables to visual properties (x, y, colour, size, …)
geom_*() – the geometric object that draws the data (points, lines, bars, …)




Note the + operator, not the pipe |>. ggplot2 layers are added with +.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
A First Scatterplot




View source
gapminder |>
  filter(year == 2007) |>
  ggplot(aes(
    x = gdpPercap,
    y = lifeExp,
    color = continent
  )) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    x = "GDP per Capita (USD)",
    y = "Life Expectancy (years)",
    color = "Continent"
  )








Figure 1: GDP per capita vs. life expectancy, 2007






The plot in Figure 1 shows the classic wealth-health relationship: richer countries tend to have longer life expectancies.
Notice:

data piped into ggplot()
aes() maps variables to visual properties
geom_point() adds the points layer
theme_bw() gives a clean look
labs() adds axis labels

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Log Scale and facet_wrap()




View source
gapminder |>
  filter(year == 2007) |>
  ggplot(aes(
    x = gdpPercap,
    y = lifeExp
  )) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  facet_wrap(~continent) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    x = "GDP per Capita (log scale)",
    y = "Life Expectancy"
  )








Figure 2: GDP vs. life expectancy by continent, 2007






scale_x_log10() – log-transforms the x axis, making the skewed GDP distribution easier to read.
facet_wrap(~ continent) – creates one small panel per continent.
We will go much deeper on ggplot2 next week. Today you are just seeing the shape of it.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Execution Options – Global vs. Cell Level
text:
Execution Options – Global vs. Cell Level
Last week you saw echo and eval as cell-level options. You can also set them globally in the YAML header so they apply to all cells at once:
execute:
  eval: true    # run all code cells
  echo: true    # show all source code
  warning: false  # suppress warnings (e.g. tidyverse startup)
  message: false  # suppress messages
  error: false  # stop on errors (set true to render despite errors)
  freeze: true  # don't re-run cells unless source changes

Cell-level options override global ones for that specific cell:
```{r}
#| echo: false   # overrides global echo: true for this cell only
my_plot
```

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Cell Options Reference








Option
Values
What it controls




eval
true / false
Whether the code is executed


echo
true / false
Whether the source code is shown


warning
true / false
Whether warnings appear in output


message
true / false
Whether messages appear in output


error
true / false
Whether errors stop rendering


include
true / false
Whether cell output appears at all


label
fig-* / tbl-* / any string
Cell identifier for cross-referencing


fig-cap
string
Caption for figure output

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Labels and Cross-References
Labelling a code cell that produces a figure enables automatic cross-references in your prose:


```{r}
#| label: fig-lifeexp
#| fig-cap: "Life expectancy over time"
#| message: false

gapminder |>
  group_by(year, continent) |>
  summarise(mean_le = mean(lifeExp),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = mean_le,
             color = continent)) +
  geom_line() +
  theme_bw()
```

As shown in @fig-lifeexp, life
expectancy has risen across all
continents since 1952.


The label must start with fig- for figures, tbl- for tables
@fig-lifeexp in prose becomes “Figure 1” (auto-numbered) in the rendered output
Figure numbers update automatically if you add or reorder figures
Works in HTML, PDF, and Word output

This is how academic papers keep figure references consistent. Your future self will thank you.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Suppressing Tidyverse Messages
When you load tidyverse, R prints a message listing attached packages. When you use read_csv(), it prints column types. These are useful interactively, but clutter your rendered document.

Suppress them with cell options or globally:


Cell level:
```{r}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
library(tidyverse)
library(gapminder)
```

Global YAML (applies to all cells):
execute:
  message: false
  warning: false



Prefer the global approach in homework submissions so your rendered PDF looks clean.

title: QMIR 2026
text:

Fast finishers: optional depth exercises at the bottom of the document

title: QMIR 2026

The homework follows the arc of today’s session. You will work with the gapminder dataset to practice dplyr wrangling and produce at least one properly labelled and captioned figure that you cross-reference in your prose.

title: QMIR 2026
text: What is Reproducible Research?

title: QMIR 2026
text:
What is Reproducible Research?



Reproducible research means:

Every results can be regenerated from raw data
Every transformation and coding decision is documented
Every table and figure can be recreated automatically
Analytical choices are transparent and traceable




Why this matters:

Reviewers can understand and verify your analysis
Coauthors can follow and extend your work
You can easily test alternative specifications (e.g., add variable X)
Your future self knows exactly what you did
If your laptop dies tomorrow, nothing is lost

title: QMIR 2026
text:
What is Reproducible Research?

Quarto \(\rightarrow\) integrates writing and code ✍️💻
R \(\rightarrow\) performs computation and analysis 📊🧮
Git \(\rightarrow\) tracks changes over time 🔄📝
GitHub \(\rightarrow\) enables remote storage and collaboration ☁️🤝

title: QMIR 2026
text:
What is Reproducible Research?
Used properly, this workflow promotes

Reproducibility
Transparency
Traceability
Automation

title: QMIR 2026
text:
What is Reproducible Research?

For small or one-off tasks, this workflow may seem excessive


Its advantages become substantial as projects increase in scope, duration, or collaboration

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Workflow













A

.qmd
(source document)



B

rendered output
(e.g. html, pdf, docx)



A->B





C

Local Git repository
(snapshot)



B->C





D

GitHub
(remote repository)



C->D








Figure 1: The basic workflow












Figure 2: Illustration of Quarto. Source: Posit Software, PBC (2025)





Repository: just a fancy word for (code-based) project folder with memory

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Markdown


# Section header

## A subsection

*This sentence is written in italics.*
**This one is bold.**
Using backticks formats text to `look like code`.

[This is a hyperlink](www.uni-mannheim.de)

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Output embedded in documents


## Example of embedded figure

```r
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| label: fig-demo
#| fig-cap: "A demo figure"
library(forcats)
library(ggplot2)

demo_data <- data.frame(
  country = c("Germany", "France", "Italy", "Spain", "Poland"),
  pop = c(83, 67, 60, 47, 38)
)

ggplot(
  data = demo_data,
  aes(x = fct_reorder(country, pop), y = pop)
) +
  geom_col(fill = "white", color = "black") +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(x = "Country", y = "Population in Million")
```

@fig-demo shows an embedded figure.

Example of embedded figure


View source
library(forcats)
library(ggplot2)

demo_data <- data.frame(
  country = c("Germany", "France", "Italy", "Spain", "Poland"),
  pop = c(83, 67, 60, 47, 38)
)

ggplot(
  data = demo_data,
  aes(x = fct_reorder(country, pop), y = pop)
) +
  geom_col(fill = "white", color = "black") +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(x = "Country", y = "Population in Million")








Figure 3: A demo figure





Figure 3 shows an embedded figure.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Version Control with Git

Repository (repo) \(\rightarrow\) project folder with memory 🗂️
git init \(\rightarrow\) Turns a normal folder into a Git repository 🚀
Stage (git add) \(\rightarrow\) Select changes you want to include in the next snapshot 📦
Commit \(\rightarrow\) A saved snapshot of staged changes 📸
Commit message \(\rightarrow\) Short explanation of what and why you changed something 💬
History (git log) \(\rightarrow\) Timeline of all committed snapshots 🕰️
Remote \(\rightarrow\) Online copy of your repository (e.g., GitHub) ☁️

title: QMIR 2026
text: Version Control with Git

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Version Control with Git
What we want to avoid:
analysis_final.R
analysis_final2.R
analysis_REALLY_final.R

draft1.pdf
draft2.pdf
draft2_final.pdf
This is manual version control – and it breaks down quickly.

Remember: Git tracks changes inside files – not by duplicating them

text:

title: QMIR 2026

GitHub as Infrastructure

Remote backup of your repositories ☁️
Collaboration via pull requests & issues 👥
Transparency & reproducibility 🔎
Platform for public research visibility 📢
Hub for open-source software development 🧩


Today, sharing code on GitHub is often part of the research output itself.

title: QMIR 2026
section: A Critical Note on Platform Choice
text:
A Critical Note on Platform Choice

GitHub is a US-based, privately owned company (Microsoft)
There are alternatives (e.g. GitLab, Codeberg)
Platform choice is therefore also a question of:

Data governance
Digital sovereignty
Infrastructure dependence

title: QMIR 2026
section: A Critical Note on Platform Choice
text:
A Critical Note on Platform Choice

GitHub is a US-based, privately owned company (Microsoft)
There are alternatives (e.g. GitLab, Codeberg)
Platform choice is therefore also a question of:

Data governance
Digital sovereignty
Infrastructure dependence



🔐 Reminder: Uploading data to US-hosted platforms can have legal implications. Never upload personal or restricted data without checking GDPR and institutional regulations.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
So why use GitHub?

It is industry standard 🌐
Most open-source projects are hosted there 🧑‍💻
Most active developers and scientists use it 🧪
Learning GitHub increases career portability 📚

It is industry standard 🌐
Most open-source projects are hosted there 🧑‍💻

For these pragmatic reasons, we will use GitHub in this course.

Step 1: Go to GitHub repo Step 2: Copy link Step 3: Open Positron Step 4: Click Open Folder from GitHub Step 5: Paste Link Step 6: Create Step 7: Change files -> show file changes viewe Step 8: Push wont work, you dont have access Step 9: For homeworks you will Step 10: Show how I see in GitHub the date and time of commits

Permitted Use: These assets are provided in this package for the official, work-related use of university staff and faculty (e.g., for presentations, posters, and publications) to create materials in line with the university’s corporate design. According to university policy, the use of the logo is strictly reserved for employees and, in specific cases, Ph.D. students.
Prohibited Use: Use of these assets by students (except for Ph.D. students on presentations and dissertations) and external partners is not permitted without prior, explicit authorization from the university’s Communications and Marketing department.

For a full overview of the university’s corporate design guidelines and official usage policies, please refer to the university’s official website. The brief descriptions provided here are for informational purposes only; the legally binding rules are those stated on the university’s website.
By using this package, you agree to abide by these terms and confirm that your use of the logo is in accordance with the university’s official policies.

title: Getting Started: Installation Guide
text: This course introduces you to a range of software tools which, taken together, form a powerful and contemporary toolbox for transparent, reproducible, and scalable empirical research. Rather than relying on a single piece of software, you will learn how different tools interact and complement one another — much like they do in real-world research workflows.

title: Getting Started: Installation Guide
text:
Configure Git
To use Git, you need to set a name and email address. These will be associated with every change (commit) you make, acting like a digital signature. You do not need to use your full name, but it is recommended.
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your@mail.com"

title: Getting Started: Installation Guide
text:
Create a GitHub account
If you don’t already have one, create a free GitHub account here.
GitHub will allow you to store repositories online, collaborate with others, and track your work from anywhere. We will use GitHub to distribute course materials.
