title: Week 9 Lab: Multivariate Regression and Interactions
text:
Before running any analysis, we load the packages we need and define a few shared colour constants that keep plots visually consistent. brms fits Bayesian regression models via Stan; bayesplot produces MCMC diagnostics; patchwork assembles multi-panel figures.

start_time <- Sys.time()  # used to measure execution time at the end

library(tidyverse)    # data manipulation and ggplot2
library(brms)         # Bayesian regression via Stan
library(bayesplot)    # MCMC diagnostic plots
library(patchwork)    # composing multi-panel figures

# Shared colour palette — reused in every plot so figures look consistent
col_1 <- "steelblue"
col_2 <- "#E07B39"
col_3 <- "#7B3F9E"
col_4 <- "goldenrod3"
chain_cols <- c(col_1, col_2, col_3, col_4)  # one colour per MCMC chain

title: Week 9 Lab: Multivariate Regression and Interactions
section: Dataset 1: Women in Parliament → Health Spending
text:
Dataset 1: Women in Parliament → Health Spending
The research question is: Does the share of women in parliament increase public health expenditure?
The key complication is confounding: wealthier countries simultaneously have more women in parliament and spend more on health. Failing to account for GDP per capita will cause us to over-estimate the legislative representation effect.
The true data-generating process (DGP) is:

gdp_pc ~ Normal(18, 7) — GDP per capita in thousands of USD (PPP)
women_parl = 15 + 0.9 × gdp_pc + noise — share of parliament seats held by women (%)
health_spend = 2 + 0.20 × gdp_pc + 0.12 × women_parl + noise — health expenditure (% GDP)

The true coefficient we want to recover is β_women = 0.12. Because GDP drives both women’s representation and health spending, a bivariate model will produce an estimate well above 0.12 — that excess is omitted variable bias (OVB).


Code
set.seed(2026)  # reproducibility: same data every time
n1 <- 120       # 120 synthetic country-years

# Simulate the confounder first, then the two outcome variables
gdp_pc       <- rnorm(n1, mean = 18, sd = 7)
women_parl   <- 15 + 0.9 * gdp_pc + rnorm(n1, 0, 5)
health_spend <- 2 + 0.2 * gdp_pc + 0.12 * women_parl + rnorm(n1, 0, 1.5)

df_controls <- tibble(gdp_pc, women_parl, health_spend)


We also pre-fit two brms models here — the bivariate model (no control) and the multivariate model (controlling for GDP) — so they are available for the OVB comparison plot and the LOO comparison later. Fitting takes a moment; cache: true in the slide deck means you will only wait once per machine.


Code
# Bivariate model: health spending ~ women in parliament only
# This model omits GDP, so it will overestimate β_women
fit_controls_biv <- brm(
  health_spend ~ women_parl,
  data = df_controls,
  prior = c(
    prior(normal(10, 5),    class = "Intercept"),  # baseline ~10% GDP
    prior(normal(0, 0.5),   class = "b"),           # weak: ±1.5pp per %
    prior(exponential(1),   class = "sigma")        # residual SD < 5 almost certainly
  ),
  chains = 4,
  iter  = 2000,
  warmup = 1000,
  seed  = 2026
)




Code
# Multivariate model: health spending ~ women in parliament + GDP per capita
# Including GDP closes the back-door path and removes the OVB
fit_controls <- brm(
  health_spend ~ women_parl + gdp_pc,
  data = df_controls,
  prior = c(
    prior(normal(10, 5),   class = "Intercept"),
    prior(normal(0, 0.5),  class = "b", coef = "women_parl"),  # ±1.5pp per %
    prior(normal(0, 1),    class = "b", coef = "gdp_pc"),       # ±3pp per $1k
    prior(exponential(1),  class = "sigma")
  ),
  chains = 4,
  iter  = 2000,
  warmup = 1000,
  seed  = 2026
)

title: Week 9 Lab: Multivariate Regression and Interactions
section: Dataset 2: Unemployment × Government Ideology → Transfer Spending
text:
Dataset 2: Unemployment × Government Ideology → Transfer Spending
The research question is: Does the effect of unemployment on transfer spending depend on government ideology?
The partisan theory of the welfare state predicts that left-wing governments respond more aggressively to rising unemployment by raising social transfers. Right-wing governments are more fiscally restrained. This is a claim about effect heterogeneity — the slope of the unemployment–spending relationship is steeper under left governments.
The true DGP is:

unemployment ~ Normal(7.5, 2.5) — unemployment rate (%)
left_gov ~ Bernoulli(0.5) — 0 = right government, 1 = left government
transfer_spend = 10 + 0.3 × unemployment + 2 × left_gov

0.9 × (unemployment × left_gov) + noise — transfer spending (% GDP)


Key quantities to recover:



Government
Intercept
Unemployment slope




Right (left_gov = 0)
10
0.3


Left (left_gov = 1)
12
0.3 + 0.9 = 1.2



The interaction coefficient β₃ = 0.9 captures how much steeper the left slope is relative to the right slope.


Code
set.seed(42)
n2 <- 150  # 150 synthetic government-years

unemployment  <- rnorm(n2, mean = 7.5, sd = 2.5)
left_gov      <- rbinom(n2, 1, prob = 0.5)  # coin flip: equal left/right share
transfer_spend <- 10 +
  0.3 * unemployment +
  2   * left_gov +
  0.9 * unemployment * left_gov +   # the interaction: slope shifts up for left govts
  rnorm(n2, 0, 2.5)

df_interact <- tibble(unemployment, left_gov, transfer_spend) |>
  mutate(government = if_else(left_gov == 1, "Left", "Right"))

title: Week 9 Lab: Multivariate Regression and Interactions
text:
Step 1: Estimand
Before writing any code, we state precisely what we are trying to estimate. Drawing a DAG before analysis keeps this honest: the estimand follows from the causal graph, not from what the model happens to produce.
Our DAG (presented in the lecture) shows GDP per capita as a confounder — it has causal arrows into both women’s parliamentary representation and health spending. A confounder opens a “back-door path” that conflates the association we care about with the spurious GDP-driven correlation. Conditioning on GDP per capita — including it as a predictor — closes that path.
Estimand: the partial association between women’s parliamentary representation and public health expenditure, adjusting for GDP per capita.
Under the assumption that GDP is the only relevant confounder (the adjustment set is sufficient), this partial association can be interpreted causally. That assumption is substantive, not statistical — the data cannot verify it.

title: Week 9 Lab: Multivariate Regression and Interactions
section: Step 2: Data Summary and OVB Visualisation
text:
Step 2: Data Summary and OVB Visualisation
The left panel below shows the raw bivariate scatter: there is a positive trend, but it conflates the legislative effect with the confounding from GDP.
The right panel shows the OVB in action. We plot the posterior mean and 95% credible interval for the women_parl coefficient from both pre-fit models. The bivariate estimate (orange) sits well above the true DGP value of 0.12. The multivariate estimate (purple), which controls for GDP, recovers the true value closely. The gap between the two posterior means is the omitted variable bias.


Code
p_raw <- df_controls |>
  ggplot(aes(x = women_parl, y = health_spend)) +
  geom_point(colour = col_1, alpha = 0.55, size = 2) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) +
  labs(
    x = "Women in Parliament (%)",
    y = "Health Expenditure (% GDP)",
    title = "Raw Bivariate Association"
  )

# Extract posterior mean and 95% CI for the women_parl coefficient from both models
post_biv   <- posterior_summary(fit_controls_biv)["b_women_parl", ]
post_multi <- posterior_summary(fit_controls)["b_women_parl", ]

coef_df <- tibble(
  Model = factor(
    c("Bivariate\n(no control)", "Multivariate\n(+ GDP per capita)"),
    levels = c("Bivariate\n(no control)", "Multivariate\n(+ GDP per capita)")
  ),
  Estimate = c(post_biv["Estimate"], post_multi["Estimate"]),
  lo = c(post_biv["Q2.5"],    post_multi["Q2.5"]),
  hi = c(post_biv["Q97.5"],   post_multi["Q97.5"])
)

p_coef <- coef_df |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Model, y = Estimate)) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = 0.12, colour = "gray50", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.9
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lo, ymax = hi, colour = Model), width = 0.12, linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(aes(colour = Model), size = 5) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(col_2, col_3), guide = "none") +
  annotate(
    "text", x = 1.52, y = 0.127, size = 3.3, colour = "gray40",
    label = expression(paste("True ", beta, " = 0.12")), hjust = 0
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = expression(hat(beta) ~ "(women in parliament -> health spending)"),
    title = "OVB: Bivariate Inflates the Estimate",
    subtitle = "Controlling for GDP brings posterior mean near true DGP value"
  )

p_raw | p_coef








Figure 1: Left: raw bivariate scatter. Right: OVB visualised — the bivariate estimate (orange) over-states the association; controlling for GDP (purple) recovers the true β = 0.12 (dashed line).

title: Week 9 Lab: Multivariate Regression and Interactions
text:
Step 3: Formal Model Specification
We write out the full statistical model — likelihood and priors — before touching brms. This makes our assumptions explicit and contestable.
Likelihood (unchanged from Week 8 — still Gaussian):
\[
Y_i \sim \text{Normal}(\mu_i,\, \sigma)
\]
\[
\mu_i = \alpha + \beta_X X_i + \beta_Z Z_i
\]
where \(X\) = women in parliament (%), \(Z\) = GDP per capita (thousands USD, PPP).
Priors (one per unknown parameter — all four must be justified):
\[
\alpha \sim \text{Normal}(10, 5) \qquad \beta_X \sim \text{Normal}(0, 0.5)
\]
\[
\beta_Z \sim \text{Normal}(0, 1) \qquad \sigma \sim \text{Exponential}(1)
\]
The prior on \(\beta_X\) says: before seeing data, a 1 pp increase in women’s representation is associated with a change of somewhere between −1.5 and +1.5 pp in health spending (the Normal(0, 0.5) range at ±3σ is [−1.5, 1.5]). This rules out extreme effects while remaining consistent with the empirical literature.

title: Week 9 Lab: Multivariate Regression and Interactions
text:
Step 4: Prior Predictive Check
A prior predictive check asks: if we sample parameter values from the priors and generate data, do the simulated outcomes look like real-world health spending? Public health expenditure as a share of GDP is typically between 0% and 30%. Prior-predicted values far outside this range would signal that our priors are miscalibrated.


Code
set.seed(2026)
n_pp <- 2000

# For each of the 2000 draws, compute the linear predictor at every observation
# and add Gaussian noise with the drawn σ.
# rowwise() + mutate(y_sim = list(...)) creates one simulated dataset per draw.
prior_pred_controls <- tibble(
  b0    = rnorm(n_pp, 10, 5),   # intercept draws
  b_wp  = rnorm(n_pp, 0, 0.5),  # women_parl slope draws
  b_gdp = rnorm(n_pp, 0, 1),    # gdp_pc slope draws
  sigma = rexp(n_pp, 1)          # residual SD draws
) |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(
    y_sim = list(
      b0 +
        b_wp  * df_controls$women_parl +
        b_gdp * df_controls$gdp_pc +
        rnorm(nrow(df_controls), 0, sigma)
    )
  ) |>
  unnest(y_sim) |>
  ungroup()

prior_pred_controls |>
  ggplot(aes(x = y_sim)) +
  annotate(
    "rect", xmin = 0, xmax = 30, ymin = 0, ymax = Inf,
    fill = col_4, alpha = 0.12
  ) +
  geom_density(fill = col_1, alpha = 0.55, color = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = c(0, 30), color = col_4, linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.9
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text", x = 15, y = Inf, vjust = 1.4, size = 3.2, color = col_4,
    label = "Plausible empirical range: 0–30% GDP"
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) +
  labs(
    x = "Predicted Health Expenditure (% GDP)",
    y = "Prior Predictive Density",
    title = "Prior Predictive Check: Health Spending Model",
    subtitle = "Before seeing data — does the prior generate plausible outcomes?"
  )








Figure 2: Prior predictive distribution: 2,000 parameter draws from the joint prior, each used to generate predicted health spending for all 120 observations. The shaded band marks the plausible 0–30% GDP range. Most prior mass falls inside — these priors are defensible.

title: Week 9 Lab: Multivariate Regression and Interactions
section: Step 5: Fit the Model with brms
text:
Step 5: Fit the Model with brms
The brm() call mirrors the formal model written in Step 3. Note that we specify a separate prior() for each coefficient using coef =. This is more precise than applying one flat prior to all slopes, and it documents our assumptions in the code itself.
brms translates the model to Stan (C++), compiles it, and samples 4 parallel Markov chains. With iter = 2000 and warmup = 1000, each chain contributes 1,000 post-warmup draws, giving 4,000 total posterior samples of (α, β_X, β_Z, σ).

# This chunk shows the fitting code for reference.
# The model was already fitted above (fit-controls-multi) and is available as fit_controls.
fit_controls <- brm(
  health_spend ~ women_parl + gdp_pc,   # outcome ~ main predictor + control
  data = df_controls,
  prior = c(
    prior(normal(10, 5),  class = "Intercept"),                   # baseline spending ~10% GDP
    prior(normal(0, 0.5), class = "b", coef = "women_parl"),      # weak: ±1.5pp per %
    prior(normal(0, 1),   class = "b", coef = "gdp_pc"),          # ±3pp per $1k GDP
    prior(exponential(1), class = "sigma")                        # residual SD < 5 almost certainly
  ),
  chains = 4,
  iter   = 2000,    # 1000 warmup + 1000 sampling draws per chain
  warmup = 1000,
  seed   = 2026     # reproducibility
)

title: Week 9 Lab: Multivariate Regression and Interactions
text:
Step 6a: MCMC Diagnostics — Traceplots
Before interpreting any results, we must confirm that MCMC has converged. Three things to check:

Rhat < 1.01 for every parameter: the chains have mixed — all four explored the same region of parameter space.
Bulk_ESS and Tail_ESS > 1,000 for every parameter: we have enough effective samples to trust posterior summaries and credible intervals.
Traceplots look like “hairy caterpillars”: each chain wanders randomly around the same range with no trends, drifts, or stuck periods.

If any diagnostic fails, stop and investigate before interpreting results.


Code
mcmc_trace(
  fit_controls,
  pars = c("b_Intercept", "b_women_parl", "b_gdp_pc", "sigma"),
  facet_args = list(ncol = 2)
) +
  scale_color_manual(values = chain_cols) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "gray90")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "MCMC Traceplots: Health Spending Model",
    subtitle = "All chains must mix well before interpreting results"
  )








Figure 3: Traceplots for the four model parameters. Each colour is one chain. The ‘hairy caterpillar’ pattern — chains overlapping and mixing rapidly — confirms convergence.

title: Week 9 Lab: Multivariate Regression and Interactions
text:
Step 6b: Posterior Predictive Check
A posterior predictive check (PPC) asks: do the model’s predictions look like the observed data? pp_check() draws 50 samples from the posterior predictive distribution (light blue lines) and overlays them on the observed outcome distribution (dark blue line). A well-specified model should produce simulated outcome distributions that closely envelope the observed one.


Code
pp_check(fit_controls, ndraws = 50) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) +
  labs(
    title = "Posterior Predictive Check: Health Spending Model",
    subtitle = "Light blue: model predictions  |  Dark: observed data"
  )








Figure 4: Posterior predictive check for the health spending model. The dark line is the observed distribution; light blue lines are 50 draws from the posterior predictive. The Gaussian model fits well.

title: Week 9 Lab: Multivariate Regression and Interactions
text:
Step 7: Interpret Results
summary() returns posterior means (Estimate), posterior standard deviations (Est.Error), and 95% credible intervals (l-95% CI / u-95% CI) for each parameter. The Rhat column confirms convergence (should be < 1.01 everywhere) and Bulk_ESS / Tail_ESS confirm adequate effective sample sizes.


Code
summary(fit_controls)


 Family: gaussian 
  Links: mu = identity 
Formula: health_spend ~ women_parl + gdp_pc 
   Data: df_controls (Number of observations: 120) 
  Draws: 4 chains, each with iter = 2000; warmup = 1000; thin = 1;
         total post-warmup draws = 4000

Regression Coefficients:
           Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS
Intercept      1.49      0.53     0.43     2.51 1.00     3319     3326
women_parl     0.15      0.03     0.10     0.21 1.00     1830     1997
gdp_pc         0.18      0.03     0.12     0.24 1.00     1800     1888

Further Distributional Parameters:
      Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS
sigma     1.36      0.09     1.20     1.55 1.00     3553     2643

Draws were sampled using sampling(NUTS). For each parameter, Bulk_ESS
and Tail_ESS are effective sample size measures, and Rhat is the potential
scale reduction factor on split chains (at convergence, Rhat = 1).


What to look for:

b_women_parl posterior mean should be near 0.12 (the true DGP value). Compare this to the bivariate estimate (~0.25) — the difference is OVB.
b_gdp_pc posterior mean should be near 0.20.
sigma should be near 1.5.


Visualising the Partial Effect
Rather than just reporting the coefficient, we visualise the estimated relationship. We create a prediction grid that varies women_parl across its observed range while holding gdp_pc fixed at its sample mean — this isolates the partial effect of legislative representation.
posterior_epred() returns a matrix of posterior draws of \(E[Y \mid X, Z]\) for each grid row. Taking quantiles across draws gives us the posterior mean and 95% credible interval for the partial effect at each value of women_parl.


Code
# Grid: vary women_parl, hold gdp_pc fixed at mean
grid_controls <- tibble(
  women_parl = seq(min(df_controls$women_parl), max(df_controls$women_parl), length.out = 80),
  gdp_pc     = mean(df_controls$gdp_pc)
)

# posterior_epred returns a draws × grid matrix of E[Y | X, Z] values
epred_mat <- posterior_epred(fit_controls, newdata = grid_controls)

grid_controls <- grid_controls |>
  mutate(
    lo  = apply(epred_mat, 2, quantile, 0.025),  # 2.5th percentile across draws
    mid = apply(epred_mat, 2, median),            # median across draws
    hi  = apply(epred_mat, 2, quantile, 0.975)   # 97.5th percentile across draws
  )

df_controls |>
  ggplot(aes(x = women_parl, y = health_spend)) +
  geom_ribbon(
    data = grid_controls,
    aes(x = women_parl, ymin = lo, ymax = hi, y = mid),
    inherit.aes = FALSE, fill = col_1, alpha = 0.25
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = grid_controls, aes(x = women_parl, y = mid),
    inherit.aes = FALSE, color = col_2, linewidth = 1.2
  ) +
  geom_point(color = col_1, alpha = 0.45, size = 1.8) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) +
  labs(
    x = "Women in Parliament (%)",
    y = "Health Expenditure (% GDP)",
    title = "Partial Effect: Women in Parliament -> Health Spending",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "GDP per capita held at mean (", round(mean(df_controls$gdp_pc), 1),
      " thousand USD)  |  posterior_epred()"
    )
  )








Figure 5: Partial effect of women’s parliamentary share on health spending, holding GDP per capita at its sample mean. The ribbon is the 95% posterior credible interval.

title: Week 9 Lab: Multivariate Regression and Interactions
text:
Step 8: LOO-IC Model Comparison
Bayesian leave-one-out cross-validation (LOO-IC) estimates how well each model would predict new data — it penalises complexity implicitly. We compare the bivariate model (no GDP control) against the multivariate model.
add_criterion() appends the LOO estimate to each fitted model. loo_compare() ranks models by expected log predictive density (elpd): higher elpd = better predictive accuracy. When |elpd_diff| > 2 × se_diff, the evidence for the better model is substantial.


Code
fit_controls     <- add_criterion(fit_controls,     "loo")
fit_controls_biv <- add_criterion(fit_controls_biv, "loo")
loo_compare(fit_controls_biv, fit_controls)


                 elpd_diff se_diff
fit_controls       0.0       0.0  
fit_controls_biv -15.0       5.1  


The multivariate model should dominate: GDP genuinely predicts health spending, so including it improves out-of-sample accuracy.
Remaining limitations to keep in mind:

GDP may not be the only confounder — country institutional quality, colonial history, and cultural factors might also drive both representation and spending.
Country-year observations within the same country are likely correlated — a violation of the independence assumption (A5) that Week 12 addresses with multilevel models.
Linearity is assumed: we should inspect residuals for non-linear patterns.

title: Week 9 Lab: Multivariate Regression and Interactions
text:
Step 1: DAG and Estimand
The partisan welfare state story involves two independent causes of transfer spending — unemployment and government ideology — where ideology also moderates the unemployment effect. The DAG has arrows from both Unemployment and Government Ideology into Transfer Spending; the research question concerns the interaction: is the unemployment slope steeper under left governments?
Because this DAG has no confounders (no variable that causes both unemployment and ideology), we do not need a control variable to close a back-door path. The interaction term itself is the quantity of interest.
Estimand 1: \(\partial\mu/\partial X|_{Z=0}\) — unemployment slope under right governments.
Estimand 2: \(\partial\mu/\partial X|_{Z=1}\) — unemployment slope under left governments.
Estimand 3: β₃ — the slope difference; how much more does unemployment associate with spending under left vs. right governments?

title: Week 9 Lab: Multivariate Regression and Interactions
text:
Step 2: Data Summary
We start with a quick look at the outcome distribution by ideology group, then examine the joint scatter and a box-plot comparison.


Code
df_interact |>
  ggplot(aes(x = transfer_spend, fill = government, colour = government)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.45, linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values   = c("Left" = col_2, "Right" = col_1)) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Left" = col_2, "Right" = col_1)) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), legend.position = "bottom") +
  labs(
    x = "Transfer Spending (% GDP)", y = "Density",
    title = "Distribution of Transfer Spending by Government Ideology",
    subtitle = "Left governments show higher average spending",
    fill = NULL, colour = NULL
  )








Figure 6: Marginal distribution of transfer spending by government ideology. Left governments show higher average spending — the intercept shift captured by β₂ in the interaction model.





The scatter below puts unemployment on the x-axis and draws separate OLS lines for each ideology group. If the lines have visibly different slopes, that is preliminary evidence for an interaction. The box-plot facets unemployment into terciles and shows the widening gap between left and right governments as unemployment rises — the visual signature of β₃ > 0.


Code
p_scatter_int <- df_interact |>
  ggplot(aes(x = unemployment, y = transfer_spend, color = government)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1.8) +
  geom_smooth(aes(group = government), method = "lm", se = FALSE, linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Left" = col_2, "Right" = col_1), name = "Government") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), legend.position = "bottom") +
  labs(
    x = "Unemployment (%)", y = "Transfer Spending (% GDP)",
    title = "Separate Slopes by Ideology", subtitle = "OLS line per group"
  )

df_interact_plot <- df_interact |>
  mutate(
    unemp_tercile = cut(
      unemployment,
      breaks = quantile(unemployment, c(0, 1/3, 2/3, 1)),
      labels = c("Low\n(~4%)", "Mid\n(~8%)", "High\n(~12%)"),
      include.lowest = TRUE
    )
  )

p_box_int <- df_interact_plot |>
  ggplot(aes(x = unemp_tercile, y = transfer_spend, fill = government)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, position = position_dodge(width = 0.75), width = 0.6) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Left" = col_2, "Right" = col_1), name = "Government") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), legend.position = "bottom") +
  labs(
    x = "Unemployment Tercile", y = "Transfer Spending (% GDP)",
    title = "Gap Widens with Unemployment", subtitle = "Signature of a positive interaction term"
  )

p_scatter_int | p_box_int








Figure 7: Left: scatter with separate OLS lines per ideology group — slopes look clearly different. Right: transfer spending by unemployment tercile and ideology — the gap widens with unemployment, the visual signature of a positive interaction.

title: Week 9 Lab: Multivariate Regression and Interactions
text:
Step 3: Formal Model Specification
The interaction model augments the additive model with a product term:
\[
Y_i \sim \text{Normal}(\mu_i,\, \sigma)
\]
\[
\mu_i = \alpha + \beta_1 U_i + \beta_2 G_i + \beta_3 (U_i \cdot G_i)
\]
where \(U\) = unemployment (%), \(G\) = left government (0/1).
The two conditional marginal effects are now functions of the moderator:
\[
\frac{\partial\mu}{\partial U} = \beta_1 + \beta_3 G \qquad
\frac{\partial\mu}{\partial G} = \beta_2 + \beta_3 U
\]
Priors:
\[
\alpha \sim \text{Normal}(12, 5) \qquad
\beta_1, \beta_2 \sim \text{Normal}(0, 1) \qquad
\beta_3 \sim \text{Normal}(0, 1) \qquad
\sigma \sim \text{Exponential}(0.5)
\]
The prior for β₃ is centred at zero — our prior expectation is no moderation. The data will update this. This is appropriately humble: we should not encode strong prior beliefs about the direction of an interaction before seeing the evidence.
We fit two competing models:

M1 (no interaction): assumes the unemployment slope is the same for all governments — a testable restriction.
M2 (interaction): relaxes this. If M2 fits substantially better via LOO-IC, we have evidence that the restriction is false.

title: Week 9 Lab: Multivariate Regression and Interactions
text:
Step 4: Prior Predictive Check
For the interaction model, we draw regression lines from the joint prior — one bundle per ideology group. The check asks: does the prior allow a wide enough range of slopes, including no moderation (similar slopes), strong moderation (very different slopes), and implausible configurations (e.g., enormous negative slopes)?


Code
set.seed(42)
n_pp2    <- 150
unemp_seq <- seq(1.5, 14, length.out = 60)

prior_lines_int <- tibble(
  sim   = 1:n_pp2,
  b0    = rnorm(n_pp2, 12, 5),  # intercept draws
  b_u   = rnorm(n_pp2, 0,  1),  # unemployment slope draws
  b_g   = rnorm(n_pp2, 0,  1),  # ideology main effect draws
  b_int = rnorm(n_pp2, 0,  1)   # interaction term draws
) |>
  expand_grid(unemployment = unemp_seq, left_gov = c(0L, 1L)) |>
  mutate(
    y_hat = b0 + b_u * unemployment + b_g * left_gov +
      b_int * unemployment * left_gov,  # slope shifts by b_int for left govts
    government = if_else(left_gov == 1, "Left", "Right")
  )

prior_lines_int |>
  ggplot(aes(
    x = unemployment, y = y_hat,
    group = interaction(sim, government), color = government
  )) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.12, linewidth = 0.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Left" = col_2, "Right" = col_1), name = "Government") +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-10, 45)) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), legend.position = "bottom") +
  labs(
    x = "Unemployment (%)", y = "Predicted Transfer Spending (% GDP)",
    title = "Prior Predictive Spaghetti: Interaction Model",
    subtitle = "150 lines per group — prior is non-dogmatic about moderation"
  )








Figure 8: Prior predictive spaghetti: 150 regression lines drawn from the joint prior, separately for left (orange) and right (blue) governments. The prior is wide but not implausible — it allows no interaction, moderate interaction, and strong interaction.

title: Week 9 Lab: Multivariate Regression and Interactions
text:
Step 5: Fit Both Models
We use the * operator in the brm() formula. unemployment * left_gov is shorthand for unemployment + left_gov + unemployment:left_gov. Always use *, not just the colon form (:). The colon-only form omits the main effects, which forces β₁ = 0 and β₂ = 0 — usually a false and arbitrary constraint.


Code
# M1: no interaction — assumes unemployment slope is identical for both ideology groups
fit_m1 <- brm(
  transfer_spend ~ unemployment + left_gov,  # additive model: same slope for all
  data = df_interact,
  prior = c(
    prior(normal(12, 5),      class = "Intercept"),  # baseline spending ~12% GDP
    prior(normal(0, 1),       class = "b"),           # weak priors on both slopes
    prior(exponential(0.5),   class = "sigma")        # residual SD likely < 5
  ),
  chains = 4,
  iter   = 2000,
  warmup = 1000,
  seed   = 42
)




Code
# M2: interaction model — allows the unemployment slope to differ by ideology
# * expands to: unemployment + left_gov + unemployment:left_gov
fit_m2 <- brm(
  transfer_spend ~ unemployment * left_gov,
  data = df_interact,
  prior = c(
    prior(normal(12, 5),      class = "Intercept"),  # baseline spending ~12% GDP
    prior(normal(0, 1),       class = "b"),           # prior centred at zero: no moderation by default
    prior(exponential(0.5),   class = "sigma")        # residual SD likely < 5
  ),
  chains = 4,
  iter   = 2000,
  warmup = 1000,
  seed   = 42
)

title: Week 9 Lab: Multivariate Regression and Interactions
section: Step 6: Diagnostics and Posterior Predictive Check
text:
Step 6: Diagnostics and Posterior Predictive Check
We run the same convergence checks as before. The traceplots and PPC are shown side-by-side for the interaction model (M2).


Code
p_trace_m2 <- mcmc_trace(
  fit_m2,
  pars = c("b_Intercept", "b_unemployment", "b_left_gov", "b_unemployment:left_gov"),
  facet_args = list(ncol = 2)
) +
  scale_color_manual(values = chain_cols) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "gray90"),
    legend.position = "none"
  ) +
  labs(title = "Traceplots: Interaction Model (M2)")

p_ppc_m2 <- pp_check(fit_m2, ndraws = 50) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) +
  labs(title = "Posterior Predictive Check (M2)")

p_trace_m2 | p_ppc_m2








Figure 9: Left: traceplots for the four parameters of M2 — all chains mix well. Right: posterior predictive check — model predictions envelope the observed distribution.

title: Week 9 Lab: Multivariate Regression and Interactions
section: Step 7: Interpreting Results and Posterior Marginal Effects
text:
Step 7: Interpreting Results and Posterior Marginal Effects
Inspect the summary output. The key quantity is b_unemployment:left_gov — the interaction coefficient β₃. Its posterior mean should be near 0.9 (the true DGP value).


Code
summary(fit_m2)


 Family: gaussian 
  Links: mu = identity 
Formula: transfer_spend ~ unemployment * left_gov 
   Data: df_interact (Number of observations: 150) 
  Draws: 4 chains, each with iter = 2000; warmup = 1000; thin = 1;
         total post-warmup draws = 4000

Regression Coefficients:
                      Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS
Intercept                11.20      0.70     9.88    12.55 1.00     2439
unemployment              0.17      0.09    -0.01     0.34 1.00     2386
left_gov                  1.05      0.77    -0.46     2.58 1.00     1769
unemployment:left_gov     0.95      0.10     0.75     1.15 1.00     1902
                      Tail_ESS
Intercept                 2839
unemployment              2630
left_gov                  2585
unemployment:left_gov     2374

Further Distributional Parameters:
      Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS
sigma     2.36      0.14     2.10     2.65 1.00     2543     2631

Draws were sampled using sampling(NUTS). For each parameter, Bulk_ESS
and Tail_ESS are effective sample size measures, and Rhat is the potential
scale reduction factor on split chains (at convergence, Rhat = 1).



Posterior Line Clouds
A powerful way to visualise an interaction model is to plot many regression lines directly from the MCMC draws. as_draws_df() returns a data frame with one row per MCMC draw and one column per parameter. For each draw, we compute the intercept and slope for each ideology group by simple arithmetic and draw the resulting line.
The bundle of blue lines (right governments) should be clearly less steep than the bundle of orange lines (left governments).


Code
# Sample 200 draws for visual clarity (4000 lines would be too dense)
post_draws_m2 <- as_draws_df(fit_m2) |> slice_sample(n = 200)

df_interact |>
  ggplot(aes(x = unemployment, y = transfer_spend, color = government)) +
  # Right government lines: intercept = b_Intercept, slope = b_unemployment
  geom_abline(
    data = post_draws_m2,
    aes(intercept = b_Intercept, slope = b_unemployment),
    color = col_1, alpha = 0.05, linewidth = 0.4
  ) +
  # Left government lines: intercept = b_Intercept + b_left_gov,
  #                          slope   = b_unemployment + b_unemployment:left_gov
  geom_abline(
    data = post_draws_m2,
    aes(
      intercept = b_Intercept + b_left_gov,
      slope     = b_unemployment + `b_unemployment:left_gov`
    ),
    color = col_2, alpha = 0.05, linewidth = 0.4
  ) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1.8) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Left" = col_2, "Right" = col_1), name = "Government") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), legend.position = "bottom") +
  labs(
    x = "Unemployment (%)", y = "Transfer Spending (% GDP)",
    title = "Posterior Line Clouds by Government Ideology",
    subtitle = "200 MCMC draws per group — orange bundle (left) is steeper"
  )








Figure 10: Posterior regression line clouds: 200 MCMC draws per ideology group. The orange bundle (left governments) is visibly steeper — posterior evidence for β₃ > 0.







Posterior Conditional Marginal Effects
The conditional marginal effect of unemployment for a given ideology group is a function of β₁ and β₃:
\[
\text{ME}_{\text{right}} = \beta_1 \qquad
\text{ME}_{\text{left}}  = \beta_1 + \beta_3
\]
Because we have full posterior draws of both β₁ and β₃, we compute these by arithmetic — no approximation or delta method needed. The resulting collections of values are the posterior distributions of the conditional marginal effects.


Code
post_all_m2 <- as_draws_df(fit_m2)

me_df <- post_all_m2 |>
  transmute(
    `Right Government` = b_unemployment,                               # ME at G = 0
    `Left Government`  = b_unemployment + `b_unemployment:left_gov`   # ME at G = 1
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "government", values_to = "marginal_effect") |>
  mutate(
    government = factor(government, levels = c("Right Government", "Left Government"))
  )

# Compute P(β₃ > 0 | data) directly from posterior draws — a direct probability statement
prob_b3_pos <- mean(post_all_m2$`b_unemployment:left_gov` > 0)

me_df |>
  ggplot(aes(x = marginal_effect, fill = government, color = government)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.55, linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, color = "gray40", linetype = "dotted", linewidth = 0.9) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Right Government" = col_1, "Left Government" = col_2), name = NULL
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("Right Government" = col_1, "Left Government" = col_2), name = NULL
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text", x = 1.7, y = 1.2, size = 3.5, color = col_3,
    label = paste0("P(beta3 > 0 | data) = ", round(prob_b3_pos, 3))
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), legend.position = "bottom") +
  labs(
    x = expression(partialdiff * mu / partialdiff ~ Unemployment),
    y = "Posterior Density",
    title = "Posterior Marginal Effects: Unemployment -> Transfer Spending",
    subtitle = "Left slope ~1.2  |  Right slope ~0.3  |  Exact by arithmetic"
  )








Figure 11: Posterior distributions of the conditional marginal effect of unemployment on transfer spending by ideology group. The left slope (~1.2) and right slope (~0.3) are well separated — strong posterior evidence for moderation.





Reading the figure: the two density curves should be well separated. P(β₃ > 0 | data) near 1.0 means we are nearly certain the interaction is positive — left governments show a steeper unemployment–spending slope. This is the Bayesian analogue of a one-sided test, but it is a direct probability statement, not a p-value.

title: Week 9 Lab: Multivariate Regression and Interactions
text:
Step 8: LOO-IC Model Comparison
We compare M1 (no interaction) against M2 (interaction) using LOO-IC. Because we generated the data with an interaction, M2 should dominate M1.


Code
fit_m1 <- add_criterion(fit_m1, "loo")
fit_m2 <- add_criterion(fit_m2, "loo")
loo_compare(fit_m1, fit_m2)


       elpd_diff se_diff
fit_m2   0.0       0.0  
fit_m1 -16.2       6.7  


Reading the output:

The model listed first has the highest elpd — it is the better predictive model.
elpd_diff = 0 for the winner; all other models show a negative difference (i.e., they predict worse).
When |elpd_diff| > 2 × se_diff, the evidence strongly favours the winner.

Remaining limitations:

The linear interaction assumes the slope difference is constant across all unemployment levels — check with a LOESS diagnostic when Z is continuous.
Government-years within the same country are correlated (A5) — a multilevel structure (Week 12) would handle this.
Omitted confounders (economic openness, union density) may bias the ideology coefficient.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Improve this prompt for claude code:
” Draft a plan to implement the following changes in the week 9 slide deck. Add your plan in (week9.md?) the section week9.qmd changes plan in line 71.
Although overall I am very satisfied with your work, I want some fundamental changes:
I really want to have 4 blocks, 1 conceptual foundation and 1 application block, once for Control variables multivariate regression, once for interaction multivariate regression.
Part I should cover all conceptual (and formalized) foundations and concepts that are new this week and necessary for students to understand what is happening. That is: DAG (as an analytical tool linking theory and empirical analysis), types of controls (based on DAGs), binary/categorical controls as shifting the intercept(s), continuous control variables to isolate association of variable of interest (avg. change holding others constant rationale), important takeaway: more control variables not always better, differing types of control variables, appropriateness depends on estimand and research context
Part II then should demonstrate how this looks in action: now the substantive example (simulated) data comes in, a hypothetical research question, the workflow steps in action. Importantly, in this section the subsections should mirror the analysis workflow. My plan is to switch to the Editor in class and have them open the source too, so I run the code live and students can follow it. So the source code needs to be clean, commented and readable.
Part III should cover all conceptual (and formalized) foundations and concepts that are new this week and necessary for students to understand what is happening. These are: What if the effect we are interested in (X on Y) depends on a third variable; Dummy/Categorical variables as drawing two/multiple lines with different slopes.
Part IV should then reiterate the practical application with an example for conditional hypothesis, like for Part II I plan to switch to showing students the source code so they can run the code too. Here it is important to include in the the subsection on data distibution exploration the distribution of the outcome for all categories or across the range of continuous interaction variables
Other things I would like you to change:

NO Causal framing throughout, I do not causal inference in class, just speak of associations among variables (occasionally one can mention that under these assumption the association can be interpreted as causal effect, but I want to teach them to speak of associations and relationships among variables, not causal effects)
Do NOT use raw unicode codes like 3b2, rather use either latex expressions or some R / ggplot way to include beta symbols in figures for instance
Do NOT do Bayesian / Frequentist comparisons, this is just confusing. We just focus on Bayes. as OLS for linear estimators is effectively the likelihood, all the “fitting a line” rhetoric still holds, only in the end we simulate 1000s of plausible lines and rank them according to their relative probability given the data.

”
Return the improved prompt as escaped source markdown

About you

What name would you like us to call you?
What natural (spoken) languages do you speak or are currently learning? (e.g., German, Spanish, Arabic – not programming languages)
How do you usually spend your time outside university? (This can be anything: a side job, vocational/civilian service, volunteering, sports, creative hobbies, gaming, family time – whatever is part of your life)

title: QMIR 2026
text: About you continued

title: QMIR 2026
text:
What you will learn

How to build a reproducible, open-source research workflow – integrating data, code, analysis, and writing into a transparent scientific process
How to use statistical models to learn from data – understanding what models assume, what they estimate, how they update our beliefs, and how to interpret results responsibly
How to communicate empirical results clearly and responsibly – through well-structured reports, visualizations, and reproducible documents

title: QMIR 2026
section: What this course does not cover
text:
What this course does not cover

Data collection and research design – we work with existing datasets and do not cover survey design, measurement strategies, or fieldwork (the university offers dedicated courses and resources in these areas)
Causal inference methods – we focus on analyzing and interpreting associations between variables rather than formal causal identification (specialized courses cover causal inference in depth)
Substantive political theory or normative argumentation – we use real political science examples, but the focus is methodological and conceptual training rather than developing theoretical frameworks

Why the course is structured this way

An (toy) example of a fully integrated, reproducible research pipeline in action: Quarto manuscript
We will build up to this step by step, learning and integrating each piece along the way

title: QMIR 2026
section: Why the course is structured this way
text:
Why the course is structured this way

Admittedly overkill for simple tasks, but it provides a scalable, FOSS toolbox for complex projects, advanced models, and fully reproducible workflows

Builds a foundation in quantitative methods and data science that’s useful across disciplines and careers, helping you understand research from other fields while gaining essential data skills

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Week 3 Goals
By the end of today, you should:

Understand the core concepts: objects, functions, and pipes
Know the main data types R uses to store information
Know the main data structures R uses to organize information
Be able to access and explore data using [] and $

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Agenda

Leftovers from Last Week
Objects, Functions & Pipes
Data Types
Data Structures
Accessing & Exploring Data
Walkthrough & Exercises

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Leftovers from Last Week

Cloning from GitHub

This is how you access and submit the homeworks and take home exam

Creating and linking your private projects with GitHub
Habits for effective and efficient coding:

learn to use shortcuts (e.g. opening and closing Positron panels)
learn to use your keyboard (e.g. highlighting text, adding code cells)
set theme and font that makes you feel good



Budget ~10 minutes

title: QMIR 2026
section: R as a Language for Storing and Transforming Information
text:
R as a Language for Storing and Transforming Information

To understand […] R, two slogans are helpful:
Everything that exists is an object
Everything that happens is a function call
– John Chambers


You give R objects – named containers holding information
You apply functions – operations that transform objects
You chain steps together using pipes


This quote is a good anchor. The point is that R has a very consistent grammar once you understand these three ideas.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Objects – Storing Information


An object has:

a name (how you refer to it)
a value (the information stored)
a type (what kind of information)


An R example:


View source
# Assign a value to a name with <-
country <- "Germany"

gdp_per_capita <- 54300

is_eu_member <- TRUE




The arrow <- is read as “gets”. country gets the value “Germany”. Emphasize that nothing is printed when you assign — you have to call the name to see it.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Objects – Storing Information


View source
# print by typing the name
country


[1] "Germany"


View source
gdp_per_capita


[1] 54300


View source
is_eu_member


[1] TRUE

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Functions – Doing Things with Objects


A function:

takes one or more inputs (arguments)
does something
returns an output

function_name(argument1, argument2, ...)




View source
gdp_values <- c(54300, 43800, 33200)

# Functions transform objects
mean(gdp_values)


[1] 43766.67


View source
sqrt(gdp_per_capita)


[1] 233.0236


View source
nchar(country)


[1] 7

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Functions – Doing Things with Objects


You can write your own functions.
Functions can be anything from simple one-liners to complex software components. Among other things, they can be:

small personal helpers

reusable analysis tools
shared with others

Collections of useful, published functions form packages. We will expore some next week.
Packages are what make R – like Python and many other programming languages – a powerful ecosystem.




View source
# A small custom helper
add_tax <- function(price) {
  price * 1.19
}

add_tax(10)


[1] 11.9

title: QMIR 2026
text: Recap

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Getting help
To open the documentation page of a function,
help(function)
?function()
for example:


View source
help(mean)
?mean()

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Control Structures in R








Structure
Syntax Example
Description




if
if (x > 0) { ... }
Executes code if condition is TRUE


if-else
if (x > 0) { ... } else { ... }
Chooses between two branches


ifelse
ifelse(x > 0, "pos", "neg")
Vectorized conditional


for
for (i in 1:5) { print(i) }
Loop over a sequence


while
while (x < 10) { x <- x + 1 }
Repeat while condition TRUE


repeat
repeat { ...; if (x>10) break }
Infinite loop until break


break
break
Exit a loop immediately


next
next
Skip current iteration in a loop

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Pipe – Chaining Operations
Without the pipe, nested calls become hard to read:


View source
round(mean(sqrt(gdp_values)), digits = 2)


[1] 208.17


With the pipe |>, read left to right – “and then”:


View source
gdp_values |> sqrt() |> mean() |> round(digits = 2)


[1] 208.17




The pipe says: take this, and then do that.


The native pipe |> was introduced in R 4.1. Students may also encounter %>% from the magrittr package — functionally very similar. For this course, |> is preferred.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Comments – Code as Communication


View source
# BAD: comments that just repeat the code
x <- x + 1 # add 1 to x

# GOOD: comments that explain WHY
x <- x + 1 # index starts at 0, shift to 1-based for plotting

# Use comments to:
# - explain decisions ("why log-transform? right skew in raw data")
# - mark sections of your script
# - temporarily disable code during testing




Your most important collaborator is your future self — six months from now, you will not remember why you did what you did. Thanks to our Quarto literate programming workflow, you are living in the golden age of commenting – you can literally write full prose between code cells instead of apologizing in hashtags later.

title: QMIR 2026
section: What Kind of Information Does R Store?
text:
What Kind of Information Does R Store?



Type
Description
Example




double
Decimal numbers
3.14, 54300.5


integer
Whole numbers
42, 2026


character
Text / strings
"Germany", "hello"


logical
True or false
TRUE, FALSE


factor
Categories
"low", "medium", "high"


Date
Calendar dates
2026-02-23

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Numeric: Double & Integer


View source
gdp <- 54300.5 # double (default for numbers)
seats <- 736L # integer (the L suffix makes it explicit)

class(gdp)


[1] "numeric"


View source
class(seats)


[1] "integer"




For most purposes in this course, the integer/double distinction doesn’t matter – R handles it behind the scenes. But it’s good to know it exists.
It is important that you know how to find out if objects are numeric.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Character / String


View source
capital <- 'Berlin' # single or double quotes both work
country <- "Germany" # but double quotes are sometimes easier to see

nchar(country) # how many characters?


[1] 7


View source
toupper(country) # string manipulation


[1] "GERMANY"


View source
paste(country, "is in the EU")


[1] "Germany is in the EU"

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Character / String
Numbers stored as characters are not numbers – R won’t do math with them:


View source
"42" + 1 # this does not work


Error in `"42" + 1`:
! non-numeric argument to binary operator


View source
as.numeric("42") + 1 # transforming the string to numeric makes it work


[1] 43

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Logical / Boolean


View source
is_eu_member <- TRUE

is_eu_member


[1] TRUE


View source
population <- 84000000

# Comparisons produce logical values
population > 50000000


[1] TRUE


View source
population == 84000000


[1] TRUE


View source
!is_eu_member # NOT


[1] FALSE


Logicals are the backbone of filtering – you’ll use them constantly when subsetting data.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Logical Operators








Operator
Meaning
Example




<
Less than
3 < 5


<=
Less than or equal
3 <= 3


>
Greater than
5 > 3


>=
Greater than or equal
5 >= 5


==
Equal to
3 == 3


!=
Not equal to
3 != 5


&
AND (vectorised)
(3 > 1) & (5 > 2)


\|
OR (vectorised)
(3 > 5) \| (5 > 2)


!
NOT
!(3 > 5)


%in%
Is element contained in vector
"Germany" %in% c("France", "Germany")

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Specialized Types: Factors & Dates


Factors – categorical variables with fixed levels


View source
regime <- factor(
  c("democracy", "autocracy", "democracy", "hybrid"),
  levels = c("autocracy", "hybrid", "democracy")
)

regime


[1] democracy autocracy democracy hybrid   
Levels: autocracy hybrid democracy


View source
levels(regime)


[1] "autocracy" "hybrid"    "democracy"



Dates


View source
today <- Sys.Date()
today


[1] "2026-03-23"


View source
treaty <- as.Date("1957-03-25") # Treaty of Rome
treaty


[1] "1957-03-25"


View source
today - treaty # date arithmetic!


Time difference of 25200 days




You’ll encounter these often in real data. The key insight: R treats them differently from plain text so it can do useful things like ordering, arithmetic, and plotting.


Don’t go too deep here. Factors will come up naturally in visualization (Week 4). lubridate for dates can be mentioned as an optional depth topic.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
How Is Information Organized?










Structure
Dimensions
Types allowed
Formal notation
Other names (across fields)




Vector
1
One
\(\mathbf{x}_i\)
feature vector, embedding, signal, series


Matrix
2
One
\(\mathbf{X}_{i_1, i_2}\)
design matrix, adjacency matrix, image, tensor (order 2)


Data frame
2
Multiple

dataset, table, rectangular data, tibble


Array
N
One
\(\mathcal{X}_{i_1 i_2 \dots i_N}\)
tensor, cube, multidimensional array


List
Any
Multiple

container, object, record, nested data

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Vectors – The Building Block


View source
# c() combines values into a vector
countries <- c("Germany", "France", "Italy", "Spain", "Turkey")
population <- c(84, 68, 60, 47, 88) # millions
eu_member <- c(TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, FALSE)

length(countries) # number of elements in vector


[1] 5


View source
class(countries) # check type


[1] "character"


View source
class(eu_member) # check type


[1] "logical"

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Vectors – The Building Block
A vector can only hold one type. If you mix types, R silently converts:


View source
mixed <- c(1, 2, "three") # all become character!
mixed


[1] "1"     "2"     "three"

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Matrices & Arrays – Briefly


View source
matrix(1:6, nrow = 2, ncol = 3)


     [,1] [,2] [,3]
[1,]    1    3    5
[2,]    2    4    6


View source
matrix(c("A", "B", "C", "D"), nrow = 2, ncol = 2)


     [,1] [,2]
[1,] "A"  "C" 
[2,] "B"  "D" 



2D structures storing elements of the same type

Fundamental for linear algebra and numerical computations

Data frames are more flexible for tabular data, but matrices remain crucial in statistical modeling


Keep this brief — 2 minutes max. It’s here for conceptual completeness.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Data Frames – The Workhorse



Rectangular (rows × columns)
Each column is a vector (one type)
Columns can be different types from each other
Each row is an observation




View source
eu_data <- data.frame(
  country = c("Germany", "France", "Italy", "Spain", "Turkey"),
  population = c(84, 68, 60, 47, 88),
  eu_member = c(TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, FALSE),
  regime = c("democracy", "democracy", "democracy", "democracy", "hybrid")
)

eu_data


  country population eu_member    regime
1 Germany         84      TRUE democracy
2  France         68      TRUE democracy
3   Italy         60      TRUE democracy
4   Spain         47      TRUE democracy
5  Turkey         88     FALSE    hybrid

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Data Frames – The Workhorse




View source
nrow(eu_data) # number of rows (observations)


[1] 5


View source
ncol(eu_data) # number of columns (variables)


[1] 4


View source
head(eu_data) # first 5 rows of df


  country population eu_member    regime
1 Germany         84      TRUE democracy
2  France         68      TRUE democracy
3   Italy         60      TRUE democracy
4   Spain         47      TRUE democracy
5  Turkey         88     FALSE    hybrid





View source
str(eu_data)


'data.frame':   5 obs. of  4 variables:
 $ country   : chr  "Germany" "France" "Italy" "Spain" ...
 $ population: num  84 68 60 47 88
 $ eu_member : logi  TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE FALSE
 $ regime    : chr  "democracy" "democracy" "democracy" "democracy" ...


View source
summary(eu_data)


   country            population   eu_member          regime         
 Length:5           Min.   :47.0   Mode :logical   Length:5          
 Class :character   1st Qu.:60.0   FALSE:1         Class :character  
 Mode  :character   Median :68.0   TRUE :4         Mode  :character  
                    Mean   :69.4                                     
                    3rd Qu.:84.0                                     
                    Max.   :88.0

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Lists – The Flexible Container


View source
country_info <- list(
  name = "Germany",
  population = 84000000,
  eu_member = TRUE,
  parties = c("SPD", "CDU", "Greens", "FDP")
)

country_info


$name
[1] "Germany"

$population
[1] 8.4e+07

$eu_member
[1] TRUE

$parties
[1] "SPD"    "CDU"    "Greens" "FDP"   


Lists can hold anything – including other lists. They’re less intuitive than data frames, but you’ll encounter them constantly as outputs of statistical models.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Reaching Inside Structures
Two main tools:

[ ] – index by position or condition
$ – index by name (for data frames and lists)

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Indexing Vectors with [ ]




View source
countries <- c("Germany", "France", "Italy", "Spain", "Poland")

countries[1] # first element


[1] "Germany"


View source
countries[c(1, 3)] # first and third


[1] "Germany" "Italy"  


View source
countries[-2] # everything except second


[1] "Germany" "Italy"   "Spain"   "Poland" 





View source
population <- c(84, 68, 60, 47, 38)

# Logical indexing -- filter by condition
countries[population > 50]


[1] "Germany" "France"  "Italy"

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Indexing DFs with [ ] and $




View source
# [row, column] -- leave blank to mean "all"
eu_data[1, ] # first row, all columns


  country population eu_member    regime
1 Germany         84      TRUE democracy


View source
eu_data[, 2] # all rows, second column


[1] 84 68 60 47 88


View source
eu_data[1, 2] # specific cell


[1] 84




View source
# $ accesses a column by name -- returns a vector
eu_data$country


[1] "Germany" "France"  "Italy"   "Spain"   "Turkey" 


View source
eu_data$population


[1] 84 68 60 47 88





View source
# Combine: filter rows by condition
eu_data[eu_data$population > 65, ]


  country population eu_member    regime
1 Germany         84      TRUE democracy
2  France         68      TRUE democracy
5  Turkey         88     FALSE    hybrid

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Walkthrough Overview

Clone the Week 3 exercise repo from GitHub (same workflow as last week)
Open exercise_week03.qmd in Positron
Work through the tasks in order
Fast finishers: optional depth exercises at the bottom of the document


Live-code the clone step briefly to warm students up. Then let them work. Circulate and help. Reserve ~10 min at the end for questions before previewing homework.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Homework

Distributed via GitHub (same workflow as Week 2)
Submit a rendered Quarto document (.qmd + rendered PDF)
Due: one week from today

The homework follows the same arc as today – you’ll work with a small political dataset to practice types, structures, and subsetting. Read the task descriptions carefully and write comments explaining your reasoning.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
QMIR 2026
Week 5: Reshaping, Merging & Data Visualization
 Tristan Muno            March 9, 2026

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Week 5 Learning Goals
By the end of today, you should be able to:

Use key Positron shortcuts and look up function documentation efficiently
Reshape data between wide and long format using pivot_longer() and pivot_wider()
Load and clean messy real-world Excel files using readxl and janitor
Combine datasets from multiple sources using left_join() and verify the result
Handle missing values transparently and understand the risks of imputation
Build and customise ggplot2 visualisations using aesthetics, geoms, scales, and themes
Produce publication-ready tables with tabyl(), adorn_totals(), and kable()
Embed computed results directly in prose using inline code

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Positron Keyboard Shortcuts
Knowing these will make your workflow noticeably faster.









Action
Windows / Linux
Mac




Run current line / selection
Ctrl + Enter
Cmd + Enter


Run current code cell (Quarto)
Ctrl + Shift + Enter
Cmd + Shift + Enter


Comment / uncomment
Ctrl + /
Cmd + /


Duplicate line
Shift + Alt + Down
Shift + Option + Down


Move line up / down
Alt + Up / Down
Option + Up / Down


Select all occurrences
Ctrl + Shift + L
Cmd + Shift + L


Restart R session
Ctrl + Shift + 0
Cmd + Shift + 0


Insert Quarto code cell
Ctrl + Shift + I
Cmd + Shift + I










Note


All shortcuts are fully customizable via File → Preferences → Keyboard Shortcuts. If a shortcut here does not work for you, it may already be assigned to something else – check and remap as needed. Visit this page for RStudio shortcuts in Positron.









Tip


Restart R frequently. A fresh session catches hidden dependencies between code chunks that can break rendering.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The package::function Notation
You will encounter this pattern often in documentation and Stack Overflow answers:
dplyr::filter(gapminder, year == 2007)

This is equivalent to loading the package and then calling the function:
library(dplyr)
filter(gapminder, year == 2007)



When to use it:

When two packages export a function with the same name (e.g., dplyr::filter vs stats::filter)
In scripts you share with others, to make dependencies explicit
When you only need one function from a package and don’t want to load the whole thing










Note


If you load the tidyverse with library(tidyverse), you rarely need :: notation within that ecosystem. But you will encounter it – and write it – more as your code grows.

title: QMIR 2026
section: How to Look Up a Function
text:
How to Look Up a Function


Inside Positron:

Type the function name and Positron shows a preview with argument hints
The argument you are currently typing is highlighted
help(function_name) or ?function_name opens the full documentation pane

# These are equivalent
help(pivot_longer)
?pivot_longer

Online:

Tidyverse functions have excellent reference pages with worked examples: tidyverse.org
R for Data Science (2e) is free online and is the textbook behind this course
Stack Overflow / AI for specific errors









Tip


Habit to build: Before Googling, try ?function_name first. The Examples section at the bottom of every help file is often the fastest way to understand how something works. When consulting AI, always force it to return a web source, so you can verify the output yourself!

title: QMIR 2026
text:
A Note on Pipes
The pipe |> works because tidyverse functions share one convention: the data frame is always the first argument.


View source
gapminder |> filter(year == 2007)
# is the same as:
filter(gapminder, year == 2007)



This means piping works for most tidyverse functions – and many others – but not universally. You will encounter base R functions that expect a vector, not a data frame, as their first argument.




This works:


View source
gapminder |>
  pull(lifeExp) |> # extracts a vector
  mean()


[1] 59.47444



This does not:


View source
# mean() expects a vector, not a tibble
gapminder |> mean() # Error


[1] NA





pull() extracts a single column as a vector – useful when you need to pipe into a base R function.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Why summarise() Exists
Last week you used mean(), median(), and similar functions inside summarise(). Here is why that pattern is necessary.



Base R functions expect vectors:
# Works on a vector
mean(gapminder$lifeExp)

# Does not work on a tibble
gapminder |> mean()  # Error

summarise() bridges tibbles and vector functions:
# Correct: summarise feeds each column
# as a vector to the function inside
gapminder |>
  summarise(
    mean_le = mean(lifeExp),
    sd_le   = sd(lifeExp)
  )



summarise() takes a tibble in, applies functions column-by-column, and returns a tibble. It is the bridge between the tidyverse world and base R summary statistics.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Controlling How Much Prints
Tibbles print only 10 rows by default. You can override this with print():


# Print first 25 rows
gapminder |> print(n = 25)

# Print all rows
gapminder |> print(n = Inf)

# Print specific columns wide
gapminder |> print(width = Inf)

This is purely for interactive inspection – do not put print(n = Inf) in a document you are rendering. Rendered output should show only what your reader needs to see, controlled with head() or careful selection.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Wide vs. Long – A Reminder
Recall from last week: tidy data means one variable per column, one observation per row.
Wide format (common in spreadsheets, Excel exports, Eurostat downloads):




country
ghg_2020
ghg_2021
ghg_2022




Germany
9.0
9.4
9.3


France
5.6
5.9
5.7





Long (tidy) format (what the tidyverse expects):




country
year
ghg




Germany
2020
9.0


Germany
2021
9.4


France
2020
5.6


France
2021
5.9







In the wide version, year is hiding inside column names. ggplot2 and dplyr cannot see it there. You must reshape first.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
pivot_longer() – Wide to Long




View source
library(tidyverse)

ghg_wide |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -country, # all columns except country
    names_to = "year", # new column for old headers
    values_to = "ghg" # new column for values
  )


Key arguments:

cols – which columns to reshape (everything that is not an ID variable)
names_to – what to call the new column that holds the old column names
values_to – what to call the new column that holds the values


Before:




country
2020
2021
2022




Germany
9.0
9.4
9.3


France
5.6
5.9
5.7




After:




country
year
ghg




Germany
2020
9.0


Germany
2021
9.4


Germany
2022
9.3


France
2020
5.6


France
2021
5.9


France
2022
5.7

title: QMIR 2026
text:
pivot_wider() – Long to Wide
The reverse: sometimes you need wide format for a table or for a specific model.




View source
ghg_long |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = "year", # column whose values become headers
    values_from = "ghg" # column whose values fill the cells
  )



Before:




country
year
ghg




Germany
2020
9.0


Germany
2021
9.4


France
2020
5.6




After:




country
2020
2021




Germany
9.0
9.4


France
5.6
NA












Note


pivot_longer() is far more common in practice. Most raw data arrives wide – tidy analysis requires long.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Practical Example: What Happens When You Load Eurostat Data

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Step 1: Skip the Metadata Rows
We know from inspecting the file that the real column headers (TIME, 2019, …) are on row 10. So we skip 9 rows:




View source
ghg_raw <- read_excel(
  "data/Eurostat_GHGpc.xlsx",
  sheet = "Sheet 1",
  skip = 9 # rows 1–9 are metadata
)

ghg_raw |> print(n = 5)


# A tibble: 38 × 5
  TIME                                      `2019` `2020` `2021` `2022`
  <chr>                                     <chr>   <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>
1 European Union - 27 countries (from 2020) 7.8       7      7.4    7.3
2 Belgium                                   10.6      9.6    9.9    9.3
3 Bulgaria                                  6.8       5.9    6.9    7.7
4 Czechia                                   12.4     11.6   12     11.4
5 Denmark                                   8.6       7.7    7.7    7.4
# ℹ 33 more rows



Better – but notice:

The first column is named TIME (we want country)
Column names 2019, 2020, … start with a number
There are stray footer rows at the bottom (Special value, :)







Tip


How to find the right skip value: Open the file in Excel and count how many rows appear before the header row. Subtract 1 – that is your skip value.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Step 2: janitor::clean_names() to the Rescue
The janitor package’s clean_names() function converts all column names to safe, consistent snake_case automatically:




View source
ghg_raw <- read_excel(
  "data/Eurostat_GHGpc.xlsx",
  sheet = "Sheet 1",
  skip = 9
) |>
  clean_names()

names(ghg_raw)


[1] "time"  "x2019" "x2020" "x2021" "x2022"




clean_names() converts 2019 → x2019, 2020 → x2020, etc. – adding an x prefix to columns that would otherwise start with a number.
This removes the backtick problem entirely. x2019 is a legal R column name that needs no special quoting.







Note


clean_names() also handles spaces, dashes, parentheses, and accented characters. Make it a habit to call it immediately after every read_excel() or read_csv().

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Step 3: Rename, Filter, Drop, Pivot


View source
ghg_clean <- ghg_raw |>
  rename(country = time) |> # TIME → country
  filter(!is.na(country), !country %in% c("Special value", ":")) |> # remove footer rows
  select(-x2019) |> # drop 2019 (not in GDP data)
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -country,
    names_to = "year",
    values_to = "ghg_pc"
  ) |>
  mutate(year = as.integer(str_remove(year, "^x"))) # "x2020" → 2020

ghg_clean |> head(4)


# A tibble: 4 × 3
  country                                    year ghg_pc
  <chr>                                     <int>  <dbl>
1 European Union - 27 countries (from 2020)  2020    7  
2 European Union - 27 countries (from 2020)  2021    7.4
3 European Union - 27 countries (from 2020)  2022    7.3
4 Belgium                                    2020    9.6









Tip


str_remove(year, "^x") strips the leading x that clean_names() added, then as.integer() converts the result to a number. The ^ means “start of string” – a small taste of regular expressions, which we will return to later.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Alternative: janitor::row_to_names()
Sometimes you cannot easily count the number of skip rows, or the header row is somewhere in the middle of the sheet. janitor::row_to_names() handles this differently: load everything, then promote a specific row to become the column names.




View source
# Load without skipping anything
ghg_raw <- read_excel(
  "data/Eurostat_GHGpc.xlsx",
  sheet = "Sheet 1",
  col_names = FALSE # don't treat row 1 as headers
)

# Promote row 10 to column names, drop rows above it
ghg_raw <- ghg_raw |>
  row_to_names(row_number = 10, remove_rows_above = TRUE) |>
  clean_names()




row_to_names() arguments:

row_number – which row contains the real headers
remove_rows_above = TRUE – drops all metadata rows above it automatically








Note


Both approaches (skip = and row_to_names()) achieve the same result. Use whichever is clearer for the file you are working with. skip = is simpler when the header row is fixed and predictable. row_to_names() is more robust when the file structure is irregular or when you are not sure how many rows to skip.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Why This Matters for Pivoting





country
x2020
x2021
x2022




Germany
9.0
9.4
9.3


France
5.6
5.9
5.7



Without clean_names(), the year columns would be named 2020, 2021 – requiring backticks everywhere. With clean_names(), they become x2020, x2021, which R handles without complaint.

After (long / tidy):



country
year
ghg_pc




Germany
2020
9.0


Germany
2021
9.4


Germany
2022
9.3


France
2020
5.6



Once pivoted, year is just a regular variable column. No backticks, no special treatment needed.






Note


The backtick trap still exists in R – column names starting with numbers or containing spaces must be wrapped in backticks. clean_names() is the easiest way to avoid it entirely.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Why Join?
Real research rarely lives in a single dataset. You might have:

GHG emissions from Eurostat in one file
GDP per capita from Eurostat in another file
EU accession dates from Wikipedia
Country codes from a reference package


Joining combines these into a single analysis-ready dataset by matching rows on a shared key – usually a country name, country code, or year.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Four Main Joins
All joins take two tables and a key (the column(s) to match on).







Join
What it keeps




left_join(x, y)
All rows from x; matching rows from y; NA where no match


right_join(x, y)
All rows from y; matching rows from x; NA where no match


inner_join(x, y)
Only rows that match in both tables


full_join(x, y)
All rows from both; NA wherever there is no match










Tip


In practice, left_join() is by far the most common. Start with your main analysis dataset on the left, and add variables from auxiliary datasets on the right. Rows in your main dataset are always preserved.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
left_join() – An Example




View source
# Both datasets are now tidy long format
# with matching columns: country + year

eu_data <- ghg |>
  left_join(gdp, by = join_by(country, year))


The by argument names the key columns – the columns that must match for two rows to be joined.
If the key column has different names in the two tables:
left_join(x, y, by = join_by(country == nation))

After joining, always verify the result:
# Check dimensions make sense
nrow(eu_data)
glimpse(eu_data)

# Check for unexpected NAs introduced by the join
eu_data |>
  filter(is.na(gdp_pc)) |>
  distinct(country, year)






Important


Always compare row counts before and after joining.
nrow(ghg)      # rows before
nrow(eu_data)  # rows after left_join
In a left_join, the row count of the result must equal the row count of the left table. If it is larger, your key has duplicates on the right side. If it is smaller, something went wrong. Check immediately – silent row loss is one of the most common data errors.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Visualising the Joins


x (GHG data):


# A tibble: 3 × 2
  country   ghg
  <chr>   <dbl>
1 Germany   9.3
2 France    5.7
3 Poland    9.4


y (GDP data):


# A tibble: 3 × 2
  country   gdp
  <chr>   <dbl>
1 Germany   117
2 France    103
3 Spain      86



left_join(x, y) – keeps all of x:


# A tibble: 3 × 3
  country   ghg   gdp
  <chr>   <dbl> <dbl>
1 Germany   9.3   117
2 France    5.7   103
3 Poland    9.4    NA


inner_join(x, y) – only matches:


# A tibble: 2 × 3
  country   ghg   gdp
  <chr>   <dbl> <dbl>
1 Germany   9.3   117
2 France    5.7   103

title: QMIR 2026
text:
NA in R
In R, missing values are represented as NA – not available.


View source
x <- c(1, 2, NA, 4)

is.na(x) # TRUE for each NA position
sum(is.na(x)) # count of NAs
mean(x) # NA -- most functions propagate NA by default
mean(x, na.rm = TRUE) # 2.33 -- explicitly remove NAs




NA is not zero. It means the value is unknown.
NA is contagious. Any arithmetic involving NA returns NA.
na.rm = TRUE is the standard argument to ignore NAs in summary functions.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Finding and Filtering Missing Values




View source
# Rows where a variable IS missing
data |>
  filter(is.na(gdp_pc))

# Rows where a variable is NOT missing
data |>
  filter(!is.na(gdp_pc))

# Count NAs per column
data |>
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(is.na(.))))





View source
# Remove rows with any NA
data |> drop_na()

# Remove rows with NA in specific column
data |> drop_na(gdp_pc)








Warning


drop_na() silently removes data. Before using it, check how many rows it would remove and ask whether those missing values are informative. A country that never reported GHG emissions is not the same as a country with a data entry error.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Problem with Imputation
A common temptation: replace NA with the column mean.


View source
data |>
  mutate(gdp_pc = ifelse(is.na(gdp_pc), mean(gdp_pc, na.rm = TRUE), gdp_pc))



What assumption does this imply?

You are asserting that the missing country’s GDP is average – but you have no evidence of that.
Mean imputation compresses variance and distorts correlations.
It treats missingness as random, which is rarely true. Poor countries may be less likely to report data.









Important


There is no free lunch with missing data. Every approach – dropping, imputing, or modelling – makes an assumption. The correct response is to be transparent: report how many observations are missing and why you handled them the way you did. Never silently drop or impute without acknowledgment.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Coding Missings on Import
Many datasets use special codes for missing values: -99, 999, "n/a", "." (Stata).


View source
# Tell readr which strings to treat as NA
my_data <- read_csv(
  "data/file.csv",
  na = c("", "NA", "-99", "n/a", ".")
)

# For Stata files, haven handles this automatically
library(haven)
my_data <- read_dta("data/file.dta")

# After loading, check for suspicious values
summary(my_data)









Tip


Always open your raw data file before loading it in R. A two-minute inspection in Excel or a text editor tells you the structure, encoding, missing value codes, and any metadata rows – exactly the information you need to load it correctly.

title: QMIR 2026
QMIR 2026
text:
Cleaning Country Names & Factors


View source
eu_data <- eu_data |>
  mutate(
    country = fct(country),
    country = fct_recode(
      country,
      "EU27" = "European Union - 27 countries (from 2020)"
    )
  )




# A tibble: 35 × 2
  country      n
  <fct>    <int>
1 EU27         3
2 Belgium      3
3 Bulgaria     3
4 Czechia      3
5 Denmark      3
# ℹ 30 more rows



Why factors? Factors store a fixed set of categories. They are essential for correct plot ordering, legend display, and later for regression dummy coding. fct_recode() renames specific levels without touching the others.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Recap: The Three Layers
Every ggplot is assembled from the same building blocks:
ggplot(data, aes(x = ..., y = ..., color = ...)) +
  geom_*() +
  scale_*() +
  labs() +
  theme_*()









Layer
Purpose




ggplot() + aes()
Canvas: data and variable-to-visual mappings


geom_*()
What to draw: points, lines, bars, boxes, text, …


scale_*()
How to map: axis limits, colors, log transforms, …


labs()
Text: title, subtitle, axis labels, captions, legend title


theme_*()
Appearance: fonts, gridlines, background, …

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Main Aesthetics
aes() maps variables to visual properties. The main ones:




Aesthetic
What it controls




x, y
Position on axes


color
Outline / line / point color


fill
Interior fill color (bars, boxes, ribbons)


alpha
Transparency (0 = invisible, 1 = opaque)


size
Point size or line thickness


shape
Point shape (circle, triangle, square, …)


linetype
Line style (solid, dashed, dotted, …)


label
Text label (used with geom_text())


group
Grouping without visual distinction











Note


Inside aes() = mapped to data. Outside aes() = fixed constant.
geom_point(color = "red") makes all points red.
geom_point(aes(color = continent)) colors by the continent variable.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Key Geoms






Geom
Use case




geom_point()
Scatterplot


geom_line()
Time series, trends


geom_col()
Bar chart (uses your values)


geom_bar()
Bar chart (counts rows)


geom_boxplot()
Distribution summary


geom_histogram()
Distribution of one variable


geom_density()
Smoothed distribution


geom_smooth()
Trend / regression line


geom_text()
Text labels at data positions


geom_hline() / geom_vline()
Reference lines





Geoms can be layered:
ggplot(data, aes(x, y)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE)
And combined with group aesthetics:
ggplot(data, aes(x = year,
                 y = value,
                 color = country)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point()

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Scales
Scales control how data values translate into visual properties.


Axis scales:
scale_x_log10()        # log transform x axis
scale_y_continuous(
  limits = c(0, 20),   # axis range
  breaks = seq(0, 20, 5) # tick marks
)
scale_x_discrete()     # for factor axes
Color scales:
scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1")
scale_color_manual(values = c("red", "blue"))
scale_fill_viridis_d()  # colorblind-friendly


Why scales matter:

A log scale compresses a skewed distribution, revealing patterns hidden on a linear scale
Color choice affects readability and accessibility – colorblind viewers cannot distinguish red and green
viridis palettes are colorblind-safe and print well in grayscale








Tip


For color-coded plots, always ask: would this still be readable printed in black and white, or by someone with red-green color blindness?

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Labels and Titles
labs(
  title    = "GHG Emissions and GDP per Capita, EU Member States",
  subtitle = "Data: Eurostat, 2020–2022",
  x        = "GDP per Capita (EU27 = 100)",
  y        = "GHG Emissions per Capita (tonnes)",
  color    = "Membership",
  caption  = "Note: EU27 average excluded."
)








Element
Guideline




Title
Describe what the plot shows, not what you conclude


Subtitle
Source, year, or key qualification


Axis labels
Always include units. “GDP” is ambiguous; “GDP per Capita (EU27 = 100)” is not


Caption
Data source, methodological notes, exclusions


Legend title
Rename via color =, fill = etc. in labs()

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Themes
