Themes control the non-data appearance of the plot.


Built-in themes:
theme_bw()       # white background, grey gridlines
theme_minimal()  # minimal gridlines, no border
theme_classic()  # white background, no gridlines
theme_void()     # blank canvas (for maps, diagrams)

Customising a theme:
theme_bw() +
theme(
  plot.title      = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
  axis.title      = element_text(size = 11),
  legend.position = "bottom",
  panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),  # remove minor gridlines
  aspect.ratio    = 0.618              # golden ratio height/width
)








Tip


For academic work, theme_bw() or theme_minimal() are good defaults. Avoid dark backgrounds for documents that will be printed. Set legend.position = "bottom" for plots with many categories – it preserves horizontal space.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Aspect Ratio – Does It Matter?
Which of these two plots do you prefer? (They show identical data.)








Note


Plot B uses aspect.ratio = 0.618 – the golden ratio (~1:1.618 width to height). Many designers and scientists find this proportion visually pleasing and it is a good default for scatter plots. For time series, a wider aspect ratio often reads better. There is no universal rule – but being deliberate about it matters.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Example: Scatterplot with Regression Line




View source
eu_data |>
  filter(year == 2022, country != "EU27") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gdp_pc, y = ghg_pc)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "steelblue") +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    x = "GDP per Capita (EU27 = 100)",
    y = "GHG Emissions per Capita (tonnes)",
    title = "Wealth and Emissions in the EU, 2022",
    caption = "Source: Eurostat"
  ) +
  theme(aspect.ratio = 0.618)








Figure 1: GDP and GHG emissions, EU member states 2022







Key elements:

geom_smooth(method = "lm") adds a linear regression line with a confidence band (se = TRUE)
alpha = 0.7 reduces overplotting
size = 2 makes points more visible
country != "EU27" excludes the aggregate from the point cloud

The positive slope suggests richer EU member states tend to emit more GHG per capita – though with substantial variation.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Example: Bar Chart with Custom Fill
text:
Example: Bar Chart with Custom Fill




View source
eu_data |>
  filter(year == 2022, !is.na(ghg_pc), ghg_pc > 0) |>
  mutate(
    is_eu27 = country == "EU27",
    country = fct_reorder(country, ghg_pc)
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = country, y = ghg_pc, fill = is_eu27)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("FALSE" = "steelblue", "TRUE" = "firebrick"),
    labels = c("Member State", "EU27 Avg"),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = "GHG per Capita (tonnes)",
    title = "GHG Emissions by EU Member State, 2022",
    caption = "Source: Eurostat"
  )








Figure 2: GHG emissions per capita by EU member state, 2022






Key elements:

fct_reorder(country, ghg_pc) sorts countries by emission value
fill = is_eu27 maps a logical variable to color
scale_fill_manual() assigns specific colors and renames the legend
coord_flip() rotates axes – equivalent to swapping x and y in aes(), but keeps your code readable when you are building a horizontal bar chart from a vertical one

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Facets
facet_wrap() splits a plot into small multiples by a grouping variable.




View source
eu_data |>
  filter(country != "EU27") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gdp_pc, y = ghg_pc)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "steelblue") +
  facet_wrap(~year) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    x = "GDP per Capita (EU27 = 100)",
    y = "GHG per Capita (tonnes)",
    caption = "Source: Eurostat"
  )








Figure 3: GDP vs. GHG by year, EU member states






facet_wrap(~ year) creates one panel per year.
facet_grid(row ~ col) for a two-dimensional grid.
Free vs. fixed scales:
# Each panel uses the same axes (default)
facet_wrap(~ year)

# Each panel scales to its own data
facet_wrap(~ year, scales = "free")






Warning


Free scales make within-panel patterns clearer, but make cross-panel comparison impossible. Be explicit in your figure caption about which you are using and why.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The .gitignore File
A .gitignore file tells Git which files to never track. Place it in the root of your repository.
# .gitignore

# R temporary files
.Rhistory
.RData
.Rproj.user/

# Rendered outputs (optional -- only if you regenerate from source)
*.pdf
*.html

# Large data files (if you store data elsewhere)
data/*.csv
data/*.xlsx

# OS files
.DS_Store        # Mac
Thumbs.db        # Windows







Tip


Never commit sensitive data (passwords, API keys, personal data) to a Git repository. Even private repositories can be accidentally made public. Add such files to .gitignore immediately.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Analytical Problem-Solving
When you face a data analysis task, divide and conquer.

Example: How do EU member states’ GHG emissions relate to their wealth?


Break this into sub-problems:


How do I load the GHG and GDP data into R? (import)
The data is wide – how do I reshape it? (pivot)
The country names differ slightly – how do I merge? (join)
I need a new column for membership status (mutate)
How do I visualise the relationship? (ggplot)
Each of these can be broken down further…










Tip


After each sub-problem:

Test in the console – does the intermediate result look right?
Render your document – does it still compile?
Commit to Git – you have a working checkpoint to fall back to

title: QMIR 2026
text:
A Practical Workflow Loop
1. Set up .qmd → render once → confirm it works
2. Write a small chunk → run in console → check result
3. When chunk is correct → render full document
4. Commit to Git with a clear message: "add data import step"
5. Repeat

Why render frequently? Problems compound. A chunk that works in isolation may fail in a rendered document because of:

Missing library() calls
Objects that exist in your environment but are not created in the document
Chunk execution order dependencies









Tip


Rule of thumb: If you would be upset to lose the last 20 minutes of work, commit it. Commits are free.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Inline Code in Quarto
You can embed R results directly in prose using the `{r} expression` syntax. Quarto evaluates the expression and inserts the result as text:

The dataset contains {r} nrow(eu_data) observations across {r} n_distinct(eu_data$country) countries.
The mean GHG per capita in 2022 was {r} eu_data |> filter(year == 2022) |> pull(ghg_pc) |> mean(na.rm = TRUE) |> round(1) tonnes.


The above renders as:

The dataset contains 105 observations across 35 countries. The mean GHG per capita in 2022 was 7.6 tonnes.









Tip


Inline code means your text and your numbers never get out of sync. When the data changes, the prose updates automatically on the next render. Use this for all specific numbers you report – never type a number by hand that R could compute for you.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
janitor: Beyond clean_names()


tabyl() – a tidy frequency table:


View source
eu_data |>
  filter(!is.na(ghg_pc)) |>
  tabyl(year)


 year  n   percent
 2020 28 0.3333333
 2021 28 0.3333333
 2022 28 0.3333333



adorn_totals() adds a totals row (or column):


View source
eu_data |>
  filter(!is.na(ghg_pc)) |>
  tabyl(year) |>
  adorn_totals("row")


  year  n   percent
  2020 28 0.3333333
  2021 28 0.3333333
  2022 28 0.3333333
 Total 84 1.0000000

title: QMIR 2026
text:
tabyl → adorn_totals() → kable()
One pipeline, publication-ready output:



View source
eu_data |>
  filter(!is.na(ghg_pc), country != "EU27") |>
  mutate(high_emitter = ghg_pc > median(ghg_pc, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  tabyl(year, high_emitter) |>
  adorn_totals(c("row", "col")) |>
  kable(
    col.names = c("Year", "Below Median", "Above Median", "Total"),
    caption = "EU member states by emission level relative to median"
  )



EU member states by emission level relative to median


Year
Below Median
Above Median
Total




2020
15
12
27


2021
14
13
27


2022
14
13
27


Total
43
38
81













Tip


tabyl() → adorn_totals() → kable() is one of the fastest ways to produce a clean, publication-ready summary table. adorn_totals(c("row", "col")) adds totals to both margins at once.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
kable() for Printed Tables
For tables in rendered documents, knitr::kable() produces clean output in both HTML and PDF:





View source
eu_data |>
  filter(year == 2022, country != "EU27") |>
  slice_max(ghg_pc, n = 5) |>
  select(country, ghg_pc, gdp_pc) |>
  kable(
    col.names = c("Country", "GHG pc (tonnes)", "GDP pc (EU27=100)"),
    digits = 1,
    caption = "Top 5 emitters in 2022"
  )



Top 5 emitters in 2022


Country
GHG pc (tonnes)
GDP pc (EU27=100)




Luxembourg
14.5
256


Ireland
13.0
235


Czechia
11.4
90


Estonia
10.7
85


Cyprus
10.2
94








kable() arguments:

col.names – rename columns in the output
digits – round numbers
caption – table caption (cross-referenceable with tbl- label)
format = "latex" / "html" – rarely needed, Quarto detects format automatically

For more control: the kableExtra package adds column alignment, row grouping, and color highlighting.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
stringr Quick Reference
stringr is the tidyverse package for working with text. All functions start with str_.


Detect & filter:
str_detect(x, "pattern")   # TRUE/FALSE -- use in filter()
str_starts(x, "A")         # starts with "A"
str_ends(x, "ia")          # ends with "ia"
str_count(x, "pattern")    # count occurrences
Extract & split:
str_sub(x, 1, 3)            # characters 1–3
str_extract(x, "[0-9]+")    # first regex match
str_split(x, "_")           # split on delimiter

Replace & clean:
str_replace(x, "old", "new")   # replace first match
str_replace_all(x, "old","new")# replace all matches
str_remove(x, "^x")            # remove first match
str_remove_all(x, " ")         # remove all spaces
str_to_lower(x)                # lowercase
str_to_upper(x)                # uppercase
str_to_title(x)                # Title Case
str_trim(x)                    # remove leading/trailing spaces
str_pad(x, width = 5,          # pad to fixed width
        side = "left", pad="0")








Note


You already used str_remove(year, "^x") in this session. The ^ is a regular expression anchor meaning “start of string”. Regular expressions are a powerful pattern language – we will return to them later, but str_detect() and str_remove() will cover most of your immediate needs.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
dplyr Select Helpers
select() accepts helper functions that match columns by name pattern – far more powerful than listing columns one by one.


# Columns that start with a prefix
data |> select(starts_with("ghg"))

# Columns that end with a suffix
data |> select(ends_with("_pc"))

# Columns containing a substring
data |> select(contains("year"))

# Columns matching a regex
data |> select(matches("^x[0-9]{4}$"))

# A range of consecutive columns
data |> select(country:gdp_pc)

# Everything except named columns
data |> select(-country, -year)

# Reorder: put country first, keep rest
data |> select(country, everything())

These helpers also work inside mutate(across(...)) and summarise(across(...)):
# Round all numeric columns ending in _pc
data |>
  mutate(across(ends_with("_pc"), round, 1))

# Mean of all columns starting with ghg
data |>
  summarise(across(starts_with("ghg"), mean,
                   na.rm = TRUE))






Tip


where() is another powerful helper – it selects columns by type:
data |> select(where(is.numeric))
data |> select(where(is.character))

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Conditional Variables: if_else() and case_when()

Two tidyverse tools for creating variables whose value depends on a condition.


if_else() — one condition, two outcomes:
gap_2007 |>
  mutate(
    rich = if_else(gdpPercap > 20000,
                   "High income",
                   "Low/mid income")
  )

First argument: the condition
Second: value if TRUE
Third: value if FALSE







Note


Prefer if_else() over base R ifelse() in tidyverse pipelines — it is stricter about types and handles NA more predictably.




case_when() — multiple conditions:
gap_2007 |>
  mutate(
    income_group = case_when(
      gdpPercap > 30000 ~ "High",
      gdpPercap > 10000 ~ "Middle",
      gdpPercap >  3000 ~ "Low",
      .default          =  "Very low"
    )
  )

Conditions are evaluated top to bottom
First TRUE condition wins — order matters
.default catches everything not matched above








Tip


Both functions work inside mutate() and return a new column. Use if_else() for simple yes/no splits. Switch to case_when() as soon as you have three or more outcomes — a chain of nested if_else() calls becomes unreadable fast.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Exercise Overview

Clone the Week 5 exercise repo from GitHub
Open exercise_week05.qmd in Positron
You will work with the Eurostat GHG and GDP datasets
Tasks follow the arc of today’s session: import → reshape → join → visualise
Fast finishers: optional extension tasks at the bottom


Show import live: load one file, demonstrate the skip argument, show what happens without skip. Then pivot, then join. Let them work on the rest. Reserve 10 min for homework preview.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Homework

Distributed via GitHub (same workflow as previous weeks)
Submit a rendered Quarto document (.qmd + rendered PDF)
The PDF should show only plots and prose – no code
Due: one week from today

You will work with the same Eurostat datasets to produce five publication-quality plots. The homework will ask you to combine everything from today: import with readxl, reshape with pivot_longer, join with left_join, and visualise with ggplot2.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Homework Preview: What You Will Produce
text:
Homework Preview: What You Will Produce

Boxplot – distribution of GHG emissions per capita across years (excluding EU27)
Bar chart – GHG emissions in 2022, countries ordered by emissions, EU27 highlighted
Scatterplot – GDP vs. GHG, colored by EU membership era (pre/post 2000 accession), with regression line
Labelled scatterplot – country ISO codes as labels instead of points, faceted by membership era
Labelled scatterplot with free scales – same as above, but each panel scales independently


For each plot: clear labels, titles, captions, and 1–3 sentences of interpretation.
Final question: Why might free scales in Plot 5 be problematic?

title: QMIR 2026
text:
QMIR 2026
Week 7: Markov Chain Monte Carlo
 Tristan Muno            March 26, 2026


Opening slide. Welcome back. Give a one-sentence orientation: “Last week: Bayesian foundations and grid approximation. Today: why grid fails and how MCMC solves it.”
Logistics reminder: this session is more lecture-heavy than usual. Hands-on component arrives in week 8. Today is about building the mental model.
Estimated session arc: Parts I-III (~20 min), Part IV (~20 min), Part V (~15 min), Parts VI-VII (~15 min), Part VIII (~20 min).

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Week 7 Learning Goals
By the end of today, you should be able to:

Articulate why grid approximation is computationally intractable for realistic models, and why an alternative is necessary
Explain the Monte Carlo idea: approximating integrals by random sampling and counting, and describe its convergence properties
Define a Markov Chain and explain what a stationary distribution is
State the Metropolis acceptance rule, explain why it produces the correct stationary distribution, and show why \(P(\text{data})\) cancels
Explain qualitatively how Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) differs from random-walk Metropolis and why it is more efficient
Interpret brms output: trace plots, \(\hat{R}\), ESS, and posterior distributions
Understand that MCMC convergence cannot be proven, only falsified, and recognize what non-convergence looks like



Read goals aloud at the start; return to this slide in Part VII to show which section addressed each goal.
Goal-to-section mapping: Goals 1-2 covered in Parts I-III; Goals 3-4 in Part IV; Goal 5 in Part V; Goals 6-7 in Parts VI and VIII.
The take-home exam will engage directly with Goals 4, 6, and 7.
Verb choice signals expected depth: “articulate,” “explain,” “define,” “state,” “interpret,” “understand.” Students do not need to derive acceptance criteria from scratch but do need to explain in plain language why P(data) cancels.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Statistics Is (Still) Counting
Before we move forward, a brief ritual re-activation.

Last week’s core claim:







Note


Statistics, at its heart, is counting.

What happened the data we actually observed
What did not happen alternative outcomes that were possible
What could have happened the full space of hypotheses about the data-generating process
How much we have counted the precision and remaining uncertainty of our inferences







And the key conceptual move of Bayesian inference:

Probability is a degree of rational belief — not a long-run frequency. Parameters are uncertain; they get probability distributions. The data is fixed; it is observed.



Activation slide. Speak it rather than read it. Students should recognize it from last week. Goal: wake up relevant neural pathways. Budget ~2 minutes max.
“Statistics is counting” framing is from McElreath’s Statistical Rethinking. Grounds everything in one simple idea from which all Bayesian machinery follows.
Quick diagnostic question if students look uncertain: “Someone remind me: what is the difference between a frequentist confidence interval and a Bayesian credible interval?” This surfaces remaining confusion before new material.
Key contrast to reinforce: in frequentist thinking, parameters are fixed unknowns; the data are random. In Bayesian thinking, parameters are uncertain and get distributions; the observed data are fixed.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Bayes’ Theorem: The Proportional Form
The three ingredients of Bayesian inference:
\[
\underbrace{P(\theta \mid \text{data})}_{\text{Posterior}}
= \frac{\underbrace{P(\text{data} \mid \theta)}_{\text{Likelihood}} \cdot \underbrace{P(\theta)}_{\text{Prior}}}{\underbrace{P(\text{data})}_{\text{Normalizing constant}}}
\propto P(\text{data} \mid \theta) \cdot P(\theta)
\tag{1}\]

The proportional form captures everything about the posterior’s shape. The normalizing constant \(P(\text{data})\) merely rescales the numerator so the posterior is a valid probability distribution.









Important


Last week’s problem. \(P(\text{data})\) is an integral over the entire parameter space: \[
P(\text{data}) = \int_{\Theta} P(\text{data} \mid \theta) \cdot P(\theta) \, d\theta
\tag{2}\] We approximated it on a discrete grid. With 7 hypotheses: trivial. With realistic models: computationally impossible. Today we fix this.






Re-state the mathematical objects clearly before showing why the denominator becomes a problem.
Equation 1: read the equation left to right. Left side is what we want (posterior). Right numerator is what we can compute (likelihood times prior). Denominator is the normalizing constant.
Equation 2: this integral is the source of all the trouble. For 50 parameters, a 50-dimensional integral. This is why grid approximation fails.
Emphasize: the proportional form (the last expression) is the scientific content. The denominator is just bookkeeping – but bookkeeping we cannot do analytically for realistic models.
Transition: “So – if we cannot compute this integral, how do we learn the shape of the posterior?” Pause. “The answer is MCMC. And it is genuinely clever.”

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Dimensional Explosion
Grid approximation: evaluate the posterior at every point on a grid.




Parameters
Grid points per dim.
Total grid cells
Feasible?




1
1,000
\(10^3\)
✓


3
1,000
\(10^9\)
Barely


10
1,000
\(10^{30}\)
No


50
1,000
\(10^{150}\)
Never





The observable universe contains approximately \(10^{80}\) atoms.









Note


A simple bivariate linear regression model has three continuous parameters (\(\alpha\), \(\beta\), \(\sigma\)). A multilevel model with 50 groups might easily have 50+ parameters. Grid approximation is not merely slow at scale – it is categorically impossible regardless of hardware.







The key insight: we do not need to visit every point in parameter space.
We need to spend time where the posterior mass actually is.



Present the table row by row, letting the numbers land. Do not rush.
The universe comparison (\(10^{80}\) atoms) is not hyperbole. \(10^{150}\) is genuinely beyond any physical scale we can imagine.
The key insight in the final box: drive it home with an analogy: “Imagine you want to know which floor of a building is most occupied. You do not need to count every room. You can find out by following the crowd. MCMC is the mathematical version of following the crowd.”

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Unpacking the Acronym
MCMC = Markov Chain Monte Carlo

Two ideas, combined:


Monte Carlo
Using random sampling to approximate quantities that are hard to compute analytically.
Taught first in Part III
The philosophical engine: approximate integrals by counting random draws.

Markov Chain
A sequence of states where the next state depends only on the current state.
Taught second in Part IV
The structural mechanism: construct a random walk whose long-run behavior is the target distribution.










Note


MCMC combines both: we design a Markov Chain whose random wandering – when run long enough – produces samples from the target posterior. Crucially, this never requires computing \(P(\text{data})\).
We teach Monte Carlo first, Markov Chains second, then show how they combine. The order of teaching is the reverse of the acronym.






This slide maps the session structure explicitly. Point to each column as you introduce it.
Key promise to make explicit: “By the end of Part IV, you will see exactly why P(data) never needs to be computed.” Do not explain it yet – let the anticipation build through the Island King story.
Budget: ~3 minutes for this slide.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Origins I: Los Alamos, 1946


Stanisław Ulam, recovering from illness, plays solitaire.
He wonders: what is the probability of a successful deal?
Analytical calculation: intractable. Simulate many games and count: natural.
The insight: random simulation can replace analytical integration. The law of large numbers guarantees convergence.
Ulam shares the idea with John von Neumann and Nicholas Metropolis at Los Alamos National Laboratory. The method is applied to neutron diffusion calculations for nuclear weapons design.
The name “Monte Carlo” – after the casino in Monaco – is coined by Metropolis as a code name for classified work (Metropolis and Ulam 1949).








Note


The physical problem: how do neutrons travel through fissile material? The collision events are random. Tracking individual particles analytically is impossible. Simulating millions of random neutron paths and averaging outcomes: feasible.
The same logic that models nuclear reactors will, fifty years later, power Bayesian inference in political science.







Tell this as a story, not as bullet points. Key narrative beats:

Ulam plays solitaire while recovering from illness, 1946 – a mundane, relatable origin.
The insight: simulation beats closed-form integration.
Los Alamos context: classified, wartime, high stakes – signals the method’s power.
The casino name: memorable.

Connection to the course: “The same mathematical idea Ulam used to simulate neutron collisions in 1946 is what Stan uses to compute your posterior distributions in 2026. The tools your bachelor’s thesis will use share a direct intellectual lineage with the Manhattan Project.” This lands every time.
Do not get drawn into discussion of the Manhattan Project itself. One sentence of context, then move on.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Origins II: From Physics to Statistics
text:
Origins II: From Physics to Statistics


1953 – The Metropolis Algorithm
Metropolis, Rosenbluth, Rosenbluth, Teller and Teller publish the first MCMC algorithm (Metropolis et al. 1953). Written by physicists, for physics. The method computes equilibrium properties of particle systems.
The acceptance rule – accept moves probabilistically in proportion to relative density – is the key innovation. We will see it today.
Three decades of dormancy
The method was essentially unknown to statisticians. The connection to Bayesian inference was not made.

1990 – The Bayesian Revolution
Gelfand and Smith (1990) introduce Gibbs sampling to statisticians, demonstrating that MCMC can compute marginal posterior distributions for realistic Bayesian models. The paper ignites a transformation of applied statistics. For a comprehensive treatment, see Gelman et al. (2013a) and Gelman et al. (2013b).
2012–present – Stan and brms
Stan (Carpenter et al. 2017) implements Hamiltonian Monte Carlo / NUTS – a dramatically more efficient algorithm. brms (Bürkner 2017) provides a formula interface accessible to applied researchers. This is what we use.



The historical arc tells a story of cross-disciplinary fertilization: physicists invent the method, statisticians ignore it for 37 years, then it revolutionizes Bayesian computation.
Point students to BDA3 (Gelman et al.) chapters 11-12 for a rigorous treatment if they want the mathematical depth.
Stan (2012): Columbia, led by Andrew Gelman and Bob Carpenter. brms translates R formula syntax to Stan code.
Budget: ~3 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Core Idea
Monte Carlo integration: replace an intractable integral with an average over random samples.

If \(\theta^{(1)}, \theta^{(2)}, \ldots, \theta^{(S)} \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} p(\theta)\), then by the law of large numbers:
\[
\mathbb{E}_{p}[f(\theta)] = \int f(\theta)\, p(\theta)\, d\theta \approx \frac{1}{S} \sum_{s=1}^{S} f\!\left(\theta^{(s)}\right)
\quad \text{as } S \to \infty
\tag{3}\]


The approximation error decreases at rate \(1/\!\sqrt{S}\): doubling the samples halves the standard error.









Note


Why does this help us? We want to summarize the posterior – compute its mean, credible intervals, tail probabilities. If we could draw samples from the posterior, we could compute any such quantity by averaging (Equation 3). The samples replace the integral.
The posterior itself is the hard object; its summaries become easy once we have samples. The challenge is to generate those samples without being able to evaluate the posterior at every point.






Equation 3: walk through the notation carefully.

\(f(\theta)\) is any function of the parameter: the posterior mean (\(f(\theta) = \theta\)), posterior variance, a tail probability, a derived quantity.
The left side is an integral – hard. The right side is an average over samples – easy.
The law of large numbers guarantees the right side converges to the left as S grows.

Critical advantage: the convergence rate \(1/\sqrt{S}\) is independent of dimension. Grid approximation costs \(k^d\) (exponential in number of parameters). Monte Carlo costs \(1/\sqrt{S}\) regardless. This is why Monte Carlo wins at scale.
Budget: ~4 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The \(\pi\) Example: Setup
Monte Carlo can approximate the value of \(\pi\) – a deterministic constant – using random sampling.



Geometry: A quarter-circle of radius 1 is inscribed in the unit square.
\[
\text{Area of quarter-circle} = \frac{\pi}{4}
\quad
\text{Area of square} = 1
\]
Strategy:

Draw points \((x, y)\) uniformly at random in \([0, 1]^2\)
A point falls inside the quarter-circle if \(x^2 + y^2 \leq 1\)
The fraction of points inside converges to \(\pi/4\):

\[
\hat{\pi} \approx 4 \cdot \frac{\text{points inside}}{S}
\tag{4}\]








Note


We never computed \(\pi/4\) analytically. We counted. This is Monte Carlo integration: approximate the area of a region by the fraction of random points that fall inside it.
The same logic applied to Bayesian posteriors: approximate the posterior by counting where random samples land – once we know how to generate those samples.







Equation 4: the fraction of points inside the arc, multiplied by 4, is the Monte Carlo estimate of \(\pi\).
Present the geometry visually before the formula. Draw the quarter-circle on the board if helpful.
Budget: ~3 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The \(\pi\) Example: Simulation


Figure 1: Left: 1,000 random points in the unit square, colored by location relative to the quarter-circle. Right: the Monte Carlo estimate of π converges toward the true value as sample size grows.


Approximation error \(\propto 1/\!\sqrt{S}\): independent of dimension. This is the crucial advantage over grid approximation (cost \(\propto k^d\), exponential in \(d\)).



Left panel: blue points fall inside the arc (\(x^2 + y^2 \leq 1\)); orange points fall outside.
Right panel: the estimated \(\hat{\pi}\) starts noisy for small S and stabilizes near the true value (orange dashed). The estimate is never exact – residual error always exists – but it converges reliably.
Bridge: “Does this remind you of anything from last week?” Grid approximation was deterministic; this is random. Both are approximation strategies. Monte Carlo has better scaling.
Budget: ~3 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Bridge: From \(\pi\) to the Posterior


The \(\pi\) problem

Sample uniformly in the unit square
Count the fraction inside the quarter-circle
The fraction approximates \(\pi/4\)
We counted; we never integrated analytically


The posterior problem

We want to summarize \(P(\theta \mid \text{data})\)
If we had samples \(\theta^{(s)} \sim P(\theta \mid \text{data})\), we could compute any posterior quantity by averaging
We cannot sample uniformly – the posterior is not uniform
We need a smart sampler that concentrates on high-posterior regions










Important


The gap: we know that random samples from the posterior would let us approximate any quantity of interest. But how do we generate samples from a distribution we cannot evaluate everywhere?
The Markov Chain answers this question. It constructs a random process that, over many steps, spends time in regions in proportion to their posterior probability – without ever needing to compute \(P(\text{data})\).






This is the conceptual hinge between Parts III and IV. Drive the parallel home explicitly, column by column.
Key difference to highlight: for \(\pi\), we could sample uniformly in the square and then check which points fall inside. For the posterior, “sampling uniformly in parameter space” wastes almost all effort in low-probability regions – exactly the grid approximation problem.
Transition line: “The Markov Chain is a random walk that is designed to be intelligent. It moves around parameter space in a way that naturally concentrates on high-probability regions, without ever needing a map of the entire landscape.”
Budget: ~3 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
What Is a Markov Chain?
Definition: A Markov Chain is a sequence of random variables \(\theta^{(1)}, \theta^{(2)}, \theta^{(3)}, \ldots\) satisfying the Markov property:
\[
P\!\left(\theta^{(t+1)} \mid \theta^{(t)},\, \theta^{(t-1)},\, \ldots,\, \theta^{(1)}\right) = P\!\left(\theta^{(t+1)} \mid \theta^{(t)}\right)
\tag{5}\]
The next state depends only on the current state. The chain has no long-term memory.

Stationary distribution: For a well-constructed chain, after sufficiently many steps, the probability of being in any state \(\theta\) stabilizes and no longer depends on the starting point. This limiting distribution is called the stationary distribution \(\pi(\theta)\).









Tip


The key insight we will exploit:
We can design a Markov Chain whose stationary distribution is exactly our posterior \(P(\theta \mid \text{data})\).
After enough steps, samples from this chain are draws from the posterior – even though we never computed \(P(\text{data})\).






Equation 5: the Markov property in formal notation. Conditional on the present state at time \(t\), the future state at \(t+1\) is independent of all past states. This is a memoryless property.
Intuitive example: today’s weather depends on yesterday’s, but not directly on the weather two weeks ago (given yesterday). That is a Markov Chain over discrete states.
Stationary distribution: if a chain has been running long enough that the distribution of its current position has stopped changing, it has reached its stationary distribution. Every step from then on is a draw from that distribution.
For deeper formal treatment, point students to Gelman et al. (2013a), Chapter 11.
Budget: ~4 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Island King: Setup
Adapted from McElreath (2020).

A king governs a ring of 10 islands, each with a different population. He wants to visit each island in proportion to its population – spending more time on larger islands.


The constraint: he cannot see the full population list. He can only ask about his current island and one proposed neighboring island at a time.








Figure 2: Island populations. The king does not know the total; he can only compare adjacent islands.








The analogy: population plays the role of posterior probability; the king’s position plays the role of the current parameter value.



Figure: bar chart of island populations {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 3, 2, 1, 2, 3}. Island 5 is most populous; islands 1 and 8 least.
Introduce the story before showing the bar chart. Let students visualize it.
The asymmetric populations are more interesting than a simple ramp: they have a non-obvious maximum and are not monotone.
State the analogy line explicitly and clearly: “Every piece of this story maps onto Bayesian inference. Island populations are posterior probabilities. The king’s visits are the MCMC samples.”
The total population is unknown to the king – that is the key. He can only compare neighbors.
Budget: ~3 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Island King: The Rule

The king follows a single, simple decision rule at each step:




Propose a random step to an adjacent island (flip a coin: left or right)
If the proposed island is more populated than the current: always go
If the proposed island is less populated: go with probability equal to the population ratio; otherwise stay





Formally, accept the proposed move with probability:
\[
p(\text{accept}) = \min\!\left(1,\; \frac{\text{pop}_{\text{proposed}}}{\text{pop}_{\text{current}}}\right)
= \min\!\left(1,\; \frac{P(\theta^* \mid \text{data})}{P(\theta^{(t)} \mid \text{data})}\right)
\tag{6}\]




Now substitute the full Bayes’ theorem and watch what happens:
\[
\frac{P(\theta^* \mid \text{data})}{P(\theta^{(t)} \mid \text{data})}
= \frac{P(\text{data} \mid \theta^*) \cdot P(\theta^*) \,/\, P(\text{data})}{P(\text{data} \mid \theta^{(t)}) \cdot P(\theta^{(t)}) \,/\, P(\text{data})}
= \frac{P(\text{data} \mid \theta^*) \cdot P(\theta^*)}{P(\text{data} \mid \theta^{(t)}) \cdot P(\theta^{(t)})}
\tag{7}\]










Important


\(P(\text{data})\) cancels exactly in the ratio (Equation 7). The acceptance rule only ever needs the proportional form of the posterior – likelihood \(\times\) prior. The intractable integral is never evaluated.






This is the most important slide of the session. Walk through it slowly and deliberately.
Step 1 (plain language rule): three cases – always go uphill, go probabilistically downhill (with probability = the ratio), stay otherwise.
Equation 6: the formal acceptance probability. It uses the posterior ratio – one posterior for the proposed state, one for the current state.
Equation 7: expand both posteriors using Bayes’ theorem. P(data) appears in both numerator and denominator with the same value (it is a constant with respect to \(\theta\)). It cancels exactly.
The punchline: the Metropolis algorithm only needs \(P(\text{data} \mid \theta)\) and \(P(\theta)\) – both computable. It never needs \(P(\text{data})\).
The mathematical guarantee is detailed balance – proven in Appendix A.
Budget: ~6 minutes. This is the conceptual core.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Island King: Demonstration


Figure 3: Left: king’s position during the first 300 steps. Right: fraction of time spent on each island after 10,000 steps vs. true target proportions.


Left panel: the king’s path over 300 steps. It is a random walk – irregular, backtracking – but it frequently visits island 5 (most populous) and rarely lingers at islands 1 and 8 (least populous). Point this out explicitly.
Right panel: after 10,000 steps, the MCMC distribution (blue) closely matches the target proportions (orange).
Critical observation to verbalize: “The king never knew the total population. He only compared his current island to a proposed neighbor. Yet the long-run behavior is exactly right.”
Budget: ~3 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
From Islands to Parameters
The translation is exact and complete:








Island world
Bayesian inference




Ring of 10 islands
Parameter space \(\Theta\) (the range of plausible \(\theta\) values)


Population of island \(k\)
Posterior probability \(P(\theta_k \mid \text{data})\)


King’s current position
Current parameter sample \(\theta^{(t)}\)


Proposed neighboring island
Proposed new parameter value \(\theta^*\)


Population ratio (acceptance rule)
Posterior ratio: \(P(\theta^*\mid\text{data}) \,/\, P(\theta^{(t)}\mid\text{data})\)


Fraction of time on island \(k\)
Posterior probability of \(\theta_k\)


Total population (unknown)
\(P(\text{data})\) – never needed, always cancels












Note


Result: The Metropolis algorithm constructs a Markov Chain over parameter space whose stationary distribution is the posterior \(P(\theta \mid \text{data})\). After warm-up, its samples are draws from the posterior – without ever computing \(P(\text{data})\).
The samples can then be used as Monte Carlo estimates (Equation 3) of any posterior quantity: means, credible intervals, tail probabilities, derived quantities.






Walk through each row of the table carefully. Every element of the story has a precise Bayesian counterpart.
The last row is the punchline. Bold font is deliberate. The king’s ignorance of the total population is not a limitation – it is precisely what makes the algorithm computationally tractable. P(data) cancels in the ratio.
Synthesis statement: “When brms runs your model next week, this is what is happening.”
Budget: ~4 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Posterior as a Landscape (1D)
The posterior distribution has a spatial interpretation: think of probability as height.


Figure 4: The same posterior, two framings. Left: the standard distribution curve — height = probability density. Right: the negative log-posterior — high-probability regions become valleys.

The negative log-posterior \(U(\theta) = -\log P(\theta \mid \text{data})\) is the potential energy landscape. High-probability regions become valleys; low-probability regions become peaks. Stan works entirely in log-probability space for numerical stability.


Left panel: familiar posterior distribution. Peak at \(\theta \approx 1\) is the posterior mode.
Right panel: negate and log-transform. The peak becomes a valley – the lowest point of the bowl. High probability region = deep valley.
Why log? Products of many probabilities quickly underflow to numerical zero in floating-point arithmetic. Logs convert products to sums. Stan works in log space for this reason.
Why negate? Stan minimizes \(U(\theta)\), which is minimized where the posterior is maximized. This allows gradient-based computation.
Budget: ~3 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Posterior as a Landscape (2D)
With two parameters, the posterior becomes a surface – a 2D landscape.


Figure 5: Left: posterior surface for two correlated parameters (top-down view; lighter = higher probability). Right: the same surface as a negative log-posterior — darker = deeper valley = higher probability.

For \(k\) parameters: a \(k\)-dimensional landscape. We cannot visualize it, but the metaphor holds in any dimension. MCMC explores this landscape rather than evaluating it everywhere.


Left panel: top-down contour map. The oval shape reflects correlation between the two parameters. Center = highest posterior probability.
Right panel: the oval becomes a bowl. The center is the MAP estimate – the deepest point of the valley.
Key point for transition to HMC: the correlation creates an elongated bowl. Random-walk Metropolis will struggle here.
Budget: ~2 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
section: HMC: The Ball on the Landscape
text:
HMC: The Ball on the Landscape
Hamiltonian Monte Carlo introduces momentum into the sampler – a frictionless ball rolling on the potential energy landscape (Hoffman and Gelman 2014; Gelman et al. 2013b).
Each trajectory: a random momentum kick launches the ball; leapfrog integration simulates Newtonian physics; the trajectory endpoint is proposed as the next sample.







Note


The three panels on the next slides show three independent illustrative examples, each starting from a different hand-chosen position on the landscape. They are not consecutive MCMC steps – they are thought experiments showing how the ball behaves from different starting positions on the same landscape.






This callout preempts the natural student question: “Why does trajectory B not start where trajectory A ended?” The answer is that these are independent illustrations, not a sequential chain.
Introduce the figure conventions before showing each panel: open circle = start, filled triangle = proposed sample after L leapfrog steps.
Budget for all three panels together: ~6 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
HMC: Trajectory A


Figure 6: Trajectory A: ball starts at θ ≈ −2.8 on the left shoulder. A positive momentum kick sends it rightward. It rolls down, overshoots the valley bottom, climbs the right side, slows, turns back under gravity, and the L = 28 leapfrog steps run out on the return journey.


Walk through the physics step by step:

Ball starts at \(\theta \approx -2.8\) on the left shoulder (high \(U\), low probability).
Positive momentum kick flicks it rightward (downhill).
Ball accelerates rolling into the valley, gains kinetic energy.
It overshoots the valley bottom (\(\theta \approx 1\)) because it has momentum.
It climbs the right side, slowing as potential energy increases.
“Gravity” (the gradient of \(U\)) pulls it back – the ball turns around.
The leapfrog counter hits L = 28 steps on the way back, and the trajectory stops wherever the ball is at that moment. That position is the proposed sample (triangle).

Key point: the ball does not stop at the valley bottom. It follows physics until the step counter runs out.
Budget: ~3 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
HMC: Trajectory B


Figure 7: Trajectory B: ball starts at θ ≈ 3.2 on the right shoulder. A negative momentum kick sends it leftward. It rolls down, crosses the valley, climbs the left side, turns back under gravity, and the steps run out partway through the return journey.


Walk through the physics: ball starts on the right shoulder (\(\theta \approx 3.2\)), receives a negative (leftward) momentum kick, rolls down into the valley from the right, overshoots the bottom, climbs the left side, turns back under gravity, and the steps run out on the way back.
Point out the parallel to Trajectory A: same physics, different starting side of the valley, different kick direction.
Key efficiency point: both trajectories crossed the full width of the posterior in a single proposal. A random-walk sampler would need many small steps to achieve this.
Budget: ~2 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
HMC: Trajectory C


Figure 8: Trajectory C: ball starts near the valley bottom (θ ≈ 0.2), close to the posterior mode. A strong rightward kick sends it far up the right shoulder. The steps run out high on the right side — proposing a low-probability sample. The Metropolis step may reject this proposal.


Each trajectory receives a fresh random momentum kick and rolls for exactly L steps. The endpoint is the proposed sample. Even when rejected, the trajectory itself explored the landscape efficiently.



Trajectory C illustrates an important case: the ball starts near the high-probability valley bottom but receives a strong kick that sends it far into a low-probability region. The proposed sample (triangle) ends up high on the right shoulder, where \(U(\theta)\) is large (low probability).
In this case, the Metropolis acceptance step is likely to reject the proposal – the chain would stay at the start (open circle). But even so, this trajectory explored the landscape.
This is also the case that illustrates why the random momentum kick is essential: without it, the ball would just sit at the bottom of the valley (the MAP estimate) and never explore uncertainty.
Summary sentence to say aloud: “Every trajectory is an independent thought experiment: different starting point, different momentum kick, different path. In a real chain, the start of each trajectory is the accepted endpoint of the previous one.”
Budget: ~3 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Why Random-Walk Metropolis Struggles
The basic Metropolis algorithm proposes small, random steps in parameter space.


Figure 9: Random-walk Metropolis on the 2D correlated posterior. Left: the first 200 steps as a path. Right: all 3,000 samples. The chain moves slowly and struggles to explore the correlated, elongated posterior.

Problems: many proposals rejected; chain moves slowly; high autocorrelation between successive samples; effective sample size is low relative to raw sample count.


Left panel: the path is jagged and mostly stays in a small neighborhood. After 200 steps, the chain has barely moved from the upper-left corner.
Right panel: after 3,000 steps, coverage is poor. The chain has not reached the lower-right tail of the posterior ellipse.
Root cause: random-walk proposals are blind. They do not use any information about the shape of the posterior.
Budget: ~3 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
section: HMC vs. Random-Walk on the 2D Posterior
text:
HMC vs. Random-Walk on the 2D Posterior


Figure 10: Same target distribution; same number of samples (3,000); same starting point. HMC explores the posterior much more efficiently than random-walk Metropolis.


The contrast with the previous slide (random-walk Metropolis) should be stark.
Left panel: HMC’s path covers much more of the posterior in the first 200 steps.
Right panel: all 3,000 samples are much better distributed across the posterior. Both center and tails are adequately sampled.
Why does it handle correlation? HMC uses the gradient of the log-posterior, which accounts for the direction of the correlation.
Technical comparison: HMC’s higher ESS per raw sample means 3,000 HMC samples may be equivalent to 10,000+ random-walk samples on a correlated posterior.
Budget: ~3 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Log-Probability: A Technical Note

Stan (and therefore brms) works entirely in log-probability space. Three reasons:



1. Numerical stability
Likelihoods of large datasets involve products of many small probabilities: \[
P(\text{data} \mid \theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(y_i \mid \theta)
\tag{8}\]
For \(N = 200\) and each factor \(\approx 0.3\), the product \(\approx 10^{-105}\) – underflows to zero in floating point. The log-likelihood is \(\sum_i \log P(y_i \mid \theta)\) – numerically well-behaved.




2. Gradient computation
HMC requires the gradient \(\nabla_\theta \log P(\theta \mid \text{data})\) at every leapfrog step. This is tractable for standard likelihood families; the product form becomes a sum in log space.




3. The sign convention
Stan minimizes \(U(\theta) = -\log P(\theta \mid \text{data})\) (the negative log-posterior). High-probability regions are valleys; the ball rolls toward high-probability regions. This is not gradient descent – the ball keeps rolling and exploring the full landscape. It does not converge to a single point.



Equation 8: demonstrate with numbers. \(0.3^{200} \approx 3.7 \times 10^{-105}\). Log converts products to sums: \(\log \prod_i P(y_i \mid \theta) = \sum_i \log P(y_i \mid \theta)\).
Gradient: Stan uses automatic differentiation to compute \(\partial / \partial\theta \log p(\theta \mid \text{data})\) exactly. Required at every leapfrog step.
Critical distinction to emphasize: students often confuse HMC with gradient descent. Gradient descent always moves downhill – it converges to a minimum (a single point estimate). HMC uses the gradient to propose trajectories, but the ball has momentum and can move uphill. It explores the full landscape in proportion to the posterior.
Connection: the MAP estimate is what you would get from gradient descent. MCMC gives you the full posterior.
Budget: ~3 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Why One Chain Is Not Enough
text:
Why One Chain Is Not Enough

Running a single MCMC chain has three fundamental problems:



1. Starting-point sensitivity
Every chain must begin somewhere. Early samples reflect the starting point, not the posterior. These samples are misleading and must be discarded. But how many? One chain alone cannot tell you when it has “found” the posterior.




2. Invisible local trapping
A single chain might explore only a subset of the posterior – a local neighborhood or one mode of a multimodal distribution – without visiting other high-probability regions. With only one chain, this failure is completely invisible.




3. No internal benchmark for convergence
Whether a chain has converged can only be assessed by comparing multiple chains started from different, overdispersed starting points. If all chains agree after warm-up, that is evidence of convergence. If they disagree, non-convergence is visible.










Note


Standard practice in brms: 4 chains, 2,000 iterations each (1,000 warm-up, 1,000 post-warm-up), for a total of 4,000 posterior draws. The 4 chains start from different dispersed points and must converge to the same distribution.






Island king analogy for point 1: “Imagine dropping the king onto a remote island far from the high-population islands. His first 100 steps will reflect where he started, not the population-proportional distribution. We discard those steps. But with only one chain, how long do we wait?”
For point 2: “If the posterior has two well-separated modes, a single chain might find one and never discover the other. The chain looks perfectly converged – stationary, well-mixed – but it represents only half the posterior.”
The 4-chain default in brms reflects current best practice as described in Gelman et al. (2013a).
The next slide illustrates problem 2 graphically.
Budget: ~2 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Why One Chain Is Not Enough: Illustrated
text:
Why One Chain Is Not Enough: Illustrated


Figure 11: A bimodal posterior with two separated modes. The orange chain starts near the left mode and never discovers the right mode. The blue chain starts near the right mode and never discovers the left. Each chain looks healthy in isolation — but each tells only half the story.


Top panel: both chains look perfectly stationary and well-mixed within their own neighborhood – the “hairy caterpillar” shape is there. If you only ran Chain A (orange), you would diagnose convergence and be wrong.
Bottom panel: the true posterior has two equal-weight modes. Chain A (orange rug, bottom) covers only the left mode. Chain B (blue rug, top) covers only the right mode. Neither alone gives you the full posterior.
Key diagnostic implication: R-hat would be very large here. The two chains have means at \(\approx -2.5\) and \(\approx +2.5\) respectively, so between-chain variance >> within-chain variance. R-hat would flag this immediately.
Real-world context: multimodal posteriors arise in mixture models, some nonlinear models, and occasionally in poorly identified models. Running 4 chains from dispersed starts and checking R-hat is your main safeguard.
Budget: ~3 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Warm-Up and Adaptation
Each chain goes through two distinct phases:


Figure 12: Default brms run: 4 chains × 2,000 iterations. The first 1,000 iterations of each chain are warm-up (discarded). The final 4 × 1,000 = 4,000 samples constitute the posterior.

Burn-in / warm-up: early samples discarded because the chain has not yet found the posterior. The chain is “traveling” from its starting point to the high-probability region.


Adaptation (specific to HMC/NUTS): during warm-up, Stan automatically tunes two algorithmic parameters:

Step size \(\varepsilon\): how far the ball moves per leapfrog step
Mass matrix \(M\): rescales parameter space to make the posterior more spherical, improving efficiency on correlated posteriors

After warm-up, adaptation freezes and sampling begins. The tuned parameters remain fixed for the post-warm-up phase.


Figure: a simple timeline bar. Gray = warm-up (discarded). Blue = sampling (kept). The split at iteration 1000 is the default.
Two distinct concepts under “warm-up”: (1) burn-in – universal to all MCMC; (2) adaptation – specific to HMC/NUTS.
Step size \(\varepsilon\): too small means many steps needed per trajectory, expensive. Too large means high rejection rate. Stan adapts \(\varepsilon\) during warm-up to target ~80% acceptance rate.
Mass matrix \(M\): estimates the posterior covariance during warm-up. In a correlated posterior, \(M\) rescales the space to make it more spherical.
After warm-up: both \(\varepsilon\) and \(M\) freeze.
Budget: ~3 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Convergence: What We Can and Cannot Know
text:
Convergence: What We Can and Cannot Know







Important


A fundamental epistemological limit:
Convergence cannot be proven. It can only be falsified.
A chain that looks converged might not be – it may not have discovered a distant mode, or may be stuck in a correlated region invisible to our diagnostics. No finite set of statistics certifies convergence; they can only fail to detect non-convergence.






This mirrors Karl Popper’s falsificationism: we cannot confirm “the chain has converged,” we can only fail to detect evidence of non-convergence.




What diagnostics do:

They are tests for non-convergence, not certificates of convergence. A model that passes all diagnostics has not been proven to have converged – it has simply not been caught failing.





The practical implication: run diagnostics systematically, report them, and treat them as minimum requirements – not as guarantees. Use multiple chains, long runs, and prior sensitivity analysis as additional safeguards.



Deliver this slide with emphasis. Students consistently underestimate this point.
Popper analogy: just as science cannot verify a theory (only falsify it), MCMC diagnostics cannot verify convergence (only detect non-convergence).
Practical consequences to list out loud:

Always run multiple chains from dispersed starts.
Prefer longer runs over shorter ones.
Treat diagnostics as necessary but not sufficient.
When in doubt, refit with more iterations, different starts, or reparameterized model.

Budget: ~3 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Convergence Diagnostics

Four key diagnostics – all reported automatically by brms:



Trace plot – plot of sampled values over iterations, one line per chain. A converged chain looks like a stationary, noisy signal – the “hairy caterpillar.” All chains overlap and interleave. Non-convergence looks like separated, trending, or stuck chains.




\(\hat{R}\) (R-hat, Gelman-Rubin) – ratio of between-chain to within-chain variance (Vehtari et al. 2021): \[
\hat{R} = \sqrt{\frac{\widehat{\text{Var}}^+(\theta \mid y)}{W}}
\tag{9}\]
If chains have converged to the same distribution, between-chain \(\approx\) within-chain variance, so \(\hat{R} \approx 1\). Rule: \(\hat{R} < 1.01\) (improved split-\(\hat{R}\), Vehtari et al. 2021). Values above this indicate non-convergence.




Effective Sample Size (ESS) – MCMC samples are autocorrelated: each depends on the previous. ESS estimates the equivalent number of independent samples. brms reports Bulk-ESS (central summaries) and Tail-ESS (extreme quantiles). Rule: ESS > 400 per parameter.




Divergent transitions – HMC-specific: the ball trajectory became numerically unstable. Even a handful of divergences indicates a problem with the posterior geometry or model specification. brms highlights them prominently.



Walk through each diagnostic. Students will see all four in summary(fit) output and in trace plots.
Equation 9: the Gelman-Rubin statistic. If chains all come from the same distribution, between-chain variance \(\approx\) 0, so \(\hat{R} \approx 1\). If chains differ, between-chain variance inflates the numerator, pushing \(\hat{R}\) above 1. The 1.01 threshold is the current standard (Vehtari et al., 2021).
ESS: if successive samples have correlation \(\rho\), ESS \(\approx S \times (1 - \rho)/(1 + \rho)\). For \(\rho = 0.5\), 4000 raw samples \(\approx\) 1333 effective. Bulk-ESS for means/medians; Tail-ESS for credible interval endpoints.
Divergences: even 1-2 per chain warrants investigation. The fix is usually reparameterization, not more iterations.
Budget: ~5 minutes (important slide).

title: QMIR 2026
text:
What Convergence Looks Like


Figure 13: Top: four well-mixed chains on a Normal(0,1) target — the ‘hairy caterpillar.’ Chains overlap and are stationary. Bottom: four non-converged chains — same target, tiny step size, extreme starting points. Chains remain separated.


Top panel: four chains of different colors interleaving densely. You cannot distinguish them by color alone – they overlap completely. The “hairy caterpillar” description is apt: furry, dense, going nowhere in particular. This is what convergence looks like.
Bottom panel: four chains clearly separated at very different locations, barely moving. Starting points were at \(\theta \in \{-9, -5, 5, 9\}\) and the tiny step size means they cannot traverse the parameter space to find each other.

\(\hat{R}\) for these chains would be large (between-chain variance >> within-chain variance).
ESS would be very low (extreme within-chain autocorrelation due to near-zero step size).

After this slide: “In Part VIII, you will see actual trace plots from a brms model fit to real data. They should look like the top panel.”
Budget: ~2 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Synthesis
1. Monte Carlo: approximate intractable integrals by drawing random samples and averaging. The law of large numbers guarantees convergence at rate \(1/\!\sqrt{S}\), independent of dimension.

2. Markov Chain: design a sequence of states – each depending only on the previous – whose stationary distribution is the target posterior. After warm-up, the chain provides samples from the posterior.


3. Metropolis / HMC: a specific acceptance rule that ensures the stationary distribution is correct. The rule uses only the ratio of posterior values at two parameter values. \(P(\text{data})\) cancels in this ratio and is never computed. HMC uses gradient information to propose efficient trajectories, rather than small random steps.









Note


In practice: specify a prior and a likelihood family. brms translates this to a log-posterior function. Stan simulates a frictionless ball rolling on the negative log-posterior landscape – across 4 chains, for 2,000 iterations each (1,000 warm-up, 1,000 post-warm-up). After checking \(\hat{R}\), ESS, and trace plots, the 4,000 post-warm-up samples are your posterior. No \(P(\text{data})\) was computed.
This workflow runs identically regardless of model complexity – from a simple linear regression to a multilevel model with 50 parameters. Only the log-posterior function changes.






Consolidation slide. Budget: ~3 minutes maximum. This is a summary, not new material.
The three bullets map onto learning goals 2, 3, and 4.
Connect each point back to its teaching story: (1) \(\pi\) approximation, (2) Island King, (3) Island King + HMC landscape.
Final sentence is the course philosophy: “The machinery does not change. The science does. Starting next week, we focus on the science.”

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Synthetic Dataset
We generate data from a known data-generating process so we can verify that brms recovers the truth.


View source
df_synth |>
  ggplot(aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_point(color = col_1, alpha = 0.35, size = 1.0) +
  geom_abline(
    intercept = 2,
    slope = 0.7,
    color = col_2,
    linewidth = 1.1,
    linetype = "dashed"
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = 1.8,
    y = 0.5,
    label = "True line: y = 2 + 0.7 x",
    color = col_2,
    size = 3.2,
    hjust = 0
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), aspect.ratio = 0.55) +
  labs(
    x = "x",
    y = "y",
    title = "Synthetic dataset (n = 1,000)",
    subtitle = "True parameters: α = 2,  β = 0.7,  σ = 1"
  )




Figure 14: Synthetic data: 1,000 observations drawn from the true model. Dashed line = the true data-generating line. Our goal: recover α = 2, β = 0.7, σ = 1 from the data alone.
\[
y_i = \underbrace{2}_{\alpha} + \underbrace{0.7}_{\beta} \cdot x_i + \varepsilon_i, \quad \varepsilon_i \sim \text{Normal}(0,\, 1), \quad i = 1, \ldots, 1000
\tag{10}\]


Figure: scatter of 1,000 data points around the dashed true regression line. The spread of points around the line reflects \(\sigma = 1\). With n = 1000, the true line is clearly visible.
Explain the synthetic data strategy: “Because we generated the data ourselves, we know the true parameter values. If brms recovers \(\alpha \approx 2\), \(\beta \approx 0.7\), \(\sigma \approx 1\), we have evidence the sampler is working correctly.”
Equation 10: this is the data-generating process. A Gaussian linear regression – the same model we study in depth in week 8.
Budget: ~3 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The brms Model Call


View source
fit_synth <- brm(
  formula = y ~ x, # model structure: linear predictor
  data = df_synth, # the data
  prior = c(
    prior(normal(0, 10), class = Intercept), # P(α): weakly informative
    prior(normal(0, 10), class = b), # P(β): weakly informative
    prior(exponential(1), class = sigma) # P(σ): positive-valued
  ),
  chains = 4, # 4 independent chains
  iter = 2000, # 2000 total iterations per chain
  warmup = 1000, # first 1000 are warm-up (discarded)
  seed = 42 # reproducibility
)





formula: the likelihood family (Gaussian by default) and the mean structure (\(\mu_i = \alpha + \beta x_i\))
prior: Normal(0, 10) is extremely broad relative to the true values (2 and 0.7), so the posterior is driven almost entirely by the likelihood; with n = 1000 the priors are essentially irrelevant
chains = 4, iter = 2000, warmup = 1000: four chains \(\times\) 1,000 post-warmup steps = 4,000 posterior draws
seed: locks the random number generator for reproducibility (Week 2 principle!)




brms translates this to Stan code, compiles to C++, and runs NUTS across all 4 chains.


Walk through each argument. Students will write this code every week from now on.
formula: the tilde syntax encodes the linear predictor. y ~ x means model y as a function of x. Intercept included automatically.
prior: Normal(0, 10) is so broad that it barely constrains the posterior at all. With n = 1000 observations, the likelihood completely dominates – the priors are irrelevant in practice.
chains, iter, warmup: 4 chains \(\times\) 1000 post-warmup = 4000 total posterior draws.
seed: critical for reproducibility.
Budget: ~4 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Prior Specification

Always verify the priors brms is using:


View source
get_prior(formula = y ~ x, data = df_synth)


                prior     class coef group resp dpar nlpar lb ub tag
               (flat)         b                                     
               (flat)         b    x                                
 student_t(3, 2, 2.5) Intercept                                     
 student_t(3, 0, 2.5)     sigma                             0       
       source
      default
 (vectorized)
      default
      default







View source
prior_summary(fit_synth)


          prior     class coef group resp dpar nlpar lb ub tag       source
  normal(0, 10)         b                                              user
  normal(0, 10)         b    x                                 (vectorized)
  normal(0, 10) Intercept                                              user
 exponential(1)     sigma                             0                user












Note


get_prior() shows what priors are available to set. prior_summary() shows what was actually used. Always check both: brms fills in defaults for any unspecified parameter. Understanding your priors is part of understanding your model.






Prior transparency is a key Bayesian principle: every analysis makes prior assumptions. Bayesian analysis makes them explicit and auditable.
get_prior(): shows all parameters in the model and which prior classes they belong to.
prior_summary(): shows the actual priors used in the fitted model.
Pedagogical point: you should always be able to answer “what did I assume before seeing the data?” for every parameter.
Budget: ~2 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The summary() Output


View source
summary(fit_synth)


 Family: gaussian 
  Links: mu = identity 
Formula: y ~ x 
   Data: df_synth (Number of observations: 1000) 
  Draws: 4 chains, each with iter = 2000; warmup = 1000; thin = 1;
         total post-warmup draws = 4000

Regression Coefficients:
          Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS
Intercept     1.99      0.03     1.93     2.06 1.00     4122     3083
x             0.71      0.03     0.65     0.77 1.00     4229     3057

Further Distributional Parameters:
      Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS
sigma     0.99      0.02     0.95     1.03 1.00     3864     3333

Draws were sampled using sampling(NUTS). For each parameter, Bulk_ESS
and Tail_ESS are effective sample size measures, and Rhat is the potential
scale reduction factor on split chains (at convergence, Rhat = 1).




Walk through the output line by line. This is the most important output to be able to read and interpret.
Header: model formula, number of observations (1000), likelihood family (gaussian), chain setup (4 chains, 2000 iterations, 1000 warmup).
Population-Level Effects table (the key part):

Estimate: posterior mean – should be very close to 2 (Intercept), 0.7 (b_x), and 1 (sigma).
Est.Error: posterior standard deviation (uncertainty).
l-95% CI and u-95% CI: 95% credible interval – the true values should lie clearly inside.
Rhat: should be 1.00 for all parameters.
Bulk_ESS and Tail_ESS: should be well above 400.

Check recovery: with n = 1000 and weakly informative priors, the likelihood dominates completely. The posterior means should be essentially identical to the OLS estimates and very close to the true data-generating values.
Budget: ~4 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Trace Plots: Diagnosing Convergence


View source
mcmc_plot(
  fit_synth,
  type = "trace",
  variable = c("b_Intercept", "b_x", "sigma")
)




Figure 15: Trace plots for the three model parameters. Four chains in different colours. All chains are stationary and overlapping — the hairy caterpillar.


Three panels, one per parameter. x-axis = iteration (1 to 1000, post-warmup only). y-axis = parameter value. Four colored lines = four chains.
What to look for:

Stationarity: the mean and spread of each chain should not trend over time.
Mixing: the four chains should be interleaved – you should not be able to visually identify them by color.
Consistent levels: all chains should explore the same region of parameter space.

What to read off the figure: Intercept chains centered around 2, b_x around 0.7, sigma around 1.
Connect back to the earlier trace plot comparison: “This looks like the good case from Part VI – the hairy caterpillar. Not like the separated chains.”
Budget: ~3 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Posterior Distributions


View source
mcmc_plot(
  fit_synth,
  type = "areas",
  variable = c("b_Intercept", "b_x", "sigma"),
  prob = 0.95
) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = c(2, 0.7, 1),
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = col_2,
    linewidth = 0.9
  ) +
  labs(subtitle = "Dashed lines = true data-generating parameter values")




Figure 16: Posterior distributions for the three model parameters. Shaded region = 95% credible interval. Dashed lines = true data-generating values. All three true values lie clearly inside the 95% credible intervals.


Each panel shows the full posterior density for one parameter.
What each element means:

The curve: full posterior density \(p(\theta \mid \text{data})\).
The shaded region: 95% credible interval. There is 95% posterior probability the true parameter lies in this range.
The vertical solid line: posterior median.
The orange dashed lines: true parameter values (only possible to show because we generated the data).

What to check: with n = 1000 and weakly informative priors, the posteriors should be narrow, centered very close to the true values, with the dashed lines clearly inside the shaded 95% regions.
Budget: ~4 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Posterior Predictive Check


View source
pp_check(fit_synth, ndraws = 50)




Figure 17: Posterior predictive check: 50 datasets simulated from posterior draws (light blue) versus the observed data (dark blue). The observed data distribution should be well within the range of simulated datasets if the model fits well.


Logic: draw parameter values \(\theta^{(s)}\) from the posterior, simulate a new dataset \(y^{\text{rep}}\) from \(P(y \mid \theta^{(s)})\), compare simulated datasets to the observed data \(y\).
If the model is well-specified, observed data should look like a typical draw from the posterior predictive distribution. A systematic discrepancy indicates model misspecification.



Figure: light blue lines = 50 simulated datasets. Dark blue line = observed data.
What to look for: the dark blue line should be similar to the light blue lines.
What success looks like here: a roughly bell-shaped distribution of \(y\) values; the observed distribution (dark blue) falls within the range of simulated distributions (light blue).
Preview: we will develop more sophisticated checks in weeks 8-13.
Budget: ~3 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Reading the Output
Connecting the brms output to the MCMC machinery:


The 4,000 rows in the posterior are samples from 4 chains \(\times\) 1,000 post-warm-up steps
Each step used the NUTS algorithm – a frictionless ball rolling on the negative log-posterior surface – to propose an accepted move
Convergence was assessed by \(\hat{R}\) (\(\approx 1.00\) for all parameters), \(ESS\) (\(> 400\) for all parameters) and visually by trace plots (stationary, mixed chains)
The posterior distributions directly encode our updated beliefs about \(\alpha\), \(\beta\), and \(\sigma\) after combining the prior and the likelihood
\(P(\text{data})\) was never computed. It cancelled in every acceptance ratio










Note


When brms prints Compiling Stan program... and Chain 1: Iteration 1/2000, you now know exactly what is happening and why. Next week, the model structure becomes more interesting. The MCMC machinery runs identically underneath.






Closing slide for the brms section. Connect each bullet back to the relevant part of the session.
“4,000 rows” = Part VI (multiple chains, warmup setup).
“NUTS algorithm” = Part V (ball on landscape, HMC).
“Convergence assessed by R-hat” = Part VI (diagnostics, falsificationism).
“Posterior distributions” = Part IV/V (stationary distribution, Monte Carlo estimator Equation 3).
“P(data) never computed” = Part IV (Equation 7, the cancellation result).
Budget: ~3 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Learning Goals: Achieved?




#
Learning Goal
Key Answer
Where




1
Why is grid approximation intractable?
Cost grows as \(k^d\) (exponential in parameters); 50 params = \(10^{150}\) cells
Part I


2
What is the Monte Carlo idea?
Approximate integrals by averaging over random samples; error \(\propto 1/\sqrt{S}\), dimension-free
Part III


3
What is a Markov Chain and its stationary distribution?
A memoryless sequence of states; stationary = the long-run distribution the chain converges to
Part IV


4
Why does \(P(\text{data})\) cancel?
The acceptance rule uses only a posterior ratio; \(P(\text{data})\) appears in both numerator and denominator and cancels (Equation 7)
Part IV


5
How does HMC differ from Metropolis?
HMC uses gradient of the log-posterior to propose long, physics-guided trajectories; much lower autocorrelation
Part V


6
How do I interpret brms output?
Check \(\hat{R} < 1.01\), ESS \(> 400\), trace plots for mixing; read Estimate/CI as posterior mean and 95% credible interval
Part VIII


7
Why can convergence not be proven?
Diagnostics are tests for non-convergence, not certificates; a chain may pass all checks yet miss a distant mode
Part VI






Return to this slide at the end to close the session loop.
Walk down the table row by row. Ask students to indicate by a show of hands which goals they feel confident about vs. uncertain about – this gives you quick formative feedback.
Goals 4, 6, and 7 are most likely to appear on the take-home exam. Flag this explicitly.
Budget: ~4 minutes.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Appendix A: Metropolis Algorithm – Full Pseudocode
text:
Appendix A: Metropolis Algorithm – Full Pseudocode



The complete Metropolis algorithm for Bayesian inference:
Input: unnormalized posterior \(\tilde{p}(\theta) = P(\text{data} \mid \theta) \cdot P(\theta)\), proposal distribution \(q(\theta^* \mid \theta^{(t)})\), starting point \(\theta^{(1)}\), number of iterations \(T\).
For \(t = 1, 2, \ldots, T\):

Propose: draw \(\theta^*\) from the proposal distribution \(q(\theta^* \mid \theta^{(t)})\)
Compute acceptance ratio: \[
r = \min\!\left(1,\; \frac{\tilde{p}(\theta^*)}{\tilde{p}(\theta^{(t)})}\right) = \min\!\left(1,\; \frac{P(\text{data} \mid \theta^*) \cdot P(\theta^*)}{P(\text{data} \mid \theta^{(t)}) \cdot P(\theta^{(t)})}\right)
\tag{11}\]
Accept or reject: draw \(u \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1)\)

If \(u \leq r\): set \(\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^*\) (accept)
If \(u > r\): set \(\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)}\) (reject, stay)


Output: sequence \(\theta^{(1)}, \theta^{(2)}, \ldots, \theta^{(T)}\) – samples from the stationary distribution \(P(\theta \mid \text{data})\) (after warm-up).









Note


Why does this work? The acceptance rule satisfies detailed balance: for any two states \(\theta\) and \(\theta'\), the probability of being in state \(\theta\) and moving to \(\theta'\) equals the probability of being in \(\theta'\) and moving back to \(\theta\): \[
\pi(\theta) \cdot P(\theta \to \theta') = \pi(\theta') \cdot P(\theta' \to \theta)
\tag{12}\] Detailed balance implies that \(\pi(\theta) = P(\theta \mid \text{data})\) is the stationary distribution. This is the mathematical guarantee that the Metropolis algorithm produces posterior samples.







Appendix slide for students wanting the formal underpinning. Present briefly if time permits; otherwise direct students to it as a reference.
Equation 11: the full algorithm written out as pseudocode with the acceptance ratio made explicit. Point out: the unnormalized posterior \(\tilde{p}(\theta)\) is all that is needed – P(data) has already been dropped.
Equation 12: the mathematical guarantee. Detailed balance is a sufficient condition for a Markov Chain to have a given stationary distribution.
Informal verification: suppose \(p(\theta') > p(\theta)\). Then \(P(\theta \to \theta') = q(\theta' \mid \theta) \times 1\) (always accept going uphill). And \(P(\theta' \to \theta) = q(\theta \mid \theta') \times p(\theta)/p(\theta')\). With symmetric proposal \(q\), detailed balance holds. QED (sketch).

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Appendix B: MCMC Algorithm Comparison












Algorithm
Year
Proposal
Key innovation
Limitation
Used in




Metropolis
1953
Symmetric random walk
First MCMC algorithm; simple acceptance rule
Slow mixing; requires tuning of step size
Historical; rarely used in practice today


Metropolis-Hastings
1970
Asymmetric proposals allowed
Generalises Metropolis to arbitrary proposals
Still requires manual tuning
Textbooks; simple models


Gibbs Sampler
1984/1990
Conditional distributions
Acceptance rate = 1; dimension-by-dimension
Requires tractable conditionals; slow on correlated posteriors
BUGS, JAGS


HMC
1987/2011
Leapfrog trajectory (gradient-guided)
Uses gradient information; large, efficient jumps
Requires differentiable posterior; trajectory length must be tuned
Stan (basic HMC)


NUTS
2014
Adaptive leapfrog (no U-turn)
Eliminates trajectory length tuning; full automation
Computationally expensive per step vs. random walk
Stan (default) → brms












Note


brms uses NUTS via Stan for all models. The progression from Metropolis to NUTS represents 60 years of algorithmic development: each generation solved the main limitation of the previous one. NUTS is currently the state of the art for general-purpose Bayesian computation with continuous parameters.
References: Metropolis et al. (1953); Gelfand and Smith (1990); Hoffman and Gelman (2014); Bürkner (2017); Gelman et al. (2013b).






Reference table for students wanting to situate the session in the broader MCMC literature. Walk through it briefly if time permits.
NUTS (2014): automated HMC. The “no U-turn” criterion detects when the trajectory starts doubling back – further simulation is wasteful. Stops automatically. This is what Stan implements.
Historical note: BUGS and JAGS (using Gibbs) dominated before Stan. Stan (2012) represented a generational change in computational efficiency.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
QMIR 2026


Opening. Take-home exam details have just been covered (separate 10-minute block). Now: the main session.
Arc: formal overview of control variables → live application → formal overview of interactions → live application → canonical workflow recap.
The two research questions are connected to real political science literatures, but the data are synthetic and the DGP is known. Emphasize that this is pedagogically deliberate: we know the truth and check whether our models recover it.
Both stories will recur in the homework and take-home exam.


Week 9: Multivariate Regression and Interactions
 Tristan Muno            April 16, 2026

title: QMIR 2026
section: Take Home Exam – What to Expect
text:
Take Home Exam – What to Expect

You will receive a dataset with some contextual background and a research question. Your task is to answer that research question using the data and methods you have learned throughout the course.

Think of your submission as the methods/research design and results/analysis sections of an empirical paper.

You are expected to conduct a full analysis on your own, covering:

Describing and exploring the data

Formulating and justifying a model

Estimating the model

Interpreting and discussing your results

title: QMIR 2026
section: Take Home Exam – What to Expect
text:
Take Home Exam – What to Expect

Your submission will be graded along four dimensions:

Argumentation — Are your methodological choices well reasoned and justified?

Interpretation — Are your results interpreted correctly and communicated clearly?

Formalities — Does your rendered PDF look and read like a section of a published paper?

Code — Does your code run? Is your repository well-structured and your code readable and well-documented?


The exam will be distributed via GitHub, just like the homework assignments.

The detailed grading scheme will be provided together with the exam. The total is 100 points. Grades are assigned according to the percentage thresholds set out in the Prüfungsordnung.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Take Home Exam – What to Expect
text:
Take Home Exam – What to Expect

Submission timeline and late policy:
