You have two weeks (14 days) to complete the exam from the time of release.

Submissions after the deadline are accepted for up to one additional week (7 days).

A penalty of 5 points per started day of delay will be applied to late submissions.

Submissions received more than 7 days after the deadline will not be accepted and will be graded as failed.

In cases of illness or other valid reasons (Härtefälle), the general provisions of the Prüfungsordnung apply. Appropriate documentation (e.g., a medical certificate) must be submitted without delay.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Week 9 Learning Goals


Read aloud. Six goals map onto four parts + summary.
Goals 1–3 → Parts I–II (control variables); Goals 4–6 → Parts III–IV (interactions).
The take-home exam will directly assess all six goals: students will need a DAG, a formal model statement, at least one control variable, and model comparison.


By the end of today, you should be able to:

Explain omitted variable bias (OVB) formally and use a DAG to classify control variables as confounders, mediators, or colliders
Extend the Bayesian regression model to multiple predictors and interpret each coefficient as a partial effect
Handle categorical predictors using dummy (indicator) variables
Specify a multiplicative interaction model and interpret conditional marginal effects formally
Extract posterior marginal effects from a brms interaction model — correctly, without a delta method approximation
Compare competing models using Bayesian leave-one-out cross-validation (LOO-IC)

title: QMIR 2026
text:
A Question Worth Fighting Over


Frame the substantive debate before introducing any methods. Students should care about the answer before they learn how to find it.
This is a real empirical debate in comparative politics (Bolzendahl & Brooks 2007, Bratton & Ray 2002, Swers 2002). The synthetic data mimics its structure.
Key tension: wealthier countries simultaneously have more women in parliament AND spend more on health. Without controlling for wealth, we cannot isolate the legislative representation effect.
Transition: “The challenge is separating the legislative effect from the wealth effect. That is what control variables are for.”



Does the share of women in parliament increase public health expenditure?


The legislative representation theory: Women legislators bring different policy priorities to parliament — specifically, greater support for social spending, preventive care, and public health infrastructure.




The empirical problem: Wealthier democracies simultaneously have: - More women in parliament (greater gender equality in politics) - Higher health spending (larger government capacity)
A simple bivariate analysis will confuse the effect of gender representation with the effect of wealth.








Note


Synthetic dataset: \(n = 120\) country-years, calibrated to realistic OECD/emerging-economy magnitudes.

\(X\) = Women in parliament (%)
\(Z\) = GDP per capita (thousands USD, PPP) — the confounder
\(Y\) = Public health expenditure (% GDP)

True DGP coefficient for \(X\): \(\beta_X = 0.12\)

title: QMIR 2026
text:
What is a DAG?


Introduce the vocabulary before showing the diagram. Students need “node” and “arrow” as shared language.
Stress the asymmetry: arrows encode our theoretical prior about direction. The data cannot tell us which way the arrow points — that is a substantive judgment.
Key point: you draw the DAG before looking at the data or fitting any model. It is a commitment, not a discovery.
Transition: “Let’s draw one for our specific research question.”


A Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) is a graphical representation of our theoretical assumptions about how variables are related.



Anatomy

Nodes (boxes) = variables
Directed arrows (\(\rightarrow\)) = theoretical claims about the direction of influence
Acyclic = no variable can cause itself (no feedback loops)


What a DAG does

Translates a verbal theory into a precise graphical structure
Makes our assumptions explicit and contestable
Tells us which variables to include, exclude, or never condition on











Note


You draw the DAG before fitting anything. It encodes your prior theoretical beliefs — not what the model estimated. The arrows are your theoretical commitments, not statistical output.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Our Research Question as a DAG
text:
Our Research Question as a DAG


Walk through each arrow explicitly: “GDP drives women’s representation, GDP drives health spending, women’s representation drives health spending.”
The confounding path: Women in Parliament <- GDP -> Health Spending. This is the back-door path we need to close.
“We draw this before we open R. The DAG is our theoretical model. The brms call is just fitting it numerically.”
Note: the arrow from Women in Parliament to Health Spending is what we care about — the partial association we want to estimate.




Figure 1: DAG for our research question. The back-door path from Women in Parliament to Health Spending runs through GDP per capita (a confounder). We close this path by conditioning on GDP per capita.


Reading the DAG: GDP per capita is a confounder — it influences both \(X\) (Women in Parliament) and \(Y\) (Health Spending). Omitting it will inflate our estimate of the \(X \rightarrow Y\) association. Controlling for it closes the back-door path.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
DAGs: Three Types of Relationships


This taxonomy determines what to control for. Spend time on each type.
Confounders: Z is associated with both X and Y through a common cause. Omitting Z biases the X -> Y estimate. MUST control.
Mediators: X -> M -> Y. M is on the path from X to Y. If we control for M, we block the indirect path — “post-treatment bias.” Usually don’t control (unless estimating direct effect only).
Colliders: X and Y both have paths into C. Controlling for C opens a spurious association between X and Y even when they are unrelated. NEVER control.
Mnemonic: “Fork = control | Pipe = careful | Inverted fork = never.”




Figure 2: Three structural types in a DAG. The rule: control for confounders (forks), be cautious with mediators (pipes), never condition on colliders (inverted forks).

Rule: Identify the minimal sufficient adjustment set — the smallest set of variables that, when controlled, blocks all non-causal (back-door) paths from \(X\) to \(Y\).









Important


More controls are not always better. A confounder that is not controlled for biases the estimate — but a mediator or collider that is controlled for introduces a different bias. The minimal sufficient adjustment set (the smallest set that closes all back-door paths) is the principled target, not the largest possible set of covariates. Appropriateness depends on the estimand and the substantive research context.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
A Note on Causal Inference


This is a crucial epistemic boundary. Students sometimes assume that drawing a DAG means we are doing causal inference. We are not.
“The regression machinery is blind to arrow direction. It can only tell you the sign and magnitude of associations. The arrows are your theoretical input, not statistical output.”
Emphasise: DAGs are useful precisely because they force us to be explicit about what we assume. But the machinery only recovers associations — the causal interpretation requires the additional (untestable) assumption that the adjustment set is sufficient.
Transition: “With that caveat in mind, let’s now formalise what happens when we omit a confounder.”









Important


We are not doing causal inference.
Our regression models recover associations, not causal effects. The statistical machinery is blind to the direction of arrows — it cannot discover them from data alone. Including GDP per capita in the model closes the back-door path if our DAG is correct — but that correctness is a substantive theoretical assumption, not something the data can verify.





So what are DAGs for?

They make our theoretical assumptions explicit and contestable
They tell us which variables to condition on (and which never to touch)
They are the bridge between substantive theory and model specification



The regression tells us how strong the association is, once we have decided what to condition on. The DAG tells us what to condition on. Neither step substitutes for the other.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Omitted Variable Bias: The Formal Result
text:
Omitted Variable Bias: The Formal Result


OVB formula: the bivariate slope is equal to the true DGP slope PLUS the product of (a) the association of the omitted variable with Y and (b) the slope from regressing the omitted variable on X.
“Both terms are positive here: GDP is associated with health spending (β_gdp > 0), and GDP is associated with women’s parliament share (Cov(X,Z)/Var(X) > 0). So we over-estimate the partial association of X with Y.”
Key lesson: OVB is a design/measurement problem. Adding GDP to the model removes the bias — but only if GDP is the only omitted confounder.


Suppose the true model has two predictors, \(X\) (women in parliament) and \(Z\) (GDP per capita):
\[
Y_i = \alpha + \beta_X X_i + \beta_Z Z_i + \epsilon_i
\tag{1}\]
But we fit only the bivariate model, omitting \(Z\). The bivariate OLS estimator converges to:
\[
\hat{\beta}_X^{\text{biv}} \xrightarrow{p} \beta_X + \beta_Z \cdot \underbrace{\frac{\text{Cov}(X, Z)}{\text{Var}(X)}}_{\text{auxiliary slope: } X \text{ on } Z}
\tag{2}\]

Omitted Variable Bias (OVB) = \(\beta_Z \cdot \frac{\text{Cov}(X, Z)}{\text{Var}(X)}\)


In our case: \(\beta_Z > 0\) (wealth is associated with health spending) and \(\text{Cov}(X, Z) > 0\) (wealth is associated with higher representation) \(\Rightarrow\) OVB is positive — we systematically over-estimate \(\beta_X\).









Important


Bayesian regression cannot fix OVB. Including \(Z\) in the model removes the bias, but only if \(Z\) is correctly measured and included. Priors regularize coefficients — they do not discover missing variables. OVB is a design problem, not a computation problem. (This is Gauss-Markov A2, from last week.)

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Multiple Regression: The Formal Model


The crucial insight: adding more predictors does NOT change the likelihood family or the MCMC machinery. Same model structure, more parameters.
Partial derivative: “β_X is the expected change in Y for a 1-unit increase in X, HOLDING Z CONSTANT. This is what ‘controlling for Z’ means mathematically.”
Formally writing out the model (likelihood + priors for every parameter) is the key discipline. Priors for control variables are subject to the same scientific scrutiny as priors for the main predictor.


Everything from Week 8 carries over. We simply extend the linear predictor:
\[
Y_i \sim \text{Normal}(\mu_i, \sigma)
\tag{3}\]
\[
\mu_i = \alpha + \beta_X X_i + \beta_Z Z_i + \cdots + \beta_k X_{ki}
\tag{4}\]

Interpreting coefficients as partial effects:
\[
\frac{\partial \mu}{\partial X} = \beta_X \qquad \text{(holding all other predictors constant)}
\tag{5}\]
\[
\frac{\partial \mu}{\partial Z} = \beta_Z \qquad \text{(holding all other predictors constant)}
\tag{6}\]









Note


Nothing structurally changes: we now have \(k + 2\) estimands (\(\alpha\), \(\beta_1, \ldots, \beta_k\), \(\sigma\)). The likelihood is still Gaussian. MCMC still samples from the joint posterior. We just need one prior per coefficient — applied with the same principled care as before.






Posterior:
\[
p(\alpha, \beta_X, \beta_Z, \sigma \mid \mathbf{Y}, \mathbf{X}, \mathbf{Z}) \propto \mathcal{L}(\alpha, \beta_X, \beta_Z, \sigma) \cdot p(\alpha)\, p(\beta_X)\, p(\beta_Z)\, p(\sigma)
\tag{7}\]

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Dummy Variables: Categorical Predictors


A dummy variable is a 0/1 indicator. Its coefficient is the expected difference in Y between the two groups, holding all other predictors constant.
K-1 rule: for K categories, we need K-1 dummies. The omitted category is the “reference baseline” — its effect is absorbed into the intercept.
Why K-1 and not K? Perfect multicollinearity. If we included a dummy for every category, their values would sum to 1 (a constant), creating perfect collinearity with the intercept. The design matrix would be singular.
brms handles dummies automatically: include the character/factor variable, and brms creates the K-1 dummies internally, choosing the first alphabetical level as the reference.
Preview: “We’ll use a binary dummy (left vs. right government) in the second application. It generalizes directly to 3+ categories.”


When a predictor is categorical (nominal), we encode it as \(K - 1\) binary indicator variables:
\[
\mu_i = \alpha + \beta_X X_i + \beta_Z Z_i + \beta_D D_i
\tag{8}\]
where \(D_i \in \{0, 1\}\) is an indicator for one of \(K = 2\) groups.

Interpretation:

\(\alpha\): expected \(Y\) when \(D = 0\) (reference category) and other predictors = 0
\(\beta_D\): expected difference in \(Y\) between \(D = 1\) and \(D = 0\), holding \(X\) and \(Z\) constant


\(D = 0\) (reference group): \(\mu_i = \alpha + \beta_X X_i + \beta_Z Z_i\)
\(D = 1\) (indicator group): \(\mu_i = (\alpha + \beta_D) + \beta_X X_i + \beta_Z Z_i\)



The dummy shifts the intercept — it gives the \(D = 1\) group a different baseline level while holding the slopes constant.









Note


For \(K > 2\) categories (e.g., regime type: democracy, hybrid, autocracy): include \(K - 1 = 2\) dummies. The omitted category is the baseline. In brms, passing a factor() variable does this automatically. The prior on each dummy coefficient is the same as any \(\beta\): typically \(\text{Normal}(0, \sigma_\beta)\), expressing prior uncertainty about group differences.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Dummy Variables: The Shifting Intercept


This is the key visual intuition. Both lines have the same slope (unemployment effect is the same for both groups), but the Left government line sits higher — that vertical gap is β_D.
Ask students: “Where is β_D in this plot? Can you estimate it visually?”
Deliberately use the df_interact data here — the same dataset students will work with in Part IV — so the visual is immediately connected to the application.
True DGP: intercept shift for Left = 2, slope difference = 0.9 (but that is the interaction, covered in Parts III–IV). Here we show only the intercept-shift logic.




Figure 3: Dummy variable as a shifting intercept. Both regression lines share the same slope (unemployment effect), but Left governments start from a higher baseline. The vertical gap between the lines is β_D — the dummy coefficient.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Marginal Effects


The key conceptual bridge: in a purely additive model the partial derivative is constant, so the AME equals the coefficient. But students need to understand the AME concept now so they can grasp why it diverges from the coefficient in interaction models (Part III) and non-linear models (Week 11).
“In the linear additive model, β_X is already the average effect — there is nothing to average over, because the slope doesn’t change across observations. But the moment we add an interaction, the slope depends on the value of the other variable. Then we need to average.”
Foreshadow: “When we get to logistic regression in Week 11, the derivative ∂μ/∂X depends on the value of X itself. We will always average over the data to get a single interpretable number.”


In a multivariate model, the effect of \(X\) on \(Y\) depends on what we hold constant.

The Average Marginal Effect (AME) of \(X\) is:
\[
\text{AME}(X) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\partial \hat{\mu}_i}{\partial X_i}
\tag{9}\]


In the additive linear model, \(\partial \hat{\mu}_i / \partial X_i = \beta_X\) for every observation — so the AME collapses to the coefficient itself:
\[
\text{AME}(X) = \beta_X \qquad \text{(additive linear model only)}
\tag{10}\]




Why bother defining it?
The AME concept generalises. As soon as the model has:

an interaction term (Part III today), or
a non-linear link function (logistic regression, Week 11)

… the partial derivative \(\partial \hat{\mu}_i / \partial X_i\) varies across observations, and we must average to get a single interpretable number.








Note


Posterior AME
In the Bayesian workflow, we compute the AME for each posterior draw and report the resulting distribution — mean, credible interval, and full density. This is the posterior_epred averaging workflow used in Parts II and IV.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Prior Specification for Multivariate Models


The key message: adding more predictors does not change the prior philosophy. Each coefficient needs its own prior, sized to reflect plausible effect magnitudes.
“A prior on β_women says: ‘Before seeing the data, I expect that each additional percentage point of women in parliament is associated with a change in health spending of between -1.5 and +1.5 pp (Normal(0, 0.5) covers 99.7% of values in that range).’”
Standardizing predictors: an alternative approach is to standardize all predictors (z-score), which makes priors easier to set uniformly (all slopes roughly Normal(0, 1)). We won’t do that here, but it’s worth knowing.
σ prior: Exponential(1) implies P(σ < 5) ≈ 0.99 — very likely the residual SD is under 5pp of GDP. Substantively justified.


For our health spending model, we need priors for four parameters:



\[
\alpha \sim \text{Normal}(10, 5)
\tag{11}\] “Baseline spending ~10% GDP when \(X = Z = 0\); very uncertain”
\[
\beta_X \sim \text{Normal}(0, 0.5)
\tag{12}\] “Per % women in parliament, change in spending probably between \(\pm 1.5\) pp”

\[
\beta_Z \sim \text{Normal}(0, 1)
\tag{13}\] “Per $1k of GDP per capita, change in spending probably between \(\pm 3\) pp”
\[
\sigma \sim \text{Exponential}(1)
\tag{14}\] “Residual SD almost certainly below 5pp of GDP”










Important


Priors must be calibrated to the scale of the outcome and predictors. A prior of \(\beta \sim \text{Normal}(0, 100)\) on a percentage-point outcome is nearly flat (uninformative and implausible). A prior of \(\text{Normal}(0, 0.5)\) for the women-in-parliament coefficient is defensible: it says extreme effects (say, \(+5\) pp per % women) are essentially impossible a priori, while effects of the size found in the literature (\(< 0.5\) pp) are plausible.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Step 1: Estimand


The DAG was introduced and discussed in Part I (see sec-dag-our-rq). Briefly recall it here and move quickly to the estimand statement.
Estimand in words: “The partial association between a 1 percentage point increase in women’s share of parliament seats and public health expenditure as a percentage of GDP, holding GDP per capita constant.”
Emphasise: the estimand is fixed before we touch any code. We commit to it based on the DAG and our research question.


Recall from Part I: GDP per capita is a confounder — it opens a back-door path from Women in Parliament to Health Spending. Conditioning on GDP per capita (including it as a predictor) closes that path.


Estimand: The partial association between women’s parliamentary representation and health spending, adjusting for GDP per capita (the minimal sufficient adjustment set that closes the GDP back-door path).
Under the assumption that the adjustment set is sufficient — i.e., no unobserved confounders remain — this partial association can be interpreted as a causal effect. That assumption is substantive, not statistical.










Note


We commit to this estimand before opening R. The model specification follows from the DAG — not the other way around.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Step 2: Data Summary


Left panel: bivariate scatter. The positive trend is real, but conflates the partial association of women in parliament and the confounding from GDP.
Right panel: OVB in action using Bayesian posterior means and 95% credible intervals. Bivariate model (orange, steeper) vs. multivariate model (purple, controlling for GDP). The gap between posterior means IS the OVB.
Ask students: “Why does the bivariate estimate overstate the association? Use the OVB formula.”
True DGP coefficient β = 0.12. The bivariate posterior mean is around 0.25. The multivariate posterior mean is near 0.12.




Figure 4: Left: raw scatter of the bivariate association. Right: OVB illustrated with Bayesian posterior means and 95% credible intervals — the bivariate estimate (orange) over-estimates the partial association; the multivariate estimate (purple, controlling for GDP) recovers the true DGP value of 0.12.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Step 3: Formal Model Specification


Walk through each line. This is the mathematical commitment that precedes any code.
“Notice that the likelihood is unchanged from last week. It is still Gaussian. All we have done is expand μ_i to include a second predictor.”
Priors: “β_women ~ Normal(0, 0.5) means: before seeing data, I believe the partial association per percentage point of women in parliament is almost certainly between -1.5 and +1.5. Is that a reasonable prior belief? Let’s discuss.”
Ask students: “What would a uniform prior on β_women look like? Is it scientifically sensible?”




Likelihood
\[
Y_i \sim \text{Normal}(\mu_i, \sigma)
\tag{15}\]
\[
\mu_i = \alpha + \beta_X X_i + \beta_Z Z_i
\tag{16}\]
where \(X\) = women in parliament, \(Z\) = GDP per capita.
Estimands (unknown, fixed): \(\alpha, \beta_X, \beta_Z, \sigma\)
We know the truth: \(\alpha = 2\), \(\beta_X = 0.12\), \(\beta_Z = 0.20\), \(\sigma = 1.5\).

Priors
\[
\alpha \sim \text{Normal}(10, 5)
\tag{17}\]
\[
\beta_X \sim \text{Normal}(0, 0.5)
\tag{18}\]
\[
\beta_Z \sim \text{Normal}(0, 1)
\tag{19}\]
\[
\sigma \sim \text{Exponential}(1)
\tag{20}\]
Posterior
\[
p(\alpha, \beta_X, \beta_Z, \sigma \mid \mathbf{Y}) \propto \mathcal{L} \cdot p(\alpha)\,p(\beta_X)\,p(\beta_Z)\,p(\sigma)
\tag{21}\]

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Step 4: Prior Predictive Check


“We sample (α, β_X, β_Z, σ) from the priors and ask: what health spending outcomes does the model predict before seeing any data?”
The shaded region should cover [0, 30] — the plausible range for public health expenditure as % of GDP.
If the prior predictive puts substantial mass below 0% or above 50%, the priors are too diffuse and need tightening.
“A sensible prior predictive is not evidence that the priors are correct — it is a minimum bar for scientific plausibility.”




View source
set.seed(2026)
n_pp <- 2000

# draw n_pp parameter sets from the joint prior, then simulate Y for all observations
prior_pred_controls <- tibble(
  b0 = rnorm(n_pp, 10, 5), # intercept draws
  b_wp = rnorm(n_pp, 0, 0.5), # women_parl slope draws
  b_gdp = rnorm(n_pp, 0, 1), # gdp_pc slope draws
  sigma = rexp(n_pp, 1) # residual SD draws
) |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(
    y_sim = list(
      b0 +
        b_wp * df_controls$women_parl + # linear predictor evaluated at observed X, Z
        b_gdp * df_controls$gdp_pc +
        rnorm(nrow(df_controls), 0, sigma) # add Gaussian noise
    )
  ) |>
  unnest(y_sim) |>
  ungroup()

prior_pred_controls |>
  ggplot(aes(x = y_sim)) +
  annotate(
    "rect",
    xmin = 0,
    xmax = 30,
    ymin = 0,
    ymax = Inf,
    fill = col_4,
    alpha = 0.12
  ) +
  geom_density(fill = col_1, alpha = 0.55, color = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = c(0, 30),
    color = col_4,
    linetype = "dashed",
    linewidth = 0.9
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = 15,
    y = Inf,
    vjust = 1.4,
    size = 3.2,
    color = col_4,
    label = "Plausible empirical range: 0–30% GDP"
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) +
  labs(
    x = "Predicted Health Expenditure (% GDP)",
    y = "Prior Predictive Density",
    title = "Prior Predictive Check: Health Spending Model",
    subtitle = "Before seeing data — does the prior generate plausible outcomes?"
  )




Figure 5: Prior predictive distribution for health spending: 2,000 draws from the joint prior, evaluated at the observed X and Z values. The shaded band marks the plausible empirical range (0–30% of GDP). Most prior mass falls in a reasonable range — these priors are defensible.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Step 5: Fit the Model with brms
text:
Step 5: Fit the Model with brms


Same syntax as last week. The only change: add gdp_pc to the formula and add a prior for it.
“Notice we specify priors for each coefficient individually using the coef argument. This precision is good scientific practice.”
Point to iter, warmup, chains: same settings as week 8. Standard defaults.
Remind students: they should type this themselves in Positron from the slide source.




View source
fit_controls <- brm(
  health_spend ~ women_parl + gdp_pc, # outcome ~ main predictor + control
  data = df_controls,
  prior = c(
    prior(normal(10, 5), class = "Intercept"), # baseline spending ~10% GDP
    prior(normal(0, 0.5), class = "b", coef = "women_parl"), # weak prior: ±1.5pp per %
    prior(normal(0, 1), class = "b", coef = "gdp_pc"), # ±3pp per $1k GDP
    prior(exponential(1), class = "sigma") # residual SD almost certainly < 5
  ),
  chains = 4, # four independent chains
  iter = 2000, # 1000 warmup + 1000 sampling per chain
  warmup = 1000,
  seed = 2026 # reproducibility
)




brms translates this to Stan (C++), runs 4 parallel chains, draws 4,000 posterior samples of \((\alpha, \beta_X, \beta_Z, \sigma)\), and returns them for our analysis.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Step 6a: MCMC Diagnostics


Same diagnostics as week 8. The criteria are identical regardless of how many parameters the model has.
Rhat < 1.01 for all parameters: chains have mixed and converged.
Bulk_ESS and Tail_ESS > 1000: enough effective samples for reliable posterior summaries.
Traceplots: should look like “hairy caterpillars” — each chain explores the same range, no trends.
If any diagnostic fails: do NOT proceed to interpretation. Investigate the chain.




View source
mcmc_trace(
  fit_controls,
  pars = c("b_Intercept", "b_women_parl", "b_gdp_pc", "sigma"),
  facet_args = list(ncol = 2)
) +
  scale_color_manual(values = chain_cols) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "gray90")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "MCMC Traceplots",
    subtitle = "All chains must mix well before interpreting results"
  )




Figure 6: Traceplots for the four key parameters. Each chain (colour) should explore the same range with no trends or divergences. The ‘hairy caterpillar’ pattern confirms convergence.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Step 6b: Posterior Predictive Check


pp_check: “Do the model’s predictions look like the observed data?”
Blue lines are posterior draws of the predictive distribution. Dark blue line is the observed data density.
If the model is well-specified, the blue lines should closely envelope the dark line.
Common failure: pp_check reveals that the model over- or under-disperses — indicates a likelihood misspecification.




View source
pp_check(fit_controls, ndraws = 50) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) +
  labs(
    title = "Posterior Predictive Check: Health Spending Model",
    subtitle = "Light blue: model predictions  |  Dark: observed data"
  )




Figure 7: Posterior predictive check: 50 simulated outcome distributions from the fitted model (light blue) versus the observed data distribution (dark). The Gaussian model fits well — the posterior predictive envelops the observed distribution.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Step 7: Interpret Results


Summary output: Estimate = posterior mean. Est.Error = posterior SD. l/u-95% CI = 95% credible interval.
Key comparison to make explicit: bivariate posterior mean was ~0.25; multivariate posterior mean is near the true DGP value of 0.12.
β_gdp: “Each additional $1,000 of GDP per capita is associated with about 0.20pp higher health spending, holding women’s representation constant.”
β_women: “Each additional percentage point of women in parliament is associated with about 0.12pp higher health spending, holding GDP constant.”
Point out that Rhat values in summary confirm convergence.




View source
summary(fit_controls)


 Family: gaussian 
  Links: mu = identity 
Formula: health_spend ~ women_parl + gdp_pc 
   Data: df_controls (Number of observations: 120) 
  Draws: 4 chains, each with iter = 2000; warmup = 1000; thin = 1;
         total post-warmup draws = 4000

Regression Coefficients:
           Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS
Intercept      1.49      0.53     0.43     2.51 1.00     3319     3326
women_parl     0.15      0.03     0.10     0.21 1.00     1830     1997
gdp_pc         0.18      0.03     0.12     0.24 1.00     1800     1888

Further Distributional Parameters:
      Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS
sigma     1.36      0.09     1.20     1.55 1.00     3553     2643

Draws were sampled using sampling(NUTS). For each parameter, Bulk_ESS
and Tail_ESS are effective sample size measures, and Rhat is the potential
scale reduction factor on split chains (at convergence, Rhat = 1).

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Step 7: Visualising Partial Effects


posterior_epred() on a grid: vary women_parl across its range while holding gdp_pc at its mean.
The ribbon shows 95% credible interval for E[Y | X, Z = Z_mean].
“This is the estimated effect of women’s representation, holding income constant. It is narrower and closer to zero than the bivariate estimate — the confounder is doing real work.”




View source
grid_controls <- tibble(
  women_parl = seq(
    min(df_controls$women_parl),
    max(df_controls$women_parl),
    length.out = 80
  ),
  gdp_pc = mean(df_controls$gdp_pc)
)

# posterior_epred: E[Y | X, Z=mean] for each row of grid_controls, averaging over all parameter uncertainty
epred_mat <- posterior_epred(fit_controls, newdata = grid_controls)

grid_controls <- grid_controls |>
  mutate(
    lo = apply(epred_mat, 2, quantile, 0.025),
    mid = apply(epred_mat, 2, median),
    hi = apply(epred_mat, 2, quantile, 0.975)
  )

df_controls |>
  ggplot(aes(x = women_parl, y = health_spend)) +
  geom_ribbon(
    data = grid_controls,
    aes(x = women_parl, ymin = lo, ymax = hi, y = mid),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    fill = col_1,
    alpha = 0.25
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = grid_controls,
    aes(x = women_parl, y = mid),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    color = col_2,
    linewidth = 1.2
  ) +
  geom_point(color = col_1, alpha = 0.45, size = 1.8) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) +
  labs(
    x = "Women in Parliament (%)",
    y = "Health Expenditure (% GDP)",
    title = "Partial Effect: Women in Parliament -> Health Spending",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "GDP per capita held at mean (",
      round(mean(df_controls$gdp_pc), 1),
      " thousand USD)  |  posterior_epred()"
    )
  )




Figure 8: Posterior predictive mean and 95% credible interval for health spending as a function of women’s parliamentary share, holding GDP per capita at its mean. The ribbon quantifies our remaining uncertainty about the partial effect after conditioning on GDP.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Step 8: LOO-IC Preview and Limitations


LOO-IC preview: the bivariate model does worse than the multivariate model — as expected, since we have added a genuinely predictive variable.
elpd_diff: “Expected log predictive density difference. Larger = better. The bivariate model has lower elpd_diff (is worse) by about X units, with SE of Y. When the difference exceeds 2×SE, we take it seriously.”
Limitations:

We assumed a linear relationship between each predictor and outcome — check residuals.
We assumed GDP is the ONLY confounder — other confounders (legal institutions, colonial history, religion) may still bias β_X.
We have panel data (country-years) — A5 (independent errors) may be violated (hierarchical model in Week 12).





View source
# add_criterion appends LOO estimates to the fitted model objects
fit_controls <- add_criterion(fit_controls, "loo")
fit_controls_biv <- add_criterion(fit_controls_biv, "loo")
loo_compare(fit_controls_biv, fit_controls)


                 elpd_diff se_diff
fit_controls       0.0       0.0  
fit_controls_biv -15.0       5.1  










Important


Remaining limitations: (1) GDP may not be the only confounder — country institutional quality, colonial history, and cultural factors may also drive both representation and spending. (2) Country-year observations are likely correlated within countries — a violation of A5 that Week 12 addresses with multilevel models. (3) Linearity is assumed: we should check residuals for nonlinear patterns.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
When Effects Are Conditional


The key insight: not all associations are the same everywhere. The association between X and Y may depend on a third variable Z.
Real political science example: the association between economic crisis and democratic backsliding may depend on institutional strength. In weak institutions, crises are associated with democratic breakdown; in strong ones, less so.
Today’s story: the association between unemployment and welfare spending depends on government ideology. Left governments respond more aggressively; right governments do not.
“A model without an interaction term is implicitly assuming the slope is constant across all levels of Z. That is a strong assumption. Test it before imposing it.”



Does the effect of unemployment on welfare spending depend on government ideology?


Partisan theory of the welfare state: left-wing governments use social spending as a counter-cyclical tool — when unemployment rises, they increase transfers. Right-wing governments are more restrained, fearing fiscal imbalance and moral hazard.




The association between \(X\) (unemployment) and \(Y\) (transfer spending) is not constant — it changes depending on \(Z\) (government ideology). This is called moderation or effect heterogeneity.

“The slope of the unemployment–spending relationship is steeper under left governments.”









Note


Synthetic dataset: \(n = 150\) government-years.

\(X\) = Unemployment rate (%)
\(Z\) = Left government (0/1 dummy)
\(Y\) = Transfer spending (% GDP)

True slopes: Right = 0.3, Left = 0.3 + 0.9 = 1.2

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Formal Interaction Model


Walk through the partial derivatives carefully.
∂μ/∂X = β₁ + β₃Z: “The slope of X on Y is a linear function of Z. When Z = 0 (right government), the slope is β₁. When Z = 1 (left government), the slope is β₁ + β₃.”
∂μ/∂Z = β₂ + β₃X: “The ‘effect’ of Z on Y also depends on X. At zero unemployment, being a left government adds β₂ to spending. At 10% unemployment, it adds β₂ + 10β₃.”
Constitutive terms: “β₁ and β₂ are the conditional effects when the other modifier equals zero. They must be included. Omitting β₁ imposes β₁ = 0 — usually wrong.”


We augment the linear predictor with a product term:
\[
\mu_i = \alpha + \beta_1 X_i + \beta_2 Z_i + \beta_3 (X_i \cdot Z_i)
\tag{22}\]

Conditional marginal effects — both slopes are now functions of the other variable:
\[
\frac{\partial \mu}{\partial X} = \beta_1 + \beta_3 Z \qquad \text{(effect of unemployment depends on ideology)}
\tag{23}\]
\[
\frac{\partial \mu}{\partial Z} = \beta_2 + \beta_3 X \qquad \text{(ideology gap depends on unemployment level)}
\tag{24}\]


Two regression lines (when \(Z\) is binary):

\(Z = 0\) (right): \(\mu = \alpha + \beta_1 X\)
\(Z = 1\) (left): \(\mu = (\alpha + \beta_2) + (\beta_1 + \beta_3) X\)

The dummy shifts the intercept by \(\beta_2\); the product shifts the slope by \(\beta_3\).

title: QMIR 2026
section: Posterior Marginal Effects: Exact by Arithmetic
text:
Posterior Marginal Effects: Exact by Arithmetic


The key insight: the conditional marginal effect is a function of β₁ and β₃. Because we have full posterior draws for both, we can compute the marginal effect at any z by simple arithmetic — no approximation needed.
For every MCMC draw (β₁^(s), β₃^(s)), compute β₁^(s) + β₃^(s)*z directly. The resulting set of S values IS the posterior distribution of the conditional marginal effect at z.
“This is not a minor technical point. It means posterior credible intervals for conditional marginal effects are exact — not approximate — as long as MCMC has converged.”
The same principle generalises: any quantity that is a function of parameters can be obtained by applying that function to each row of the posterior draws data frame.


For each of the \(S\) MCMC draws, compute the conditional marginal effect at a fixed value \(z\) of the moderator:
\[
\text{ME}^{(s)} = \beta_1^{(s)} + \beta_3^{(s)} \cdot z
\tag{25}\]
The resulting \(\{\text{ME}^{(s)}\}_{s=1}^S\) is the posterior distribution of the marginal effect at \(z\) — obtained by ordinary arithmetic.



View source
# as_draws_df: one row per MCMC draw, one column per parameter
post <- as_draws_df(fit)
post |>
  mutate(
    me_left = b_unemployment + # marginal effect at Z = 1 (left gov.)
      `b_unemployment:left_gov`
  )











Note


This generalises immediately: compute any quantity that is a function of posterior draws using ordinary arithmetic — predicted probabilities, differences in predicted values, ratios, non-linear transformations. No additional packages or approximations needed.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Diagnostic I: Linearity of Moderation


The interaction model assumes the heterogeneous effect is LINEAR in Z. “The slope increases by β₃ for each unit increase in Z.” This is a strong assumption.
Brambor et al. (2006) show this assumption is violated in roughly half of empirical studies.
Diagnostic: compare linear fit vs. LOESS within each Z-group. If LOESS and the linear fit track closely, linearity holds. If they diverge substantially, the interaction may be non-linear.
For binary Z: linearity of moderation means the slope changes by a constant amount between groups. This is inherently satisfied for a binary moderator — there are only two levels.
For continuous Z: this diagnostic matters. We’ll see this in future weeks.


The interaction model imposes that the marginal effect of \(X\) is linear in \(Z\):
\[
\frac{\partial \mu}{\partial X} = \beta_1 + \beta_3 Z \quad \text{(linear in } Z\text{)}
\tag{26}\]

When \(Z\) is binary (our case today): the assumption is trivially satisfied — there are only two groups and we estimate one slope per group.


When \(Z\) is continuous (a future concern): check by comparing the linear fit with a flexible nonparametric smoother (LOESS) within quantile-based groups of \(Z\):








Figure 9: Linearity check for a continuous moderator: split into thirds and compare linear vs. LOESS slopes. If they diverge, the linearity-of-moderation assumption is violated.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Continuous Moderators: Interpreting the Interaction Coefficient
text:
Continuous Moderators: Interpreting the Interaction Coefficient


When Z is continuous, the marginal effect of X is a continuously changing function of Z.
At Z = 0: ME = β₁. At Z = 1: ME = β₁ + β₃. At Z = z: ME = β₁ + β₃ * z.
Geometrically: the relationship between X and Y is a family of lines, each line corresponding to a fixed value of Z. The slope increases (or decreases) continuously as Z changes — like twisting a flat surface into a ramp.
In the 2D cross-section you draw X vs Y at three fixed values of Z (low, mid, high). Three parallel lines? No interaction. Fan-shaped divergence? Positive β₃.


When \(Z\) is continuous, the conditional marginal effect \(\frac{\partial \mu}{\partial X} = \beta_1 + \beta_3 Z\) is a continuous linear function of \(Z\):

At \(Z = 0\): the effect of \(X\) is \(\beta_1\)
At \(Z = 1\): the effect of \(X\) is \(\beta_1 + \beta_3\)
At \(Z = z\): the effect of \(X\) is \(\beta_1 + \beta_3 z\) — the slope changes continuously as \(Z\) changes










Figure 10: Predicted Y vs. X at three fixed values of a continuous moderator Z (low = -1, median = 0, high = +1), using hypothetical coefficients β₁ = 0.5, β₃ = 0.8. The fan-shaped divergence is the visual signature of a positive interaction term.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Diagnostic II: Common Support


Common support: we need sufficient variation in X within each level of Z. If X only takes high values when Z=0 and low values when Z=1, we are extrapolating, not estimating.
“We cannot estimate the slope under left governments at unemployment = 2% if no left governments in our data experienced 2% unemployment. The model will extrapolate — and extrapolation is unreliable.”
Diagnostic: density/histogram of X, faceted by Z. If the distributions overlap substantially, we have common support. If they are disjoint, we are in trouble.
For today’s binary Z case: the setup uses rbinom(n, 1, 0.5) so left and right governments are evenly split. Both groups see the full range of unemployment. Common support is satisfied by design.


Common support: the estimation of conditional marginal effects is credible only where the predictor \(X\) has sufficient variation within each level of the moderator \(Z\).

Diagnostic: compare the distribution of \(X\) across \(Z\) groups.








Figure 11: Common support check: compare X distributions across Z groups. Left: good overlap — we can estimate marginal effects across the full X range. Right: poor overlap — estimates in the non-overlapping regions are extrapolations, not estimates.






Rule: if the \(X\)-distributions barely overlap across \(Z\) groups, conditional marginal effects in the non-overlapping region are extrapolations, not estimates. Report honestly.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Constitutive Terms Rule


This is a common and consequential mistake. Students will make it.
“If you use y ~ x:z instead of y ~ x * z, you are constraining β₁ = 0 and β₂ = 0. You are saying: ‘At Z=0, the effect of X is exactly zero. At X=0, the effect of Z is exactly zero.’ This is almost never true.”
“Always use *. The model with constitutive terms is more general; the model without them is a special case with two hard constraints. Let the data decide.”
In brms: y ~ x * z expands to y ~ x + z + x:z automatically. Use the * notation as shorthand and as a reminder of the requirement.


Always include the main effects (\(X\), \(Z\)) when specifying an interaction \((X \cdot Z)\):









Important


Wrong: omitting constitutive terms
brm(y ~ x:z, ...)
# implicitly: β₁ = 0 and β₂ = 0
# At Z = 0: no effect of X
# At X = 0: no effect of Z
# These are strong constraints, usually false












Note


Correct: include all constitutive terms
brm(y ~ x * z, ...)
# equivalent to: y ~ x + z + x:z
# β₁, β₂, β₃ all estimated freely
# No artificial constraints imposed







In brms: y ~ x * z is the recommended syntax. It expands to y ~ x + z + x:z automatically and documents the constitutive terms requirement.
Prior for \(\beta_3\): \(\text{Normal}(0, 1)\) is a sensible weakly informative prior. It says: “Before seeing the data, the slope difference between groups is probably between \(-3\) and \(+3\) — no moderation is the prior expectation.”

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Step 1: DAG and Estimand


The DAG for an interaction model is simpler than for a confounding model: no back-door paths to close.
“Unemployment is associated with transfer spending. Government ideology is associated with transfer spending. But the association between unemployment and spending may depend on ideology — ideology moderates the unemployment-spending relationship.”
DAG notation: both U and G have arrows into Y. The moderation is notated by the curved ‘interaction’ arrow, but in standard DAG notation it is just shown as two arrows entering Y.
Estimand: the conditional marginal effect (partial association) of unemployment on transfer spending for right and left governments separately — two quantities.
The interaction β₃ itself answers: “How much does the unemployment slope differ between left and right governments?”




Figure 12: DAG for the partisan welfare state model. Unemployment and government ideology are both causes of transfer spending. The research question concerns the interaction: is the unemployment-to-spending slope larger under left governments?


Estimand 1: \(\frac{\partial \mu}{\partial X}\Big|_{Z=0}\) — partial association of unemployment with spending under right governments.
Estimand 2: \(\frac{\partial \mu}{\partial X}\Big|_{Z=1}\) — partial association of unemployment with spending under left governments.
Estimand 3: \(\beta_3\) — the slope difference: how much more does unemployment associate with spending under left governments?
Under the assumption that the adjustment set is sufficient — no unobserved confounders of unemployment or ideology — these associations can be interpreted as causal effects. That assumption is substantive, not statistical.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Step 2: Data Summary


First look: marginal distribution of the outcome by group. Ask students: “Do left and right governments already differ in average spending, ignoring unemployment?”
The answer is yes (left governments spend more on average) — this is the intercept shift captured by β₂.
The distribution shapes also differ — some skew at the upper end for left governments.




Figure 13: Marginal distribution of transfer spending by government ideology. Left governments show higher average spending — the intercept shift captured by β₂ in the interaction model.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Step 2 (continued): Joint Patterns


The left panel: scatter with separate points and lines for each government type. Ask students: “Do the lines look like they have different slopes?”
The right panel: box plots of transfer spending by unemployment tercile × ideology. Shows the increasing gap between orange and blue as unemployment rises — the visual signature of an interaction.
“This preliminary look motivates fitting the interaction model. But it doesn’t prove the interaction is real — we need the posterior to quantify uncertainty.”




Figure 14: Left: scatter coloured by government ideology with separate OLS lines. The slopes look clearly different. Right: transfer spending by unemployment tercile and ideology — the gap between left and right governments widens as unemployment rises, the visual signature of an interaction.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Step 3: Formal Model Specification


Walk through the full model block. Emphasize that β₃ is the focal quantity — the interaction term.
“β₁ = 0.3 means: under a right government, each percentage point of unemployment is associated with 0.3pp higher transfer spending. β₁ + β₃ = 0.3 + 0.9 = 1.2 means the same under a left government.”
Prior for β₃: Normal(0, 1). “The prior expectation is zero interaction — no moderation. This is appropriately humble; the data will update us.”
Note: brms handles the product term automatically when we write unemployment * left_gov.




Likelihood
\[
Y_i \sim \text{Normal}(\mu_i, \sigma)
\tag{27}\]
\[
\mu_i = \alpha + \beta_1 U_i + \beta_2 G_i + \beta_3 (U_i \cdot G_i)
\tag{28}\]
\(U\) = unemployment, \(G\) = left government (0/1)
True DGP (known because synthetic):
\(\alpha = 10, \beta_1 = 0.3, \beta_2 = 2, \beta_3 = 0.9, \sigma = 2.5\)

Priors
\[
\alpha \sim \text{Normal}(12, 5)
\tag{29}\]
\[
\beta_1,\, \beta_2 \sim \text{Normal}(0, 1)
\tag{30}\]
\[
\beta_3 \sim \text{Normal}(0, 1)
\tag{31}\]
\[
\sigma \sim \text{Exponential}(0.5)
\tag{32}\]
Prior for \(\beta_3\): centered at zero — prior expectation is no moderation. The data will update this.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Step 4: Prior Predictive Check


Show prior predictive lines separately for left and right governments.
The key question: “Do the prior-predicted slope ranges overlap with what we would expect based on substantive knowledge?”
“Under a right government, the prior allows slopes from negative to strongly positive. Under a left government, the interaction prior adds additional spread. Both ranges are wide but not implausible.”




View source
set.seed(42)
n_pp2 <- 150
unemp_seq <- seq(1.5, 14, length.out = 60)

# draw n_pp2 parameter sets from joint prior; expand over (unemployment, ideology) grid
prior_lines_int <- tibble(
  sim = 1:n_pp2,
  b0 = rnorm(n_pp2, 12, 5), # intercept draws
  b_u = rnorm(n_pp2, 0, 1), # unemployment slope draws
  b_g = rnorm(n_pp2, 0, 1), # ideology main effect draws
  b_int = rnorm(n_pp2, 0, 1) # interaction term draws
) |>
  expand_grid(unemployment = unemp_seq, left_gov = c(0L, 1L)) |>
  mutate(
    y_hat = b0 +
      b_u * unemployment +
      b_g * left_gov +
      b_int * unemployment * left_gov, # interaction: slope shift for left govts
    government = if_else(left_gov == 1, "Left", "Right")
  )

prior_lines_int |>
  ggplot(aes(
    x = unemployment,
    y = y_hat,
    group = interaction(sim, government),
    color = government
  )) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.12, linewidth = 0.5) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("Left" = col_2, "Right" = col_1),
    name = "Government"
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-10, 45)) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), legend.position = "bottom") +
  labs(
    x = "Unemployment (%)",
    y = "Predicted Transfer Spending (% GDP)",
    title = "Prior Predictive Spaghetti: Interaction Model",
    subtitle = "150 lines per group drawn from joint prior — non-dogmatic about moderation"
  )




Figure 15: Prior predictive spaghetti: 150 regression lines drawn from the joint prior, separately for left and right governments. The prior is non-dogmatic — it allows a wide range of slopes including no interaction (similar slopes) and strong interaction (very different slopes).

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Step 5: Fit Both Models


We fit two models: one without and one with the interaction term. We will compare them with LOO-IC in Step 8.
M1 (no interaction) assumes the unemployment slope is the same regardless of ideology — a testable restriction.
M2 (with interaction) relaxes this. If M2 fits substantially better, we have evidence that the restriction is false (i.e., that the interaction is real).
The * operator in brms: shorthand for x + z + x:z. Always use this, not the colon-only form.




View source
# M1: assumes the unemployment-spending association is the same for all governments
fit_m1 <- brm(
  transfer_spend ~ unemployment + left_gov, # no interaction term
  data = df_interact,
  prior = c(
    prior(normal(12, 5), class = "Intercept"), # baseline spending ~12% GDP
    prior(normal(0, 1), class = "b"), # weak priors on both slopes
    prior(exponential(0.5), class = "sigma") # residual SD likely < 5
  ),
  chains = 4,
  iter = 2000,
  warmup = 1000,
  seed = 42
)




View source
# M2: allows the unemployment-spending association to differ by ideology (interaction term)
fit_m2 <- brm(
  transfer_spend ~ unemployment * left_gov, # * expands to: unemp + left_gov + unemp:left_gov
  data = df_interact,
  prior = c(
    prior(normal(12, 5), class = "Intercept"), # baseline spending ~12% GDP
    prior(normal(0, 1), class = "b"), # prior centred at zero: no moderation by default
    prior(exponential(0.5), class = "sigma") # residual SD likely < 5
  ),
  chains = 4,
  iter = 2000,
  warmup = 1000,
  seed = 42
)

title: QMIR 2026
section: Step 6: Diagnostics and Posterior Predictive Check
text:
Step 6: Diagnostics and Posterior Predictive Check


Rhat and ESS: same criteria as always. Check summary output.
PPC: key check — does the model’s predictive distribution match the observed distribution?
For M2 (interaction model), the PPC should look better than M1 — the model has more flexibility to capture the different slopes.




View source
p_trace_m2 <- mcmc_trace(
  fit_m2,
  pars = c(
    "b_Intercept",
    "b_unemployment",
    "b_left_gov",
    "b_unemployment:left_gov"
  ),
  facet_args = list(ncol = 2)
) +
  scale_color_manual(values = chain_cols) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "gray90"),
    legend.position = "none"
  ) +
  labs(title = "Traceplots: Interaction Model (M2)")

p_ppc_m2 <- pp_check(fit_m2, ndraws = 50) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) +
  labs(title = "Posterior Predictive Check (M2)")

p_trace_m2 | p_ppc_m2




Figure 16: Diagnostic checks for the interaction model (M2). Left: traceplots confirming convergence. Right: posterior predictive check — model predictions envelope the observed distribution well.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Step 7: Interpreting Results and Posterior Marginal Effects
text:
Step 7: Interpreting Results and Posterior Marginal Effects


summary(fit_m2): point out that b_unemployment:left_gov is the key quantity. Its posterior mean should be near 0.9 (the true β₃).
The posterior cloud plot: two bundles of lines, one per ideology group. The orange bundle (left) has clearly steeper lines than the blue bundle (right) — the visual representation of β₃ > 0.
Posterior marginal effects: the density plot shows the full posterior distribution of each slope. Their separation quantifies the evidence for moderation.




View source
# as_draws_df: data frame with one row per MCMC draw, one column per parameter
post_draws_m2 <- as_draws_df(fit_m2) |> slice_sample(n = 200)

df_interact |>
  ggplot(aes(x = unemployment, y = transfer_spend, color = government)) +
  geom_abline(
    data = post_draws_m2,
    aes(intercept = b_Intercept, slope = b_unemployment),
    color = col_1,
    alpha = 0.05,
    linewidth = 0.4
  ) +
  geom_abline(
    data = post_draws_m2,
    aes(
      intercept = b_Intercept + b_left_gov,
      slope = b_unemployment + `b_unemployment:left_gov`
    ),
    color = col_2,
    alpha = 0.05,
    linewidth = 0.4
  ) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1.8) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("Left" = col_2, "Right" = col_1),
    name = "Government"
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), legend.position = "bottom") +
  labs(
    x = "Unemployment (%)",
    y = "Transfer Spending (% GDP)",
    title = "Posterior Line Clouds by Government Ideology",
    subtitle = "200 MCMC draws per group — orange bundle (left) is steeper"
  )




Figure 17: Posterior regression line clouds by government ideology. Each line is one MCMC draw. The orange bundle (left governments) is visibly steeper than the blue bundle (right governments) — the posterior evidence for β₃ > 0.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Step 7 (continued): Posterior Conditional Marginal Effects


This is the key figure. Two density plots: one for the right-government slope, one for the left-government slope.
The right slope is concentrated around 0.3 (true β₁ = 0.3).
The left slope is concentrated around 1.2 (true β₁ + β₃ = 1.2).
The distributions barely overlap — strong posterior evidence that the slopes are different.
P(β₃ > 0 | data): near 1. Direct probability statement: “We are virtually certain the interaction effect is positive.”
“This is the Bayesian advantage: we express our confidence in the moderation as a direct probability statement, not a p-value.”




View source
# as_draws_df: one row per MCMC draw, one column per parameter — arithmetic gives posterior marginal effects
post_all_m2 <- as_draws_df(fit_m2)

me_df <- post_all_m2 |>
  transmute(
    `Right Government` = b_unemployment,
    `Left Government` = b_unemployment + `b_unemployment:left_gov`
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(
    everything(),
    names_to = "government",
    values_to = "marginal_effect"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    government = factor(
      government,
      levels = c("Right Government", "Left Government")
    )
  )

prob_b3_pos <- mean(post_all_m2$`b_unemployment:left_gov` > 0)

me_df |>
  ggplot(aes(x = marginal_effect, fill = government, color = government)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.55, linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = 0,
    color = "gray40",
    linetype = "dotted",
    linewidth = 0.9
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Right Government" = col_1, "Left Government" = col_2),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("Right Government" = col_1, "Left Government" = col_2),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = 1.7,
    y = 1.2,
    size = 3.5,
    color = col_3,
    label = paste0("P(beta3 > 0 | data) = ", round(prob_b3_pos, 3))
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), legend.position = "bottom") +
  labs(
    x = expression(partialdiff * mu / partialdiff ~ Unemployment),
    y = "Posterior Density",
    title = "Posterior Marginal Effects: Unemployment -> Transfer Spending",
    subtitle = "Left slope ~1.2  |  Right slope ~0.3  |  Exact by arithmetic"
  )




Figure 18: Posterior distributions of the conditional marginal effect of unemployment on transfer spending, separately for right (left panel) and left (right panel) governments. Each distribution is computed by arithmetic on MCMC draws — no delta method required.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Step 8: LOO-IC Model Comparison


loo_compare: the model listed first is the better one (higher elpd).
elpd_diff: the difference in expected log predictive density. Larger magnitude = more evidence for the winner.
When elpd_diff > 2*se_diff, the difference is substantial (rule of thumb, not a hard threshold).
M2 (interaction) should dominate M1 (no interaction) here — we generated the data with an interaction.
Key message: LOO-IC does not just tell us which model fits the SAMPLE better (that would always favour the more complex model). It estimates which model would predict NEW data better — penalising complexity implicitly.




View source
fit_m1 <- add_criterion(fit_m1, "loo")
fit_m2 <- add_criterion(fit_m2, "loo")
loo_compare(fit_m1, fit_m2)


       elpd_diff se_diff
fit_m2   0.0       0.0  
fit_m1 -16.2       6.7  










Note


Reading LOO-IC output:

The model with the highest elpd (expected log predictive density) is listed first — this is the better predictive model.
elpd_diff: difference relative to the best model. The best model always shows 0.0.
se_diff: standard error of the difference. When |elpd_diff| > 2 × se_diff, the evidence strongly favours the better model.
LOO-IC evaluates out-of-sample predictive accuracy — penalising over-fitted models. A complex model only “wins” if the interaction genuinely improves prediction on new data.














Important


Limitations: (1) The linear interaction assumes a constant slope difference across all unemployment levels — we should verify with a LOESS diagnostic. (2) Government-years within the same country are correlated (A5) — a multilevel structure (Week 12) would handle this. (3) Omitted confounders (economic openness, union density) may bias the ideology coefficient.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Canonical Workflow


Read through the eight steps. This is the explicit workflow students will execute in the take-home exam.
“Every analysis you submit must have these eight elements. Not all in equal depth — but all present.”
The workflow applies regardless of model complexity: bivariate, multivariate, interaction, logistic (Week 11), multilevel (Week 12). The likelihood changes; the workflow does not.
Spend a few minutes on Q&A. Ask students: “Which step felt least familiar today?” and plan accordingly.


The same eight-step workflow applies to every model, regardless of complexity:










Step
What you do
Why




1. DAG + Estimand
Draw the causal graph; state the quantity of interest
Clarifies what you’re estimating and what to control for


2. Data Summary
Visualise and numerically describe your variables
Check for patterns, outliers, and whether an interaction is plausible


3. Formal Model
Write out \(\mathcal{L}\) and all priors before coding
Forces precision; makes assumptions explicit


4. Prior Predictive
Simulate \(Y\) from priors; check plausibility
Catches implausible priors before MCMC


5. Fit with brms
Run brm() with explicit priors
Obtains posterior draws


6a. MCMC Diagnostics
Check \(\hat{R}\), ESS, traceplots
Confirms convergence before interpreting


6b. PPC
Run pp_check()
Checks model adequacy


7. Interpretation
Posterior summaries, credible intervals, marginal effects
Answers the research question


8. Limitations
State what could still bias results
Scientific honesty

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Learning Goals: Check


Read through quickly. Connect each goal to the part of today’s session where it was covered.
Homework: students will apply the full workflow to a real dataset. The interaction application is directly relevant.
Week 10 preview: we go deeper into model assumptions and diagnostics — residual plots, heteroscedasticity, outlier detection.


By the end of today, you should be able to:

✓ Explain omitted variable bias formally (\(\hat{\beta}^{\text{biv}} = \beta + \text{bias}\)) and use a DAG to classify variables as confounders, mediators, or colliders (Part I)
✓ Extend the Bayesian regression model to multiple predictors and interpret partial effects as \(\partial \mu / \partial X_j\) (Parts I–II)
✓ Handle categorical predictors as \(K - 1\) dummy variables with an intercept-shift interpretation (Part I)
✓ Specify a multiplicative interaction model and interpret \(\partial \mu / \partial X = \beta_1 + \beta_3 Z\) as conditional on \(Z\) (Part III)
✓ Extract posterior marginal effects using as_draws_df() arithmetic — correctly, without a delta method (Parts III–IV)
✓ Compare competing models using LOO-IC and interpret elpd_diff relative to se_diff (Parts II + IV)









Important


Homework (due before next session): Apply the full 8-step workflow to a new dataset combining multivariate controls and an interaction term. Instructions and GitHub Classroom link on the course website. The take-home exam will expect the same workflow.
